summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Importance of Financial Literacy
summary_sections.content_html:
Importance of Financial Literacy

Financial literacy is the ability to understand and effectively manage personal finances. It includes budgeting, investing, debt management, and planning for the future.

Why It Matters

  • ~56% of Americans cannot cover a $1,000 emergency expense from savings.
  • Average US household credit card debt: ~$6,500.
  • Financial stress is the #1 source of stress for adults.
  • Financially literate individuals save more, invest better, and retire earlier.

flashcards.definition: The ability to understand and effectively use financial skills including budgeting, investing, and debt management.
Managing individual or family financial activities: income, spending, saving, investing, and protection.
Interest earned on both principal and accumulated interest — Einstein reportedly called it the 'eighth wonder of the world.'
The rate at which prices increase over time, reducing the purchasing power of money (~2-3% annually on average).
Total assets minus total liabilities — the fundamental measure of financial health.

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial literacy includes the ability to:

options.text: Understand and effectively manage budgeting, investing, debt, and financial planning

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately _____ of Americans cannot cover a $1,000 emergency expense from savings.
Compound interest is powerful because it:

options.text: Earns returns on both principal AND previously earned interest, creating exponential growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Inflation at 3% means that $100 today will buy only _____ worth of goods in 10 years.
Net worth is calculated as:
The #1 source of stress for American adults is:
Financial literacy education leads to:

options.text: Higher savings rates, better investment returns, less debt, and earlier retirement

quiz_questions.question_text: The time value of money means:

options.text: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future (because today's dollar can be invested to grow)

quiz_questions.question_text: People with higher financial literacy tend to:

options.text: Plan better for retirement, carry less debt, and build larger emergency funds

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is NOT a component of personal finance?
The Rule of 72 estimates:

options.text: How long it takes money to double at a given interest rate (72 ÷ rate = years)

quiz_questions.question_text: At 6% annual return, money doubles in approximately:
Financial decisions should be based on:

options.text: Informed analysis of costs, benefits, risks, and personal goals

quiz_questions.question_text: A financially literate person understands the difference between:

options.text: Assets (things you own that increase in value) and liabilities (debts that cost you money)

quiz_questions.question_text: The FDIC insures bank deposits up to:
Delayed gratification in finance means:

options.text: Choosing to wait for a larger future reward rather than taking a smaller immediate reward

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial literacy should be taught because:

options.text: Many people make costly financial mistakes due to lack of knowledge, and outcomes can be dramatically improved
It's too complex for most people

quiz_questions.question_text: The pay-yourself-first strategy means:

options.text: Automatically saving/investing a portion of income BEFORE spending on anything else

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial illiteracy costs the average American approximately _____ per year in fees and poor decisions.
The most important financial habit to develop is:

options.text: Living below your means (spending less than you earn) and investing the difference

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Sources of Income (wages, salaries, investments)
summary_sections.content_html:
Sources of Income

Types

  • Earned income: Wages (hourly), salaries (annual), tips, commissions, bonuses.
  • Investment income: Dividends, interest, capital gains, rental income.
  • Passive income: Income requiring minimal ongoing effort (royalties, rental, business ownership).
  • Transfer payments: Government benefits (Social Security, unemployment, disability).

Gross vs. Net Income

Gross income = total earnings before deductions. Net income = take-home pay after taxes, insurance, retirement contributions.

flashcards.definition: Money received from labor: wages, salary, tips, commissions, and bonuses.
Income requiring little active effort: rental income, royalties, dividends from investments.
Total earnings before any deductions (taxes, insurance, retirement contributions).
Take-home pay after all deductions — the money actually available to spend or save.
Profit from selling an asset (stock, property) for more than you paid for it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Wages are typically paid on a(n) _____ basis.
Salary differs from wages in that salary is:

options.text: A fixed annual amount paid in regular installments regardless of hours worked

quiz_questions.question_text: Gross income minus deductions equals:
Common paycheck deductions include:

options.text: Federal/state income taxes, FICA (Social Security + Medicare), health insurance, and retirement contributions

quiz_questions.question_text: FICA taxes fund:

options.text: Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) — totaling 7.65% of gross wages

quiz_questions.question_text: Investment income includes:

options.text: Dividends, interest, capital gains, and rental income

quiz_questions.question_text: A capital gain occurs when you:

options.text: Sell an asset for more than you paid for it

quiz_questions.question_text: Passive income is income that:

options.text: Requires minimal ongoing effort after initial setup (e.g., rental property, royalties, dividend stocks)

quiz_questions.question_text: If someone earns $50,000 gross salary and takes home $38,000, their effective tax/deduction rate is:
Diversified income sources are important because:

options.text: Multiple income streams reduce financial risk if one source is lost

quiz_questions.question_text: Commission-based income is common in:

options.text: Sales (real estate, car sales, financial services) — typically a percentage of sales made

quiz_questions.question_text: Transfer payments include:

options.text: Government benefits: Social Security, unemployment insurance, disability, welfare, veterans' benefits

quiz_questions.question_text: A W-2 form shows:

options.text: Your total earnings and taxes withheld from employment for the year

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1099 form is issued for:

options.text: Independent contractor income, interest, dividends, and other non-employment income

quiz_questions.question_text: Overtime pay (for hourly workers) is typically:

options.text: 1.5× the regular hourly rate for hours exceeding 40 per week (under FLSA)

quiz_questions.question_text: The federal minimum wage (2024) is:
Benefits like health insurance and 401(k) matching are considered:

options.text: Part of total compensation (valuable beyond base salary — can add 20-30% to total comp)

quiz_questions.question_text: Side income or 'gig' economy work:

options.text: Is taxable income that must be reported, even if no W-2 is issued

quiz_questions.question_text: Interest income from a savings account is an example of:
Understanding income sources matters because:

options.text: Knowing how income is earned, taxed, and diversified is essential for financial planning and wealth building

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budgeting Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Budgeting Basics

A budget is a plan for how to allocate income to expenses, savings, and investments.

The 50/30/20 Rule

  • 50% Needs: housing, food, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments.
  • 30% Wants: entertainment, dining out, hobbies, subscriptions.
  • 20% Savings/Debt repayment: emergency fund, retirement, extra debt payments.

Zero-Based Budgeting

Every dollar is assigned a purpose: Income − All allocations = $0. Forces intentional spending.

flashcards.definition: A financial plan allocating income to expenses, savings, and investments over a specific period.
Budget guideline: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings and debt repayment.
Budget where every dollar of income is assigned a specific purpose (income minus all allocations = $0).
Costs that stay the same each period: rent/mortgage, car payment, insurance premiums.
Costs that fluctuate: groceries, utilities, entertainment, clothing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 50/30/20 rule allocates _____ to needs, _____ to wants, and _____ to savings.
In the 50/30/20 rule, 'needs' include:

options.text: Housing, utilities, groceries, insurance, transportation, and minimum debt payments

quiz_questions.question_text: Zero-based budgeting means:

options.text: Assigning every dollar of income a specific purpose so income minus allocations equals zero

quiz_questions.question_text: Fixed expenses differ from variable expenses in that they:

options.text: Remain the same each billing period (rent, car payment, insurance)

quiz_questions.question_text: If someone earns $4,000/month and follows the 50/30/20 rule, savings should be:
The first step in creating a budget is:

options.text: Tracking current income and expenses to understand where money actually goes

quiz_questions.question_text: A budget surplus occurs when:

options.text: Income exceeds expenses (positive cash flow — money left over for savings/investing)

quiz_questions.question_text: A budget deficit means:

options.text: Expenses exceed income (negative cash flow — you're spending more than you earn)

quiz_questions.question_text: Discretionary spending refers to:

options.text: Non-essential purchases you choose to make (entertainment, dining out, hobbies)

quiz_questions.question_text: An envelope budgeting system works by:

options.text: Putting cash for each spending category in separate envelopes — when an envelope is empty, that category is done for the month

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest budgeting mistake most people make is:

options.text: Not tracking their spending (most people significantly underestimate what they spend)

quiz_questions.question_text: Housing costs should ideally not exceed _____ of gross income.
A budget should be reviewed and adjusted:

options.text: Monthly (at minimum) to account for changing circumstances

quiz_questions.question_text: Budgeting apps and spreadsheets help by:

options.text: Automating tracking, categorizing expenses, showing trends, and alerting to overspending

quiz_questions.question_text: An effective budget balances:

options.text: Current needs, future goals, and enjoyment of life (sustainable balance)

quiz_questions.question_text: The pay-yourself-first approach to budgeting means:

options.text: Automatically transfer savings/investments BEFORE spending on anything else

quiz_questions.question_text: Budget categories should include:

options.text: All expenses: housing, food, transport, insurance, debt, entertainment, clothing, personal care, savings, and giving

quiz_questions.question_text: Irregular expenses (car repair, holidays) should be budgeted by:

options.text: Setting aside money monthly in a sinking fund for expected irregular costs

quiz_questions.question_text: The purpose of a budget is NOT to:

options.text: Restrict all enjoyment of life (it's a tool for intentional spending, not deprivation)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, budgeting connects to:

options.text: All other topics: interest, debt management, investing, taxes, and long-term financial planning

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Needs vs. Wants
summary_sections.content_html:
Needs vs. Wants

Needs are essential for survival and basic functioning: food, shelter, clothing, healthcare, transportation to work.

Wants are things that improve quality of life but aren't essential: dining out, streaming services, designer clothing, vacations.

Gray Areas

Many expenses fall between needs and wants. A smartphone may be a need (for work) but the latest model is a want. Basic transportation is a need; a luxury car is a want.

flashcards.definition: Essential expenses required for survival and basic functioning: food, shelter, healthcare, basic clothing, transportation.
Non-essential expenses that enhance quality of life: entertainment, dining out, luxury items, vacations.
The value of the next-best alternative given up when making a choice.
Increasing spending as income rises — upgrading wants to feel like needs.
Choosing to wait for a better outcome rather than taking an immediate, smaller reward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Basic groceries for home cooking are classified as a:
A streaming subscription (Netflix) is classified as a:

options.text: Want (entertainment that enhances life but isn't essential)

quiz_questions.question_text: The distinction between needs and wants is important for budgeting because:

options.text: Needs should be prioritized; wants can be reduced or eliminated when money is tight

quiz_questions.question_text: Lifestyle inflation occurs when:

options.text: Spending increases proportionally with income, preventing wealth building despite earning more

quiz_questions.question_text: A smartphone for someone who needs it for work is:

options.text: A need (the device) but the latest premium model may be a want (basic phone would suffice)

quiz_questions.question_text: Opportunity cost of buying a $1,000 designer bag is:

options.text: The value of what else that $1,000 could have been used for (e.g., invested, saving, emergency fund)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is generally a need?
The 'latte factor' illustrates how:

options.text: Small daily expenses ($5 coffee × 365 days = $1,825/year) add up significantly over time

quiz_questions.question_text: A practical approach to needs vs. wants is:

options.text: Cover all needs first, then allocate remaining funds to wants based on priorities

quiz_questions.question_text: Basic transportation to work is a _____, while a brand-new luxury car is a _____.
When distinguishing needs from wants, consider:

options.text: Whether you could survive and function without it — and if a less expensive alternative would serve the same purpose

quiz_questions.question_text: Impulse purchases are often:

options.text: Wants bought on emotion without considering budget impact or opportunity cost

quiz_questions.question_text: Housing is a need, but _____ is often a want.

options.text: The size, location, or luxury level beyond basic adequate shelter

quiz_questions.question_text: Utilities (electricity, water) are needs, but:

options.text: Usage levels can vary — running AC at 68°F vs. 75°F represents the need/want spectrum

quiz_questions.question_text: Healthcare is a need, and health insurance is:

options.text: Also a need (one medical emergency without insurance can cause financial ruin — average ER visit: ~$2,200)
Too expensive to be a need

quiz_questions.question_text: Children's basic needs include:

options.text: Food, shelter, clothing, healthcare, and education

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'wants' category in budgeting should be:

options.text: Maintained in moderation — a budget that allows no enjoyment is unsustainable

quiz_questions.question_text: Peer pressure often leads to spending on _____ disguised as _____.

options.text: Wants disguised as needs ('everyone has one; I need it too')

quiz_questions.question_text: A financially healthy approach is:

options.text: Consciously choosing which wants bring the most value and joy after all needs are met

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding needs vs. wants helps with all of these EXCEPT:

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Opportunity Cost in Financial Decisions
summary_sections.content_html:
Opportunity Cost

Opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone when making a choice. Every financial decision has an opportunity cost.

Examples

  • Spending $5,000 on vacation vs. investing it (at 8% for 30 years: ~$50,000).
  • Attending college: tuition + 4 years of potential wages vs. higher lifetime earnings.
  • Buying a car with cash vs. investing and financing at low interest.

flashcards.definition: The value of the next-best alternative given up when a choice is made.
A situation where gaining something requires giving up something else.
Money already spent that cannot be recovered — should NOT influence future decisions.
Comparing the additional benefit vs. additional cost of a decision.
Systematic comparison of all costs and benefits of a decision to determine the best option.

quiz_questions.question_text: Opportunity cost is defined as:

options.text: The value of the best alternative you give up when making a choice

quiz_questions.question_text: If you invest $5,000 at 8% for 30 years instead of spending it on vacation, it grows to approximately:
The opportunity cost of attending 4-year college includes:

options.text: Tuition + lost wages during those 4 years (though lifetime earnings are typically much higher with a degree)
No cost since college is an investment

quiz_questions.question_text: A sunk cost is:

options.text: Money already spent that cannot be recovered and should not influence future decisions

quiz_questions.explanation: Don't throw good money after bad.

quiz_questions.question_text: Continuing to watch a bad movie you paid $15 for because 'you already paid' is an example of:

options.text: Sunk cost fallacy (the $15 is gone regardless; staying wastes your time too)

quiz_questions.question_text: Every financial decision involves opportunity cost because:

options.text: Resources (money, time) are limited — choosing one option means giving up another

quiz_questions.question_text: The opportunity cost of keeping $10,000 in a checking account (0% interest) vs. investing at 7% is:

options.text: ~$700 per year in lost investment returns (growing larger each year due to compounding)

quiz_questions.question_text: Marginal analysis asks:

options.text: Does the additional benefit of one more unit exceed the additional cost?

quiz_questions.question_text: A trade-off differs from opportunity cost in that:

options.text: A trade-off is the general concept of giving something up; opportunity cost is the VALUE of the specific best alternative forgone

quiz_questions.explanation: Value of the specific forgone alternative.

quiz_questions.question_text: Renting vs. buying a house involves opportunity costs such as:

options.text: Down payment investment returns, maintenance costs, flexibility, equity building, and tax implications

quiz_questions.question_text: Making a financial decision without considering opportunity cost leads to:

options.text: Potentially suboptimal choices because you haven't evaluated what you're giving up

quiz_questions.question_text: The opportunity cost of paying off a 4% mortgage early instead of investing at 8% is:

options.text: ~4% return differential (you earn 4% less than you could have by investing instead)

quiz_questions.question_text: Time has opportunity cost because:

options.text: Hours spent on one activity can't be spent on another — time spent commuting is time not spent working, learning, or with family

quiz_questions.question_text: A cost-benefit analysis helps with financial decisions by:

options.text: Systematically comparing ALL costs against ALL benefits to identify the best option

quiz_questions.question_text: When choosing between investing in stocks vs. bonds, opportunity cost is:

options.text: The return you give up on the option you don't choose (choose bonds = give up potentially higher stock returns, but with less risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'nothing is free' in economics relates to opportunity cost because:

options.text: Even 'free' things cost time and attention — every choice has an opportunity cost

quiz_questions.question_text: Calculated opportunity cost helps avoid:

options.text: Impulsive decisions by forcing consideration of alternatives before committing resources

quiz_questions.question_text: For a business, the opportunity cost of using retained earnings for expansion is:

options.text: The returns those funds could have earned in alternative investments or as dividends to shareholders

quiz_questions.question_text: Opportunity cost is a _____ concept in financial math.

options.text: Fundamental (it underlies nearly every financial decision from spending to investing to career choices)

quiz_questions.question_text: The best financial decisions are made by:

options.text: Explicitly considering opportunity costs of each option and choosing the one with the greatest net benefit

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Short-Term vs. Long-Term Goals
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Goals

Short-Term Goals (< 1 year)

  • Build emergency fund ($500–$1,000 starter).
  • Pay off a specific credit card.
  • Save for a vacation or purchase.

Medium-Term Goals (1–5 years)

  • Build full emergency fund (3–6 months expenses).
  • Save for down payment on house or car.
  • Pay off student loans.

Long-Term Goals (5+ years)

  • Retirement savings (start early: compound interest!).
  • College fund for children.
  • Achieve financial independence.

flashcards.definition: Financial goals that are Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound.
Savings covering 3–6 months of essential expenses for unexpected events (job loss, medical emergency).
Having sufficient passive income or savings to cover living expenses without active employment.
Initial upfront payment when purchasing an asset (typically 3–20% for a home).
Investing fixed amounts at regular intervals regardless of market price — reduces timing risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: A short-term financial goal is typically achieved within:
An emergency fund should cover:
SMART goals are:

options.text: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound

quiz_questions.question_text: 'Save $5,000 for a car down payment by December 2025' is an example of:

options.text: A SMART goal (specific amount, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound)

quiz_questions.question_text: Starting retirement savings at age 25 vs. 35 makes a huge difference because:

options.text: Compound interest has 10 extra years to work — starting at 25 can result in nearly DOUBLE the retirement savings as starting at 35

quiz_questions.question_text: If you invest $200/month starting at age 25 (at 8% return), by age 65 you'd have approximately:
If you start the same $200/month at age 35 (10 years later), by 65 you'd have approximately:

options.text: ~$300,000 (less than half despite only starting 10 years later)

quiz_questions.question_text: Medium-term financial goals (1–5 years) include:

options.text: Saving for a down payment, paying off student loans, building full emergency fund

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial goal prioritization should typically start with:

options.text: Building a starter emergency fund, then paying off high-interest debt, then building full emergency fund

quiz_questions.question_text: Automating savings helps achieve goals because:

options.text: It removes the temptation to spend first (pay-yourself-first becomes automatic)

quiz_questions.question_text: A sinking fund is:

options.text: Regular savings set aside for a specific planned future expense (vacation, new car, annual insurance premium)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar-cost averaging works by:

options.text: Investing a fixed amount regularly regardless of market price, which averages out the cost per share over time

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest obstacle to achieving financial goals is usually:

options.text: Lack of a specific plan and follow-through (most people have vague goals rather than SMART goals)

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial independence means:

options.text: Having enough passive income or savings to cover living expenses without needing to work for money

quiz_questions.question_text: The FIRE movement (Financial Independence, Retire Early) focuses on:

options.text: Extreme savings rates (50–70% of income) and investing to achieve early financial independence

quiz_questions.question_text: Short-term savings should be kept in _____ accounts; long-term in _____ accounts.

options.text: High-yield savings/CDs (safe, liquid); retirement and investment accounts (growth potential)

quiz_questions.question_text: Adjusting financial goals over time is:

options.text: Normal and necessary — life circumstances change, so goals should be reviewed and updated regularly

quiz_questions.question_text: Competing financial goals (e.g., save vs. pay debt) should be handled by:

options.text: Prioritizing based on interest rates and urgency, potentially doing both at reduced levels
Doing neither until you can afford both

quiz_questions.question_text: Writing down financial goals increases the chance of achieving them because:

options.text: It creates commitment, clarity, and accountability — studies show written goals are significantly more likely to be achieved

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, goal-setting connects to:

options.text: Interest calculations, investment planning, debt repayment schedules, and time-value-of-money concepts

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Personal Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Personal Finance

Case 1: The Power of Starting Early

Investor A saves $200/month from age 22–32 (10 years, $24,000 total) then stops. Investor B saves $200/month from age 32–62 (30 years, $72,000 total). At 8% return, Investor A has MORE at 62 (~$470,000 vs. ~$300,000) despite investing less, because of compound interest and time.

Case 2: Debt Spiral

$5,000 credit card balance at 20% APR, paying only minimums: takes ~20 years to pay off, total paid: ~$12,000 (more than double the original debt).

flashcards.definition: Starting to invest 10 years earlier with less total money can yield MORE than investing 3× more starting later, due to compound interest.
When minimum payments mostly cover interest, causing debt to take decades to pay off and cost multiples of the original amount.
Making only minimum credit card payments extends debt repayment for decades and dramatically increases total cost.
Debt avalanche (highest interest first) saves the most money; debt snowball (smallest balance first) provides psychological wins.
The more frequently interest compounds (daily vs. monthly vs. annually), the more total interest accumulated.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the early vs. late investor case, the early investor wins despite investing LESS because:

options.text: Compound interest had more time to work — 10 extra years of compounding outweighs 20 extra years of contributions
They had a higher return rate

quiz_questions.question_text: A $5,000 credit card balance at 20% APR with minimum payments takes approximately _____ to pay off.
The total amount paid on that $5,000 credit card debt with minimums is approximately:
The debt snowball method pays off:

options.text: Smallest balance first (for psychological motivation from quick wins)

quiz_questions.question_text: The debt avalanche method pays off:

options.text: Highest interest rate first (mathematically optimal — saves the most in total interest)

quiz_questions.question_text: A person earning $60,000 who saves 15% ($9,000/year) at 8% return for 30 years accumulates approximately:
The key lesson from the early investor case study is:

options.text: It doesn't matter when you start
Start investing as early as possible — even small amounts benefit enormously from time and compound interest
Waiting for higher income is better

quiz_questions.explanation: Time is the most valuable asset.

quiz_questions.question_text: Credit card debt is particularly dangerous because:

options.text: Interest rates are very high (15-25%+ APR), and interest compounds on unpaid balances including previous interest
Minimum payments pay it off fast

quiz_questions.question_text: The '30-year mortgage vs. 15-year mortgage' case shows:

options.text: A 30-year mortgage has lower monthly payments but total interest paid is often DOUBLE that of a 15-year mortgage

quiz_questions.question_text: A case study comparing renting vs. buying shows the decision depends on:

options.text: How long you stay (break-even typically 5-7 years), local prices, maintenance costs, opportunity cost of down payment, and tax benefits

quiz_questions.question_text: Emergency fund case studies show that unexpected expenses occur:

options.text: Frequently (car repair, medical bill, job loss) — most people face a significant unexpected expense at least annually

quiz_questions.question_text: A case study of lottery winners shows that many end up bankrupt because:

options.text: Financial literacy is more important than income — without money management skills, even large sums are quickly depleted

quiz_questions.question_text: Case studies of successful savers show common habits:

options.text: Pay-yourself-first, live below means, avoid high-interest debt, invest consistently, and have a written plan

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'millionaire next door' research found that most millionaires:

options.text: Live modestly, save consistently, and avoid lifestyle inflation (most are first-generation wealthy)

quiz_questions.question_text: Case studies in financial math apply concepts to:

options.text: Real-world situations that demonstrate how interest, time, decisions, and behavior affect financial outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: The behavioral finance lesson from case studies is:

options.text: Human psychology (fear, greed, instant gratification) often undermines financial success — awareness helps overcome these tendencies

quiz_questions.question_text: A case study comparing 8% returns vs. 10% returns over 40 years shows that:

options.text: Even 2% difference in returns multiplies into huge wealth differences over decades (over 2× difference at 40 years)

quiz_questions.question_text: Insurance case studies illustrate that going without insurance:

options.text: Is a gamble — one serious event (accident, illness, natural disaster) can cause financial devastation

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, case studies demonstrate that:

options.text: Math is irrelevant to real life
Formulas and concepts directly apply to real-world financial decisions with enormous long-term impact

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important takeaway from personal finance case studies is:

options.text: Financial behavior and consistent good habits matter more than income level for building long-term wealth

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Financial Decision-Making Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Decision-Making Models

Rational Decision-Making Process

  • Step 1: Identify the decision (e.g., buy vs. lease a car).
  • Step 2: Gather information (costs, terms, alternatives).
  • Step 3: Identify alternatives and their opportunity costs.
  • Step 4: Evaluate using criteria (cost, risk, time, alignment with goals).
  • Step 5: Make and implement the decision.
  • Step 6: Review and learn from the outcome.

flashcards.definition: Systematic approach: identify → gather info → list alternatives → evaluate → decide → review.
Study of how psychological biases affect financial decisions (overconfidence, loss aversion, herd behavior).
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains — losing $100 hurts more than gaining $100 feels good.
Over-relying on the first piece of information encountered when making decisions (e.g., original price as anchor).
Tool for comparing options by scoring each against weighted criteria to identify the best choice.

quiz_questions.question_text: The first step in rational financial decision-making is:

options.text: Clearly identifying the decision to be made

quiz_questions.question_text: Behavioral finance studies how _____ affects financial decisions.
Loss aversion means people:

options.text: Feel losses about 2× more intensely than equivalent gains

quiz_questions.question_text: Anchoring bias in shopping occurs when:

options.text: The original 'sale' price (often inflated) makes the discounted price seem like a great deal even if it's not

quiz_questions.question_text: A decision matrix helps by:

options.text: Scoring alternatives against weighted criteria to systematically identify the best option

quiz_questions.question_text: The sunk cost fallacy affects decisions when people:

options.text: Continue investing in something because of what they've already spent, even when quitting would be better

quiz_questions.question_text: Herd behavior in finance means:

options.text: Following what others are doing (buying/selling) without independent analysis — amplifies market bubbles and crashes

quiz_questions.question_text: Confirmation bias causes investors to:

options.text: Only seek information that confirms their existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence

quiz_questions.question_text: Overconfidence bias leads to:

options.text: Excessive trading, under-diversification, and underestimation of risk — overconfident investors often underperform

quiz_questions.question_text: Mental accounting (Thaler) is the tendency to:

options.text: Treat money differently based on its source or intended use (e.g., treating a tax refund as 'found money' and spending it)

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simple Interest Formula
summary_sections.content_html:
Simple Interest

Simple interest is calculated only on the original principal.

Formula

I = P × r × t

  • I = interest earned (or owed)
  • P = principal (initial amount)
  • r = annual interest rate (as decimal)
  • t = time in years

Total amount: A = P + I = P(1 + rt)

Example: $1,000 at 5% for 3 years → I = $1,000 × 0.05 × 3 = $150. Total = $1,150.

flashcards.definition: Interest calculated only on the original principal: I = P × r × t.
The original amount of money deposited or borrowed before interest.
The percentage charged or earned per year, expressed as a decimal in formulas (e.g., 5% = 0.05).
Duration in years for the interest calculation (convert months with t = months ÷ 12).
Principal plus interest: A = P(1 + rt) for simple interest.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Compound Interest Formula
summary_sections.content_html:
Compound Interest

Compound interest is calculated on the principal plus accumulated interest.

Formula

A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)

  • A = final amount
  • P = principal
  • r = annual rate (decimal)
  • n = compounding periods per year
  • t = time in years

Example: $1,000 at 5% compounded monthly for 3 years → A = 1000(1 + 0.05/12)^(12×3) = $1,161.62

flashcards.definition: Interest calculated on principal plus all previously accumulated interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt).
How often interest is calculated per year: annually (1), semi-annually (2), quarterly (4), monthly (12), daily (365).
The effective annual rate accounting for compounding — always ≥ APR for the same nominal rate.
Each interval after which interest is calculated and added to the balance.
The value of an investment at a specific future date, accounting for compound interest growth.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Continuous Compounding
summary_sections.content_html:
Continuous Compounding

When interest compounds infinitely often, we use the continuous compounding formula:

A = Pe^(rt)

  • e ≈ 2.71828 (Euler's number)
  • This is the mathematical limit of (1 + r/n)^(nt) as n → ∞

Example: $1,000 at 5% continuously for 3 years → A = 1000e^(0.05×3) = 1000e^0.15 = $1,161.83

flashcards.definition: Interest compounded infinitely often: A = Pe^(rt), where e ≈ 2.71828.
Mathematical constant ≈ 2.71828; base of the natural logarithm; limit of (1+1/n)^n as n→∞.
The inverse of e^x; used to solve for time or rate in continuous compounding: t = ln(A/P)/r.
The rate at which a quantity grows when modeled with continuous compounding.
As compounding frequency n→∞, A = P(1+r/n)^(nt) approaches A = Pe^(rt).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Growth vs. Decay Models
summary_sections.content_html:
Exponential Growth & Decay in Finance

Growth: A = P(1 + r)^t

Investments, savings, economic growth — amount increases over time.

Decay: A = P(1 − r)^t

Depreciation, inflation erosion, declining values — amount decreases over time.

Example (depreciation): Car purchased for $30,000 depreciating at 15%/year → After 5 years: 30000(1−0.15)^5 = $13,312

flashcards.definition: Amount increases by a fixed percentage each period: A = P(1+r)^t (investments, savings).
Amount decreases by a fixed percentage each period: A = P(1−r)^t (depreciation, purchasing power).
The decrease in value of an asset over time (cars, equipment, buildings) — typically modeled as exponential decay.
What a dollar can actually buy — decreases over time due to inflation (decay of money's value).
Time for an asset to lose half its value: t = ln(0.5)/ln(1−r).

summary_sections.content_html:
Savings Account Applications

Types of Savings Vehicles

  • Regular savings: Low interest (~0.01–0.5% at traditional banks).
  • High-yield savings: ~4–5% APY (online banks, 2024).
  • Certificates of Deposit (CDs): Fixed rate for fixed term; higher rate for longer terms.
  • Money market accounts: Higher rates than regular savings; may have check-writing privileges.

FDIC Insurance

US bank deposits insured up to $250,000 per depositor per institution.

summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Banking

Case 1: Traditional vs. Online Banking

Sarah has $25,000 in emergency savings. Traditional bank: 0.05% APY = $12.50/year. Online high-yield: 4.5% APY = $1,125/year. Difference: $1,112/year for the same FDIC-insured money.

Mike uses a CD ladder: $10,000 split into 5 CDs (1-5 year terms). As each matures, he reinvests at the longest term. Balances access with higher rates.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Loan Terminology & Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Loan Fundamentals

  • Principal: Amount borrowed.
  • Interest rate: Cost of borrowing, expressed as APR (Annual Percentage Rate).
  • Term: Loan duration (e.g., 30-year mortgage, 5-year auto loan).
  • Collateral: Asset pledged to secure a loan (home for mortgage, car for auto).
  • Secured vs. unsecured: Secured has collateral (lower rate); unsecured does not (higher rate).

Key Concepts

  • APR: Total annual cost of borrowing including fees.
  • Down payment: Upfront portion paid to reduce principal borrowed.
  • Amortization: Gradual repayment through scheduled payments.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Amortization Schedules
summary_sections.content_html:
Amortization Schedules

An amortization schedule shows the breakdown of each payment into principal and interest.

Monthly Payment Formula

PMT = P × r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n − 1]

  • P = loan principal
  • r = monthly rate (annual rate ÷ 12)
  • n = total number of monthly payments

Early payments are mostly interest; later payments are mostly principal.

flashcards.definition: Table showing each payment's split between principal reduction and interest expense over the life of a loan.
PMT = P × r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n − 1] where r = monthly rate and n = total payments.
Early loan payments go mostly toward interest; principal reduction accelerates in later payments.
Additional payments applied to principal reduce total interest and shorten the loan term significantly.
After each payment, the outstanding principal = previous balance − principal portion of that payment.

quiz_questions.question_text: The loan payment formula PMT = P × r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n − 1] gives:

options.text: The fixed monthly payment amount that amortizes the loan over n periods

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mortgage Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Mortgage Calculations

Key Numbers

  • Home price − down payment = loan amount
  • Total monthly cost = PITI (Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance)
  • Affordability rule: Housing should be ≤ 28% of gross monthly income.

Example

Home: $350,000. Down: $70,000 (20%). Loan: $280,000 at 6.5% for 30 years.

Monthly P&I: ~$1,770. Taxes: ~$365. Insurance: ~$150. Total PITI: ~$2,285.

flashcards.definition: Total monthly mortgage cost: Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance.
Housing costs should not exceed 28% of gross monthly income for affordability.
One-time fees at purchase: typically 2–5% of loan amount (appraisal, title, origination, etc.).
Account held by lender to pay property taxes and insurance from your monthly payment.
Upfront fees paid to reduce the interest rate; 1 point = 1% of the loan amount.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Credit Card Mathematics
summary_sections.content_html:
Credit Card Mathematics

Key Features

  • APR: Typical credit card APR ranges from 15–25%.
  • Daily rate: APR ÷ 365 (used for daily balance calculations).
  • Minimum payment: Often 1–3% of balance or $25 (whichever is greater).
  • Grace period: ~25 days to pay in full before interest accrues on purchases.

Example: $5,000 balance at 20% APR with minimum payments only → takes ~25 years and costs ~$8,400 in interest!

flashcards.definition: Annual Percentage Rate on a credit card, typically 15–25%; applied daily to the outstanding balance.
Paying only the minimum extends repayment to decades and costs many times the original purchase.
~25 days after the billing cycle ends where no interest accrues if you pay in full each month.
Moving debt from one card to another (often at a promotional 0% rate) to save on interest.
The percentage of your available credit that you're using; keeping it < 30% helps your credit score.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Student Loan Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Student Loan Analysis

Types

  • Federal subsidized: Government pays interest while in school.
  • Federal unsubsidized: Interest accrues while in school.
  • Private loans: From banks/lenders; usually higher rates, fewer protections.

Repayment Plans

  • Standard (10-year): Fixed payments, least total interest.
  • Income-driven: Payments based on income (IBR, PAYE, REPAYE).
  • Extended (25-year): Lower payments but more total interest.

flashcards.definition: Federal student loan where the government pays interest while you're in school at least half-time.
Federal student loan where interest accrues from disbursement — even while in school.
Unpaid interest that is added to the principal balance, increasing the amount on which future interest is calculated.
Plans that cap monthly payments at a percentage (10-20%) of discretionary income.
Programs that cancel remaining student loan balance after meeting certain conditions (e.g., PSLF after 10 years of public service).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Real Estate & Auto Loan Math
summary_sections.content_html:
Real Estate & Auto Loan Math

Auto Loans

  • Typical terms: 36, 48, 60, or 72 months.
  • New car: ~5–7% APR; Used car: ~7–10% APR (2024).
  • Cars depreciate ~20% in year 1, ~60% over 5 years.

Real Estate Investment

  • Rental income − expenses = cash flow
  • Cap rate = Net Operating Income / Property Value
  • ROI considers appreciation, cash flow, tax benefits, and equity building.

flashcards.definition: Duration of car financing; longer terms = lower payments but more interest + risk of being underwater.
Owing more on a loan than the asset is worth — common with long auto loans due to rapid depreciation.
Capitalization Rate = Net Operating Income / Property Value; measures investment property return.
Rental income minus all expenses (mortgage, taxes, insurance, maintenance, vacancy).
The portion of the property you own: market value − remaining mortgage balance.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Debt Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Debt Management

Case 1: Debt Avalanche

Maria has 3 debts: Credit card ($5,000 at 22%), Student loan ($20,000 at 6%), Car loan ($15,000 at 5%). Extra $300/month. Avalanche: Pay minimums on all; put extra toward 22% card first. Saves the most interest.

Case 2: Debt Consolidation

James consolidates $25,000 in credit card debt at 20% average into a personal loan at 10%. Monthly savings: ~$200. Total savings: ~$8,000.

flashcards.definition: Pay off highest-interest debt first while making minimum payments on others — mathematically optimal strategy.
Pay off smallest balance first for psychological wins; mathematically costs more but motivates many people.
Combining multiple debts into a single loan at a lower interest rate to simplify and reduce cost.
Total monthly debt payments ÷ gross monthly income; lenders use this to assess borrowing capacity.
Structured approach to eliminating debt: assess all debts, choose a strategy, automate payments, build emergency fund.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Loan Comparison & Decision-Making
summary_sections.content_html:
Loan Comparison & Decision-Making

Comparing Loan Offers

  • Compare total cost: Not just monthly payment — calculate total payments over life of loan.
  • APR vs. interest rate: APR includes fees; same interest rate can have different APRs.
  • Opportunity cost: Could the money earn more invested than the interest saved?

Decision Framework

1. Calculate total cost of each option. 2. Consider flexibility and risks. 3. Account for opportunity costs. 4. Choose based on complete analysis.

flashcards.definition: Sum of all payments − principal = total interest; the true measure of borrowing cost.
Compare loans by APR (not just interest rate) because APR includes fees for a more complete picture.
If loan rate < potential investment return, extra money may be better invested than used for extra payments.
Loan amount ÷ property value; lower LTV = less risk = better terms from lenders.
Calculate how long it takes for savings from a financial decision (e.g., refinancing) to exceed the costs.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important metric when comparing loans is:

options.text: Total cost (total payments over the life of the loan — not just the monthly payment)

quiz_questions.question_text: Two loans: A) $200K at 6% for 30 years; B) $200K at 5.75% with $6,000 in fees. The better deal:

options.text: Requires calculating total cost of each (fees add to B but lower rate saves over time)

quiz_questions.question_text: Refinancing costs $4,000 and saves $150/month. Break-even point:

options.text: ~27 months ($4,000 ÷ $150/month) — worth it if you stay longer than ~2.25 years

quiz_questions.question_text: If your mortgage rate is 4% and investments return 8%, extra money is mathematically better:

options.text: Investing (8% return > 4% loan cost, though this assumes consistent returns and ignores risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: However, paying off a 4% mortgage gives a _____ return vs. uncertain 8% investment return.

options.text: Guaranteed 4% (risk-free — the emotional value of being debt-free also matters)

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0% auto loan vs. $3,000 cash discount on $30,000: the better choice depends on:

options.text: What rate you'd get otherwise and what you can earn on the $3,000 if investing it

quiz_questions.question_text: Loan A: 36 months at 3%, payment $291, total $10,476. Loan B: 60 months at 5%, payment $189, total $11,340. Best:

options.text: Loan A (less total cost — saves $864 despite higher payment)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Loan Estimate form (required by law) shows:

options.text: APR, monthly payment, closing costs, total interest, and other key terms for comparison

quiz_questions.question_text: When comparing adjustable vs. fixed-rate mortgages, consider:

options.text: The worst-case scenario (how high could the ARM go?), how long you'll own the home, and rate environment

quiz_questions.question_text: Paying 'points' (each = 1% of loan) to lower the rate makes sense if:

options.text: You stay long enough to recoup the upfront cost through lower monthly payments (calculate the break-even)

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 personal loan: Bank A offers 8% for 3 years; Bank B offers 7% for 5 years. Total interest:

quiz_questions.question_text: Federal student loans vs. private for the same amount:

options.text: Federal usually offers better protections (income-driven plans, forgiveness, forbearance) even if rates are similar

quiz_questions.question_text: When choosing between investing extra money vs. paying down debt, a key consideration is:

options.text: Tax implications (mortgage interest deduction, tax-advantaged investment accounts), risk tolerance, and behavioral factors

quiz_questions.question_text: Biweekly mortgage payments vs. monthly: making 26 half-payments = 13 full payments/year saves:

options.text: Thousands in interest over the life of the loan AND several years off the term

quiz_questions.question_text: HELOC (Home Equity Line of Credit) is typically:

options.text: A revolving credit line secured by your home — variable rate, interest-only option, tax-deductible interest (with limits)

quiz_questions.question_text: Consolidation loan at 10% for $25,000 cards at 22% average: annual interest savings ≈:
The cognitive bias of 'payment anchoring' means people:

options.text: Focus on monthly payment instead of total cost — a lower payment can cost far more over a longer term

quiz_questions.question_text: A comprehensive loan decision should consider:

options.text: Total cost, APR, term flexibility, prepayment options, fees, opportunity cost, and personal financial situation

quiz_questions.question_text: Good-faith estimate replaced by Loan Estimate and Closing Disclosure under _____ regulations.

options.text: TRID (TILA-RESPA Integrated Disclosure) rules — designed to help consumers compare offers

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, loan comparison requires:

options.text: Combining amortization, present/future value, break-even analysis, and opportunity cost into comprehensive decision-making

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Stocks, Bonds, & Mutual Funds
summary_sections.content_html:
Core Investment Types

Stocks

  • Shares of ownership in a company.
  • Returns: dividends + capital gains (price increase).
  • Higher risk, higher potential return.

Bonds

  • Loans to a government or corporation.
  • Returns: regular interest (coupon) payments + face value at maturity.
  • Lower risk, lower return.

Mutual Funds & ETFs

  • Pooled investments holding many stocks/bonds.
  • Instant diversification; managed (mutual funds) or passive (index ETFs).

flashcards.definition: A share of ownership in a company; returns come from dividends and price appreciation (capital gains).
A loan to a government or corporation; pays regular interest (coupon) and returns face value at maturity.
A pooled investment fund managed by a professional that holds a diversified portfolio of stocks, bonds, or both.
Similar to a mutual fund but trades on an exchange like a stock; often tracks an index with low fees.
A portion of a company's profits distributed to shareholders, typically quarterly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A stock represents:
A bond represents:

options.text: A loan to a government or corporation

quiz_questions.question_text: Stocks generally offer _____ risk and _____ potential return than bonds.
Average annual return of the S&P 500 (historically) is approximately:
The coupon rate on a bond is:

options.text: The annual interest payment as a percentage of face value

quiz_questions.question_text: A $1,000 bond with a 5% coupon pays _____ per year in interest.
Mutual funds provide _____ through holding many different investments.
Index funds track a market index (like S&P 500) and have:

options.text: Very low fees (since they're passively managed, not actively picked)

quiz_questions.question_text: The expense ratio of a mutual fund represents:

options.text: The annual fee charged as a percentage of your investment (e.g., 0.03% for index funds vs. 1%+ for active)

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1% expense ratio on $100,000 costs _____ per year.
$100,000 over 30 years at 7%: with 0.1% fees = $740K vs. 1% fees = $574K. Lost to fees:
Dividends can be:

options.text: Reinvested (DRIP) to buy more shares, accelerating compound growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Capital gains occur when:

options.text: You sell an investment for more than you paid for it

quiz_questions.question_text: Bond prices and interest rates move:

options.text: Inversely (when rates rise, bond prices fall; when rates fall, bond prices rise)

quiz_questions.question_text: A bond's credit rating (AAA, AA, etc.) indicates:

options.text: The likelihood the issuer will repay (higher rating = lower risk = lower yield)

quiz_questions.question_text: Government bonds are considered low-risk because:

options.text: They're backed by the government's ability to tax (virtually no default risk for US Treasuries)

quiz_questions.question_text: ETFs differ from mutual funds in that they:

options.text: Trade throughout the day like stocks (mutual funds only trade at end of day) and often have lower fees

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar-cost averaging means:

options.text: Investing a fixed amount regularly regardless of price (automatically buying more shares when prices are low)

quiz_questions.question_text: For a young investor with a 40-year horizon, the recommended allocation is typically:

options.text: Mostly stocks (aggressive growth — time to recover from market drops)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding stocks, bonds, and funds is essential for:

options.text: Everyone — retirement savings, college funds, and financial security depend on investment knowledge

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk vs. Return
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk vs. Return

Core Principle

Higher potential returns come with higher risk. The key is finding the right balance for your situation.

Risk Spectrum

  • Low risk: Savings accounts, CDs, T-bills (1–5% return).
  • Medium risk: Bonds, balanced funds (4–8% return).
  • High risk: Stocks, real estate, crypto (8–12%+ return, with volatility).

Measuring Risk

Standard deviation measures how much returns vary from the average.

flashcards.definition: Higher expected returns require accepting higher risk — there is no free lunch in investing.
Statistical measure of volatility; higher SD = wider range of possible outcomes (more risk).
The degree of variation in investment returns — high volatility means large price swings.
An individual's ability and willingness to endure investment losses; depends on time horizon, income, and temperament.
Measure of a stock's volatility relative to the overall market; beta > 1 means more volatile than the market.

quiz_questions.question_text: The risk-return tradeoff means:

options.text: Higher potential returns require accepting higher risk
Low risk investments earn the most

quiz_questions.question_text: Which has the lowest risk?

options.text: US Treasury bills (essentially risk-free — backed by the full faith and credit of the US government)

quiz_questions.question_text: Which has the highest expected return over 30+ years?
Standard deviation measures:

options.text: How much returns vary from their average (higher SD = more unpredictable outcomes)

quiz_questions.question_text: An investment with returns of 8%, 12%, 6%, 14% has _____ risk than one with 9%, 10%, 9.5%, 10.5%.

options.text: More (wider variation in returns = higher standard deviation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Beta of 1.5 means a stock is:

options.text: 50% more volatile than the market (if market drops 10%, this stock might drop 15%)

quiz_questions.question_text: Risk tolerance is influenced by:

options.text: Age, time horizon, income stability, financial goals, family situation, and personal temperament

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old saving for retirement in 40 years can tolerate _____ risk than a 60-year-old retiring in 5 years.

options.text: More (more time to recover from market downturns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Diversification reduces risk by:

options.text: Spreading investments across different assets so poor performance in one is offset by better performance in others

quiz_questions.explanation: Don't put all eggs in one basket.

quiz_questions.question_text: Systematic risk (market risk) is:

options.text: Risk affecting the entire market that CANNOT be diversified away (recession, inflation, war)

quiz_questions.question_text: Unsystematic risk (specific risk) is:

options.text: Risk specific to a company or industry that CAN be reduced through diversification

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sharpe ratio measures:

options.text: Risk-adjusted return (return above risk-free rate per unit of risk — higher is better)

quiz_questions.question_text: A portfolio with 12% return and 15% SD vs. 10% return and 8% SD: risk-adjusted, the second is _____ risky per unit of return.

options.text: Less (lower SD relative to return = better risk-adjusted performance)

quiz_questions.question_text: Inflation risk means:

options.text: Your investment returns may not keep up with rising prices, reducing purchasing power

quiz_questions.question_text: Keeping all savings in cash carries _____ risk.

options.text: Inflation risk (your money loses purchasing power over time even though the nominal amount stays the same)

quiz_questions.question_text: Liquidity risk is:

options.text: The risk of not being able to sell an investment quickly without significant loss of value (e.g., real estate)

quiz_questions.question_text: The efficient frontier shows:

options.text: The set of portfolios offering the highest expected return for each level of risk (optimal portfolios)

quiz_questions.question_text: Asset allocation (stocks vs. bonds vs. cash mix) accounts for approximately _____ of portfolio performance variation.

options.text: ~90% (much more important than individual stock picking or market timing)

quiz_questions.question_text: Modern Portfolio Theory suggests:

options.text: Combining assets with different correlations (that don't move together) reduces overall portfolio risk

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding risk vs. return helps you:

options.text: Make informed decisions aligned with your goals, time horizon, and comfort level — not react emotionally to markets

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Annuities & Pension Calculations
summary_sections.content_html:
Annuities

Future Value of an Annuity

FV = PMT × [(1+r)^n − 1] / r

Calculates what regular payments grow to over time.

Present Value of an Annuity

PV = PMT × [1 − (1+r)^(−n)] / r

Calculates the lump sum value of a stream of future payments.

Example

$500/month at 7%/12 for 30 years: FV = 500 × [(1.00583)^360 − 1]/0.00583 ≈ $566,765

flashcards.definition: A series of equal payments made at regular intervals (monthly, quarterly, annually).
Future value of regular payments: FV = PMT × [(1+r)^n − 1] / r.
Present value of future payment stream: PV = PMT × [1 − (1+r)^(−n)] / r.
Payments made at the BEGINNING of each period (vs. ordinary annuity at end); FV is slightly higher.
Regular retirement income guaranteed by an employer; value calculated as present value of future annuity payments.

options.text: At the beginning of each period (slightly more valuable due to one extra period of compounding)

options.text: Calculate PV of pension (~$597,000) and compare — the pension is worth more in this case

quiz_questions.question_text: Deferred annuities:

options.text: Delay payments until a future date (e.g., start paying at age 65); money compounds during the deferral period

quiz_questions.question_text: The risk of a pension is:

options.text: The employer may not fully fund the pension (underfunding) or the company may go bankrupt

quiz_questions.question_text: PBGC (Pension Benefit Guaranty Corporation) protects:

options.text: Some pension benefits if an employer's defined benefit pension plan fails (up to limits)

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing the payment by _____ dramatically increases the final FV of an annuity.

options.text: Even small increases ($50 extra/month can add $100K+ over 30 years at 7%)

quiz_questions.question_text: An annuity in insurance context means:

options.text: An insurance product that converts a lump sum into a guaranteed income stream (often for retirement)

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest weakness of fixed annuity products is:

options.text: Fees (often 2-3%+ annually in variable annuities) and limited liquidity — may not be best for most people

quiz_questions.question_text: Social Security payments are essentially:

options.text: A government annuity — lifetime monthly payments based on your earnings history

quiz_questions.question_text: Annuity calculations in financial math demonstrate:

options.text: Time value of money, compound interest, and how regular small contributions grow into substantial sums

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Retirement Account Planning
summary_sections.content_html:
Retirement Account Planning

Account Types

  • 401(k)/403(b): Employer-sponsored; pre-tax contributions; employer match = free money.
  • Traditional IRA: Tax-deductible contributions; taxed on withdrawal.
  • Roth IRA: After-tax contributions; tax-FREE growth and withdrawals.

Key Numbers (2024)

  • 401(k) max: $23,000/year ($30,500 if 50+).
  • IRA max: $7,000/year ($8,000 if 50+).
  • Full Social Security age: 67 for those born after 1960.

flashcards.definition: Employer-sponsored retirement plan with pre-tax contributions; many employers offer matching contributions.
Individual retirement account funded with after-tax dollars; all growth and qualified withdrawals are tax-free.
Individual retirement account with tax-deductible contributions; withdrawals in retirement are taxed as income.
Employer contributes to your 401(k) based on your contributions (e.g., 50% match up to 6% = instant 50% return).
Mandatory annual withdrawals from traditional retirement accounts starting at age 73.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 401(k) uses _____ contributions.

options.text: Pre-tax (reduces your taxable income now; you pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement)

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth IRA uses _____ contributions.

options.text: After-tax (you pay taxes now, but all growth and qualified withdrawals are completely tax-free)

quiz_questions.question_text: An employer match of 50% up to 6% of salary on a $60,000 salary means the employer contributes:
Not contributing enough to get the full employer match is:

options.text: Leaving free money on the table (the match is an instant 50-100% return on your contribution)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2024 401(k) contribution limit is:
$500/month into a 401(k) from age 25 to 65 at 8% grows to approximately:
The tax advantage of a Roth IRA for a young person is:

options.text: Tax-free growth for decades — contributions are taxed now (lower bracket) but all growth is NEVER taxed

quiz_questions.question_text: $7,000/year Roth IRA from age 22 to 67 at 8% grows to approximately:
Traditional IRA vs. Roth IRA choice depends primarily on:

options.text: Whether you expect to be in a higher tax bracket now or in retirement

quiz_questions.question_text: The general rule of thumb for retirement savings rate is:
The 4% rule suggests retirees can withdraw _____ annually from savings.

options.text: 4% of initial portfolio (adjusted for inflation) with low risk of running out over 30 years

quiz_questions.question_text: A $1,000,000 retirement portfolio with the 4% rule provides approximately _____ per year.
Social Security replaces approximately _____ of pre-retirement income for average earners.

options.text: ~40% (not enough alone — personal savings are critical)

quiz_questions.question_text: Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) from traditional accounts start at age:
A Roth IRA has _____ required minimum distributions.

options.text: No RMDs (Roth IRAs do not require withdrawals at any age during your lifetime)

quiz_questions.question_text: Catch-up contributions for those 50+ allow:

options.text: Additional contributions above the standard limit ($7,500 extra for 401(k), $1,000 extra for IRA in 2024)

quiz_questions.question_text: Early withdrawal from a 401(k) before 59½ incurs:

options.text: 10% penalty PLUS income taxes on the withdrawal (few exceptions apply)

quiz_questions.question_text: Target-date funds automatically:

options.text: Adjust allocation from aggressive (more stocks) to conservative (more bonds) as the target retirement date approaches

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest factor in retirement savings is:

options.text: Starting early — time for compounding is irreplaceable (20s vs. 30s can mean doubling your final amount)

quiz_questions.question_text: Retirement planning combines concepts of:

options.text: Compound interest, annuities, tax optimization, risk management, and inflation protection

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Long-Term Investment Strategies
summary_sections.content_html:
Long-Term Investment Strategies

Core Strategies

  • Buy and hold: Invest consistently; ignore short-term fluctuations.
  • Dollar-cost averaging: Fixed amount invested regularly captures market dips.
  • Rebalancing: Periodically adjust portfolio to maintain target allocation.
  • Tax-loss harvesting: Sell losers to offset gains and reduce tax burden.

Key insight: Time in the market beats timing the market.

flashcards.definition: Strategy of purchasing and holding investments long-term regardless of short-term market volatility.
Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals; automatically buys more shares when prices are low.
Periodically selling winners and buying laggards to maintain your target asset allocation.
Selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains, reducing your tax bill.
The percentage split between stocks, bonds, and cash — the most important investment decision.

quiz_questions.question_text: 'Time in the market beats timing the market' means:

options.text: Staying invested consistently outperforms trying to predict market highs and lows

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar-cost averaging helps by:

options.text: Buying more shares when prices are low and fewer when high, resulting in a lower average cost per share

quiz_questions.question_text: If you invest $500/month and miss the 10 best market days over 20 years, your return drops by roughly:

options.text: ~50% or more (missing just a few key days dramatically hurts long-term returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Rebalancing a 60/40 stock/bond portfolio means:

options.text: Selling some of the outperforming asset and buying the underperforming one to restore 60/40 (sell high, buy low automatically)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-loss harvesting is useful because:

options.text: Selling at a loss offsets capital gains, reducing your tax bill while reinvesting in similar (but not identical) assets

quiz_questions.question_text: The S&P 500 has had a positive return over every _____ rolling period in history.

options.text: 20-year (no 20-year period has ever lost money — patience is rewarded)

quiz_questions.question_text: Asset allocation should shift toward more bonds as you:

options.text: Approach retirement (reducing risk as your time horizon shortens)

quiz_questions.question_text: A common age-based allocation rule is:

options.text: Stock % = 110 − your age (so a 30-year-old: 80% stocks, 20% bonds)

quiz_questions.question_text: Compound annual growth rate (CAGR) measures:

options.text: The smoothed annual return over a period (as if growth were constant each year)

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 growing to $40,000 over 20 years has a CAGR of:
The impact of inflation on long-term investments means:

options.text: You need real returns (above inflation) — a 10% return with 3% inflation is really ~7% growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Index fund investing follows the philosophy that:

options.text: Most actively managed funds underperform the index over time after fees — so just match the market cheaply

quiz_questions.question_text: Automatic investment plans help because:

options.text: They remove emotion from investing decisions and ensure consistent contributions regardless of market conditions

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest enemy of long-term investing is:

options.text: Emotional decision-making (selling during crashes, chasing hot stocks, stopping contributions during downturns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Value investing means buying:

options.text: Stocks trading below their intrinsic value (undervalued by the market) — buy good companies at a discount

quiz_questions.question_text: Growth investing focuses on:

options.text: Companies with above-average growth potential (may look expensive but expected to grow into valuation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dividend reinvestment (DRIP) accelerates growth because:

options.text: Automatically buying more shares with dividends creates compound growth on your growth

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 invested at 7% with dividends reinvested vs. not, after 30 years:

options.text: Reinvested: ~$76,100 vs. Not reinvested: ~$40,000 + dividends spent (reinvestment nearly doubles outcomes)

quiz_questions.question_text: The three keys to long-term investment success are:

options.text: Start early, invest consistently, stay the course (time + discipline + patience)

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term strategies in financial math emphasize:

options.text: The mathematical power of compound interest, consistent contributions, and time as the dominant wealth-building factors

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Diversification & Portfolio Theory
summary_sections.content_html:
Diversification & Portfolio Theory

Core Concepts

  • Diversification: Spreading investments across different assets to reduce risk without sacrificing return.
  • Correlation: How assets move relative to each other. Low or negative correlation provides the best diversification.
  • Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT): Optimal portfolios maximize return for a given level of risk.

flashcards.definition: Spreading investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce risk.
Statistical measure (−1 to +1) of how two assets move relative to each other; lower = better diversification.
Framework showing how to construct portfolios that maximize expected return for a given risk level.
Curve showing optimal portfolios that offer the best expected return for each level of risk.
Market risk (can't be diversified away) vs. company-specific risk (can be diversified away).

quiz_questions.question_text: Diversification reduces:

options.text: Unsystematic (company-specific) risk — but not systematic (market-wide) risk

quiz_questions.question_text: Correlation of +1 between two assets means:

options.text: They move exactly together (no diversification benefit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Correlation of −1 between two assets means:

options.text: They move exactly opposite (perfect diversification — one's loss is the other's gain)

quiz_questions.question_text: For diversification, you want assets with _____ correlation.

options.text: Low or negative (they don't move in the same direction at the same time)

quiz_questions.question_text: A portfolio of 30 stocks from one sector vs. 30 stocks from different sectors:

options.text: Different sectors provide much better diversification (sector-specific risks are reduced)

quiz_questions.question_text: International diversification helps because:

options.text: Different economies/markets don't perfectly correlate with US markets, reducing overall portfolio risk

quiz_questions.question_text: The efficient frontier shows portfolios that:

options.text: Offer the highest expected return for each level of risk (or lowest risk for each expected return)

quiz_questions.question_text: A portfolio below the efficient frontier is:

options.text: Sub-optimal (could achieve higher return for the same risk, or same return with less risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: How many stocks are needed to diversify away most unsystematic risk?

options.text: ~20–30 stocks across different sectors and sizes

quiz_questions.question_text: Adding bonds to a stock portfolio typically:

options.text: Reduces overall risk while modestly reducing expected return (bonds and stocks have low correlation)

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60% stock / 40% bond portfolio is considered:

options.text: Moderate (balances growth potential with stability; suitable for many investors)

quiz_questions.question_text: During the 2008 financial crisis, diversification across stocks:

options.text: Was limited because ALL stock sectors declined together (systematic risk can't be diversified away)

quiz_questions.question_text: REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts) add diversification because:

options.text: They provide real estate exposure with different return drivers than traditional stocks and bonds

quiz_questions.question_text: Commodities (gold, oil) can diversify a portfolio because:

options.text: They often have low or negative correlation with stocks — gold especially tends to rise during market stress

quiz_questions.question_text: Over-diversification (diworsification) can occur when:

options.text: Adding investments doesn't reduce risk further but increases complexity and fees

quiz_questions.question_text: The capital asset pricing model (CAPM) says expected return depends on:

options.text: Beta (systematic risk) — the market rewards bearing systematic risk, not unsystematic risk

quiz_questions.question_text: Index funds automatically provide diversification because they:

options.text: Hold hundreds or thousands of stocks matching an index, spreading risk broadly at very low cost

quiz_questions.question_text: Target-date funds diversify across:

options.text: US stocks, international stocks, bonds, and sometimes other assets — automatically rebalancing over time

quiz_questions.question_text: The key takeaway of portfolio theory is:

options.text: The whole portfolio's risk and return matter more than any individual investment — diversification improves the risk/return profile

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, portfolio theory uses:

options.text: Statistics (correlation, standard deviation), optimization (efficient frontier), and probability to make better investment decisions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Case Studies in Investing
summary_sections.content_html:
Case Studies in Investing

Case 1: Dollar-Cost Averaging vs. Lump Sum

Alex inherits $60,000. Option A: Invest all now. Option B: $5,000/month for 12 months. Historically, lump sum wins ~66% of the time (market tends to go up), but DCA reduces regret risk.

Case 2: The Power of Starting Early

Emily saves $300/month from age 22-32 (10 years, $36,000 total). Mark saves $300/month from age 32-62 (30 years, $108,000 total). At 8%: Emily has $478,000; Mark has $440,000.

flashcards.definition: Investing all at once (lump sum) historically beats DCA ~66% of the time, but DCA reduces timing risk.
Starting 10 years earlier can outperform even 3× longer investing periods due to compounding.
Emotional reactions (panic selling, FOMO buying) are the biggest destroyers of investment returns.
Markets have recovered from every crash in history; staying invested during downturns is critical.
Even 1% in annual fees can cost hundreds of thousands over a career due to compounding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Historically, lump sum investing beats DCA approximately:
DCA is psychologically beneficial because:

options.text: It reduces the regret of investing everything right before a market drop

Someone who panics and sells during a 30% market crash and buys back after recovery:

options.text: Locks in losses and misses the recovery (selling low and buying high — the worst possible behavior)

options.text: Won decisively (S&P 500 index fund: 125.8% return vs. hedge funds: ~36%)

quiz_questions.question_text: During the 2008 crash, the S&P 500 dropped ~50%. By 2013 it:

options.text: Had fully recovered and hit new all-time highs (patience was rewarded)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'coffee cup' example: $5/day invested at 8% for 40 years grows to:
A couple earning $100K, saving 15%, with employer match 50% up to 6%:

options.text: Saves $15K + $3K match = $18K/year; after 30 years at 7% ≈ $1.7 million

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-advantaged accounts (Roth IRA) vs. taxable accounts at the same return:

options.text: Roth grows much more because gains are tax-free (no annual tax drag on dividends/gains)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'lost decade' (2000-2010) for US large-cap stocks:

options.text: Was recovered by 2013; international diversification helped during that period

quiz_questions.question_text: Cryptocurrency as an investment:

options.text: Is highly volatile and speculative; should only be a small portion (if any) of a diversified portfolio

quiz_questions.question_text: Real estate investment: buying a home to live in is _____ a pure investment.

options.text: Often not comparable to a pure investment (maintenance, taxes, illiquidity — but provides housing value)

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest lesson from investing case studies is:

options.text: Start early, invest consistently, keep costs low, diversify, and stay the course through ups and downs

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math exams, investment case studies test:

options.text: Calculation of future values, comparison of scenarios, understanding of compound interest, and critical analysis of strategies

quiz_questions.question_text: The fundamental equation of wealth building is:

options.text: Earn − Spend = Savings → Invest wisely over time → Compound interest builds wealth

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Investment Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Investment Analysis

Tools

  • Robo-advisors: Automated portfolio management (Betterment, Wealthfront). Low cost, diversified.
  • Brokerage platforms: Fidelity, Schwab, Vanguard — research tools, screening, charting.
  • Spreadsheets: Custom modeling, what-if analysis, portfolio tracking.
  • Apps: Mint (budgeting), Personal Capital (net worth), Morningstar (fund research).

flashcards.definition: Automated investment platform that builds and manages a diversified portfolio based on your goals and risk tolerance.
Tool for filtering stocks based on criteria: market cap, P/E ratio, dividend yield, sector, etc.
Computer model running thousands of random scenarios to estimate the probability of investment outcomes.
Testing an investment strategy against historical data to see how it would have performed in the past.
Application Programming Interface for accessing real-time and historical financial data programmatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: Robo-advisors like Betterment and Wealthfront typically charge:
Robo-advisors are best for:

options.text: Beginners or those who want automated, diversified, low-cost portfolio management without active involvement

quiz_questions.question_text: Stock screeners help investors:

options.text: Filter thousands of stocks based on specific criteria (P/E ratio, dividend yield, market cap, sector)

quiz_questions.question_text: P/E ratio (Price-to-Earnings) measures:
