options.text: How much investors pay per dollar of earnings (lower P/E may indicate undervaluation, but context matters)

quiz_questions.question_text: Morningstar ratings (1-5 stars) are based on:

options.text: Past risk-adjusted performance relative to peers (helpful but past performance doesn't guarantee future results)

quiz_questions.question_text: Monte Carlo simulations help investors by:

options.text: Showing the probability distribution of outcomes under various scenarios (e.g., 90% chance of not running out of money)

quiz_questions.question_text: Backtesting a strategy shows:

options.text: How a strategy would have performed using historical data (useful but not predictive)

quiz_questions.question_text: The limitation of backtesting is:

options.text: Past performance doesn't guarantee future results — market conditions change

quiz_questions.question_text: Excel/Sheets for investment analysis can:

options.text: Build custom models, track portfolios, run scenarios, calculate returns, and create amortization tables

quiz_questions.question_text: The XIRR function in Excel calculates:

options.text: The internal rate of return for cash flows at irregular intervals (true return of investments with varying timing)

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial news aggregators (Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance) should be used:

options.text: As information sources, but with awareness that news creates emotional reactions that can harm investment decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: Commission-free trading (Robinhood, Schwab) has:

options.text: Made investing more accessible but can encourage excessive trading (overtrading hurts returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fractional shares allow:

options.text: Buying a portion of an expensive stock (e.g., $100 of a $3,000 stock) — making diversification accessible to small investors

quiz_questions.question_text: Personal finance apps like Mint and YNAB help with:

options.text: Budgeting, expense tracking, and net worth monitoring — the foundation of financial planning

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial calculators online (Bankrate, NerdWallet) are useful for:

options.text: Quick calculations: mortgage payments, retirement projections, debt payoff timelines — accessible to everyone

quiz_questions.question_text: Python/R for financial analysis offers:

options.text: Advanced capabilities: data analysis, custom models, Monte Carlo simulations, machine learning for pattern recognition

quiz_questions.question_text: The key risk of fintech apps that gamify investing is:

options.text: They can encourage impulsive, frequent trading which typically hurts long-term returns

quiz_questions.question_text: Automated rebalancing by robo-advisors:

options.text: Maintains your target allocation and implements tax-loss harvesting automatically — both improving outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, technology tools enable:

options.text: Complex scenario modeling, real-time analysis, and applying formulas to actual market data — bridging theory and practice

quiz_questions.question_text: The best technology tool for investing is:

options.text: The one you actually use consistently — simple, low-cost tools used diligently beat complex tools used sporadically

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Creating a Personal Budget
summary_sections.content_html:
Creating a Personal Budget

The 50/30/20 Rule

  • 50% — Needs: Housing, food, utilities, transportation, insurance.
  • 30% — Wants: Entertainment, dining out, hobbies, subscriptions.
  • 20% — Savings & debt: Retirement, emergency fund, extra debt payments.

Budget Equation

Income − Expenses = Surplus (or Deficit)

flashcards.definition: A plan for how income will be spent, saved, and invested over a specific time period.
Budgeting guideline: 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings/debt repayment.
Costs that stay the same each month (rent, loan payments, insurance premiums).
Costs that fluctuate monthly (groceries, utilities, entertainment).
Income minus expenses: positive = surplus (save/invest); negative = deficit (spending more than earning).

quiz_questions.question_text: The 50/30/20 rule allocates 50% of income to:

options.text: Needs (essential expenses: housing, food, utilities, transportation)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 20% in the 50/30/20 rule goes toward:

options.text: Savings and debt repayment (retirement, emergency fund, extra debt payments)

quiz_questions.question_text: With $4,000 monthly take-home pay under 50/30/20: needs budget is:
The budget equation is:
Fixed expenses include:
Variable expenses include:

options.text: Groceries, utilities, entertainment (amounts change month to month)

quiz_questions.question_text: If income is $5,000 and expenses are $5,500, the budget shows a:
The first step in budgeting is:

options.text: Tracking current income and expenses to understand where money actually goes

quiz_questions.question_text: Zero-based budgeting means:

options.text: Every dollar of income is assigned a job (spending, saving, or investing) — income minus all allocations = $0

quiz_questions.question_text: Pay yourself first means:

options.text: Automatically save/invest before spending on wants — treat savings as a non-negotiable 'expense'

quiz_questions.question_text: A budget review should happen:

options.text: Monthly (at minimum) to track against plan and adjust for changes

quiz_questions.question_text: The envelope system involves:

options.text: Putting cash in labeled envelopes for each budget category — when an envelope is empty, spending stops

quiz_questions.question_text: Discretionary spending is:

options.text: Spending on non-essential items you CHOOSE to buy (dining out, entertainment, shopping)

quiz_questions.question_text: If a person earns $60,000/year, the 50/30/20 rule suggests saving _____ per year.
The biggest budgeting mistake is:

options.text: Not having a budget at all (most people don't know where their money goes)

quiz_questions.question_text: A sinking fund is:

options.text: Money set aside regularly for a planned future expense (car repair, vacation, holiday gifts)

quiz_questions.question_text: Needs vs. wants: a smartphone is typically a:

options.text: A need (communication tool) but the latest model at $1,200 is a want (a cheaper phone fulfills the need)

quiz_questions.question_text: Budgets should be _____ to be sustainable.

options.text: Realistic and flexible — including some fun spending to avoid burnout and budget abandonment

quiz_questions.question_text: Tracking expenses reveals that many people:

options.text: Significantly underestimate spending on small recurring purchases (coffee, subscriptions, impulse buys)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, budgeting applies:

options.text: Arithmetic, percentages, and planning skills to real-world money management — the foundation of all personal finance

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tracking Income & Expenses
summary_sections.content_html:
Tracking Income & Expenses

Income Sources

  • Gross income: Total earnings before deductions.
  • Net income: Take-home pay after taxes and deductions.

Expense Categories

  • Essential: Housing (25-35%), food (10-15%), transportation (10-15%), insurance/health.
  • Discretionary: Entertainment, subscriptions, dining, shopping.
  • Financial: Savings, investments, debt payments.

flashcards.definition: Total earnings before any deductions (taxes, insurance, retirement contributions).
Take-home pay after all deductions — the actual amount available for budgeting.
The percentage of income spent on each category; helps identify areas for adjustment.
Regular, predictable expenses (subscriptions, memberships, loan payments).
Recording all spending to compare actual vs. budgeted amounts and identify patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Gross income is:

options.text: Total earnings BEFORE taxes and deductions

quiz_questions.question_text: Net income is:

options.text: Take-home pay AFTER taxes and all deductions

quiz_questions.question_text: If gross income is $70,000 and deductions total 30%, net income is approximately:
Housing should typically not exceed _____ of take-home pay.
On $4,500/month net income, maximum housing at 30% is:
The best way to track expenses is:

options.text: Systematically recording every expenditure (app, spreadsheet, or bank categorization)

quiz_questions.question_text: 'Latte factor' spending (small daily purchases) of $7/day totals _____ per year.
That $2,555/year invested at 8% for 30 years would grow to approximately:
Subscription audit reveals the average American spends _____ per month on subscriptions.

options.text: ~$200-300+ (streaming, apps, gym, services — often more than estimated)

quiz_questions.question_text: Income tracking should include:

options.text: ALL income: salary, freelance, side jobs, investment returns, tax refunds

quiz_questions.question_text: A common expense leak is:

options.text: Unused subscriptions and memberships (people often forget recurring charges)

quiz_questions.question_text: Categorizing expenses helps by:

options.text: Identifying where money actually goes vs. where you THINK it goes — often revealing surprises

quiz_questions.question_text: Cash flow analysis shows:

options.text: When money comes in and goes out during the month — timing matters for avoiding overdrafts

quiz_questions.question_text: An irregular expense like car insurance paid every 6 months should be:

options.text: Divided by 6 and included as a monthly budget item (set aside 1/6 each month in a sinking fund)

quiz_questions.question_text: Annual expenses that people often forget to budget for:

options.text: Holiday gifts, car registration, home maintenance, medical copays, clothing

quiz_questions.question_text: Income stability (W-2 employee) vs. income variability (freelancer) affects budgeting because:

options.text: Variable income requires budgeting based on minimum expected income and saving extra during good months

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial ratios: Savings rate = savings ÷ income. 20% savings rate on $50,000 =
Expense tracking apps (Mint, YNAB, Copilot) help by:

options.text: Automatically categorizing transactions, showing trends, and alerting when you exceed budget categories

quiz_questions.question_text: A spending journal for 30 days typically reveals that people:

options.text: Spend 10-30% more than they think — awareness alone changes behavior

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, expense tracking provides:

options.text: Real data for calculations: ratios, percentages, projections, and optimization of personal finances

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Emergency Funds & Savings Goals
summary_sections.content_html:
Emergency Funds & Savings Goals

Emergency Fund

  • Target: 3–6 months of essential expenses.
  • Where: High-yield savings (liquid, FDIC-insured).
  • Purpose: Job loss, medical emergency, major car repair, home emergency.

Savings Goals

Use the formula: Monthly savings = Goal amount ÷ Months until deadline (or use FV/PV for interest).

flashcards.definition: 3–6 months of essential expenses saved in a liquid, accessible account for unexpected major expenses.
A specific financial target with a timeline and required monthly contribution to achieve it.
How quickly an asset can be converted to cash without significant loss — emergency funds need high liquidity.
Money saved incrementally for a specific planned future expense (vacation, car down payment, wedding).
Cash reserve that prevents small emergencies from becoming financial disasters or debt spirals.

quiz_questions.question_text: The recommended emergency fund size is:
If monthly essential expenses are $3,000, a 6-month emergency fund should be:
The emergency fund should be kept in:

options.text: High-yield savings account (liquid + FDIC-insured + earns some interest)

quiz_questions.question_text: An emergency fund protects against:

options.text: Job loss, medical emergencies, major car/home repairs — unexpected events that could otherwise create debt

quiz_questions.question_text: Without an emergency fund, unexpected expenses often go on:

options.text: Credit cards (at 20%+ interest — creating expensive debt from a temporary problem)

quiz_questions.question_text: To save $12,000 in 12 months (no interest), monthly savings needed:
To save $20,000 for a car down payment in 3 years with 4% APY, monthly savings ≈:

options.text: ~$524 (PMT calculation with interest reduces the required amount)

quiz_questions.question_text: Building an emergency fund should start:

options.text: Immediately — even $500-$1,000 provides a starter buffer while paying off debt

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'financial runway' means:

options.text: How many months you can survive without income using your savings and emergency fund

quiz_questions.question_text: A common mistake is using the emergency fund for:

options.text: Non-emergencies (vacation, holiday shopping, sale items) — depleting the fund for wants, not needs

quiz_questions.question_text: $500/month into a high-yield savings at 4.5% for 2 years grows to approximately:
For irregular income earners, an emergency fund of _____ months is more appropriate.

options.text: 6-9 months (more cushion needed due to income uncertainty)

quiz_questions.question_text: Savings goals should be SMART:
Short-term savings goals (< 3 years) should be in:

options.text: Savings accounts or CDs (low risk — you need the money soon and can't afford a market drop)

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term savings goals (5+ years) can include:

options.text: Investment accounts (time horizon allows recovery from market dips, earning higher returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Automatic transfers to savings help because:

options.text: They remove the temptation to spend first — saving becomes a habit, not a decision each month

quiz_questions.question_text: The psychological benefit of separate savings accounts for each goal is:

options.text: Visual progress tracking for each goal motivates continued saving (wedding fund, vacation fund, etc.)

quiz_questions.question_text: If rent increases $200/month, the emergency fund should be:

options.text: Increased ($200 × 6 = $1,200 more needed for the same 6-month buffer)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'pay yourself first' approach to emergency fund building means:

options.text: Automatically transfer to emergency fund BEFORE paying discretionary expenses

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, savings goals use:

options.text: Future value, present value, and annuity formulas to calculate required monthly contributions for any target

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Short- & Long-Term Financial Planning
summary_sections.content_html:
Short- & Long-Term Financial Planning

Time Horizons

  • Short-term (0–3 years): Emergency fund, vacation, minor purchases.
  • Medium-term (3–10 years): Home down payment, car, graduate school.
  • Long-term (10+ years): Retirement, children's college, wealth building.

Planning Framework

1. Define goals. 2. Set timelines. 3. Calculate required savings. 4. Choose appropriate accounts. 5. Automate. 6. Review and adjust.

flashcards.definition: The process of setting goals, assessing resources, and creating strategies to achieve financial objectives.
The expected time period until a financial goal is needed; determines risk tolerance and investment choices.
Total assets minus total liabilities; the comprehensive measure of financial health.
Key target on the path to a larger goal (e.g., paying off credit cards before attacking student loans).
What you give up when choosing one option over another (e.g., spending now vs. investing for later).

quiz_questions.question_text: Short-term financial goals have a time horizon of:
Short-term goals should be funded with:

options.text: Low-risk vehicles like savings accounts and CDs (money is needed soon)

quiz_questions.question_text: Medium-term goals (3-10 years) can include:

options.text: Home down payment, car purchase, graduate school tuition

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term goals (10+ years) typically include:

options.text: Retirement savings and children's college funds

quiz_questions.question_text: Net worth =
If you have $50K in assets (savings, investments, property equity) and $30K liabilities (loans), net worth is:
The financial planning lifecycle goes from _____ to _____.
A 25-year-old's primary financial priorities should typically be:

options.text: Build emergency fund → pay off high-interest debt → start retirement saving → invest for growth

quiz_questions.question_text: The Rule of 72 for doubling money at 7%: ≈ _____ years.
Inflation of 3% means $100,000 in 20 years has the purchasing power of approximately:
Planning for education: a 529 plan offers:

options.text: Tax-free growth for qualified education expenses (state tax deductions in many states too)

quiz_questions.question_text: The cost of a 4-year public university (2024) averages approximately _____ total.
Starting to save for a newborn's college ($120K in 18 years at 7%) requires approximately:
Life insurance needs are calculated based on:

options.text: Income replacement, outstanding debts, future education costs, and final expenses for dependents

quiz_questions.question_text: A financial plan should be reviewed and updated:

options.text: At least annually and after major life events (marriage, baby, job change, etc.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Estate planning includes:

options.text: Will, power of attorney, healthcare directive, beneficiary designations, and potentially a trust

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'financial independence' concept means:

options.text: Having enough passive income or savings that working is optional — often calculated as 25× annual expenses

quiz_questions.question_text: If annual expenses are $40,000, financial independence requires approximately:
Lifestyle creep is:

options.text: Increasing spending as income rises, preventing savings rate from improving despite higher earnings

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, financial planning integrates:

options.text: ALL previous concepts: compound interest, loans, investments, budgeting, taxes, and risk management into a comprehensive strategy

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budget Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Budget Case Studies

Case 1: Recent College Graduate

Income: $45,000/year ($3,200/month net). Budget: Rent $1,050, food $400, transport $350, loans $350, insurance $200, savings $400, discretionary $450.

Case 2: Young Family

Income: $85,000/year ($5,500/month net). Budget: Mortgage $1,650, childcare $1,200, food $600, utilities $250, insurance $350, cars $400, savings $550, discretionary $500.

flashcards.definition: How income is distributed across expense categories — reveals financial priorities and potential imbalances.
Percentage of income saved: a 12-15% rate is healthy; 20%+ accelerates wealth building.
Housing expenses ÷ income; should stay below 30% for financial stability.
Intentionally choosing spending priorities based on values, not defaults or social pressure.
Building in 'flex' or buffer money for unexpected expenses within the budget framework.

quiz_questions.question_text: A recent graduate earning $3,200/month net with $1,050 rent spends _____ of income on housing.

options.text: ~33% (slightly above 30% guideline but common for new graduates in high-cost areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: The graduate's $400 savings represents a savings rate of:

options.text: 12.5% ($400/$3,200) — a good start, with room to increase

quiz_questions.question_text: The graduate can increase savings by:

options.text: Finding a roommate to reduce rent, cooking more, or increasing income through side work

quiz_questions.question_text: The young family spends $1,200/month on childcare, which is _____ of income.

options.text: ~22% ($1,200/$5,500) — childcare is often the second-largest expense after housing

quiz_questions.question_text: The family's combined housing + childcare is _____ of income.
The family saves $550/month (10%). To reach 15%, they need to save:
A single person earning $75,000 ($4,800/month net) following 50/30/20 should allocate:
If the graduate gets a $5,000 raise, the BEST use is:

options.text: Split: increase savings rate + small lifestyle improvement (avoid full lifestyle creep)

quiz_questions.question_text: A dual-income couple earning $120,000 total should build their budget on:

options.text: Primarily one income — using the second for accelerated savings and debt payoff provides enormous security

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'latte factor' in Case 1: the graduate's $5/day coffee habit costs _____ per year.

options.text: $1,825 (or 38% of their annual discretionary spending of $5,400)

quiz_questions.question_text: Case study analysis: which expense is easiest to reduce?

options.text: Discretionary spending (food out, entertainment, subscriptions — most flexibility)

quiz_questions.question_text: A budget that allocates 0% to savings is:

options.text: A crisis-level situation that will lead to debt when any unexpected expense occurs

quiz_questions.question_text: For both case studies, automated transfers for savings help because:

options.text: It removes the temptation to spend savings money — what you don't see, you don't spend

quiz_questions.question_text: The family could reduce childcare costs by:

options.text: Exploring subsidized programs, employer benefits, nanny shares, or FSA (Dependent Care FSA saves ~30% in taxes)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dependent Care FSA saves _____ on $5,000 of childcare expenses (25% tax bracket).
Budget case studies help students understand:

options.text: How real financial decisions involve trade-offs, priorities, and the math learned in class applied to actual scenarios

quiz_questions.question_text: If the graduate increases savings from $400 to $600/month at 8% for 30 years, the additional $200/month adds:

options.text: ~$298,000 (the extra $200 alone grows to ~$298K with compound interest!)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most critical budget line for long-term wealth is:

options.text: Savings/investments (consistently funding this category creates compound growth that builds wealth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Budget case studies demonstrate that financial math is:

options.text: Directly applicable to daily life decisions that compound into major financial outcomes over years and decades

quiz_questions.question_text: The common thread in all successful budgets is:

options.text: Spending intentionally: knowing where money goes and making conscious choices aligned with goals

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budgeting Technology Tools
summary_sections.content_html:
Budgeting Technology Tools

Popular Tools

  • YNAB (You Need A Budget): Zero-based; every dollar assigned a job. ~$99/year.
  • Mint/Credit Karma: Free; auto-categorization from linked accounts.
  • Spreadsheets: Full customization; templates widely available.
  • Bank apps: Built-in spending trackers; round-up savings features.

flashcards.definition: You Need A Budget — zero-based budgeting app that assigns every dollar a specific purpose.
Technology that automatically classifies transactions into spending categories (food, transport, etc.).
Feature that rounds purchases to the nearest dollar and saves the difference (e.g., $3.75 purchase → $0.25 saved).
Notifications triggered when approaching or exceeding budget category limits.
Technology-generated forecast of future income and expenses based on historical patterns.

quiz_questions.question_text: YNAB's budgeting approach is:

options.text: Zero-based (every dollar gets a job before it's spent — income minus all allocations = $0)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mint/Credit Karma helps by:

options.text: Automatically categorizing spending from linked bank/credit accounts and showing trends

quiz_questions.question_text: Round-up savings on $4.50 purchase with a $5 round-up:

options.text: Saves $0.50 (rounds to $5, difference goes to savings)

quiz_questions.question_text: If round-ups average $0.50 per transaction and you make 40 transactions/month:
Spending alerts are useful because:

options.text: They provide real-time awareness when approaching budget limits, preventing overspending

quiz_questions.question_text: The advantage of spreadsheets for budgeting is:

options.text: Full customization — you can build exactly the budget model you want with custom formulas and charts

quiz_questions.question_text: A disadvantage of spreadsheets is:

options.text: Require manual data entry (unless connected to bank APIs) and discipline to maintain

quiz_questions.question_text: Bank apps with built-in budgeting features are good for:

options.text: Basic spending tracking — convenient since data is already there, but often limited in customization

quiz_questions.question_text: The best budgeting tool is:

options.text: The one you actually use consistently (the tool matters less than the habit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial dashboard tools (Personal Capital) combine:

options.text: Bank accounts, investments, credit cards, and loans into one view showing net worth and cash flow

quiz_questions.question_text: AI-powered budgeting insights can:

options.text: Identify spending patterns, predict upcoming expenses, and suggest optimization opportunities

quiz_questions.question_text: Automated bill pay reduces:

options.text: Late payment fees and credit score damage from missed payments

quiz_questions.question_text: Subscription management apps (Truebill/Rocket Money) help by:

options.text: Identifying all recurring charges, highlighting unused services, and even canceling them for you

quiz_questions.question_text: Couples should use budgeting tools that:

options.text: Both partners can access and contribute to — transparency and alignment are key to financial harmony

quiz_questions.question_text: For students, free tools like Mint are ideal because:

options.text: They provide adequate tracking at no cost while building the budgeting habit early

quiz_questions.question_text: Gamification in budgeting apps (progress bars, streaks, badges):

options.text: Can make budgeting more engaging and help build consistent tracking habits

quiz_questions.question_text: API connections to bank accounts in budgeting apps are:

options.text: Generally secure (using bank-level encryption and read-only access through official APIs)

quiz_questions.question_text: Exporting budget data to CSV/Excel allows:

options.text: Custom analysis, historical trend research, and integration with other financial planning tools

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2024 trend in budgeting technology is:

options.text: AI-powered insights, automatic categorization improvement, and integration across all financial accounts

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, budgeting tools apply _____ to real money management.

options.text: Percentages, tracking, categorization, and visualization — making abstract financial math tangible and actionable

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Lifestyle Choices & Financial Impact
summary_sections.content_html:
Lifestyle Choices & Financial Impact

Major Financial Decisions

  • Housing: Rent vs. buy; city vs. suburb. Can differ by $500-1,500/month.
  • Transportation: New car vs. used vs. public transit. $400-900/month range.
  • Education: Community college → university (save $30,000+) vs. 4-year private.
  • Career: High-cost city + high salary vs. low-cost area + lower salary.

flashcards.definition: The amount needed to cover basic expenses in a particular area; varies dramatically by region.
Financial comparison of renting vs. purchasing a home, considering down payment, maintenance, equity, and market conditions.
Purchase price + insurance + fuel + maintenance + depreciation + registration — the TRUE cost of car ownership.
The financial return foregone by choosing one lifestyle option over another.
Living in a lower-cost area while earning income at higher-cost area rates (e.g., remote work).

quiz_questions.question_text: The difference between living in a high-cost vs. low-cost city can be _____ per year.
A new car costs approximately _____ more per year in depreciation than a 3-year-old used car.

options.text: $3,000-5,000 (new cars lose 20% in year 1; used cars depreciate much less)

quiz_questions.question_text: Total cost of car ownership includes:

options.text: Payment + insurance + fuel + maintenance + depreciation + registration + parking

quiz_questions.question_text: Average annual total cost of car ownership is approximately:
Switching from a $10K/year car to a $3K/year transit pass saves $7,000/year. Invested at 8% for 30 years:
Community college for 2 years then university vs. 4-year university saves approximately:

options.text: $30,000-50,000+ (community college averages $4,000/year vs. $12,000-25,000/year at state university)

quiz_questions.question_text: Renting vs. buying a home depends on:

options.text: Length of stay, local market conditions, opportunity cost of down payment, and lifestyle flexibility needs

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'rent vs. buy' break-even is typically:

options.text: 5-7 years (if staying shorter, renting is often cheaper when factoring in closing costs, maintenance, and opportunity cost)

quiz_questions.question_text: A salary of $100K in NYC vs. $70K in Dallas (with cost-of-living adjustment) results in:

options.text: Similar or better lifestyle in Dallas (lower housing, taxes, and general expenses can make $70K go further)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cooking at home vs. eating out saves approximately _____ per person per month.

options.text: $200-400 (average restaurant meal costs 3-5× the home-cooked equivalent)

quiz_questions.question_text: $300/month saved by cooking, invested at 7% for 30 years:
Subscription creep (adding streaming, gym, apps over years) can cost:
'Keeping up with the Joneses' (spending to match peers) leads to:

options.text: Overspending, undersaving, and financial stress — spending based on appearance rather than values

quiz_questions.question_text: The concept of 'enough' in lifestyle design means:

options.text: Identifying the level of spending that maximizes happiness without sacrificing financial security

quiz_questions.question_text: Research shows happiness increases with income up to about _____; after that, the effect plateaus.
The biggest lifestyle choice affecting long-term wealth is:

options.text: Housing choice (it's the largest expense — getting this right/wrong impacts everything else)

quiz_questions.question_text: Remote work enables 'geographic arbitrage' because you can:

options.text: Earn a high-cost-city salary while living in a lower-cost area, saving the difference

quiz_questions.question_text: A frugal lifestyle that saves an extra $1,000/month at 8% for 30 years creates:
Lifestyle inflation (increasing spending with income) is the #1 reason:

options.text: High earners can still be financially stressed — earning more doesn't help if spending more too

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, lifestyle analysis applies:

options.text: Compound interest, opportunity cost, NPV, and cost-benefit analysis to real decisions that span decades

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Budget Adjustments & Life Changes
summary_sections.content_html:
Budget Adjustments & Life Changes

Triggers for Budget Revision

  • Income changes: Raise, job loss, side income, retirement.
  • Life events: Marriage, baby, home purchase, health crisis.
  • Goal changes: New priorities, completed goals, shifting timelines.

Adjustment Strategy

1. Reassess income. 2. Re-prioritize goals. 3. Adjust allocations. 4. Implement and monitor.

flashcards.definition: Updating a budget to reflect changed circumstances — income, expenses, goals, or life events.
Recalculating budget allocations when income increases (raise/new job) or decreases (job loss/retirement).
Adjusting finances for major changes: marriage (combined income), baby ($15K+/year), divorce, etc.
The ability to adapt financially to unexpected changes without crisis or excessive debt.
Strategy for handling large one-time income (inheritance, bonus, tax refund, lottery): save, invest, or pay debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10% raise on $50,000 gives $5,000 more. The smartest allocation is:

options.text: Split: increase savings rate + modest lifestyle improvement (e.g., 50% to savings, 50% to enjoyment)

quiz_questions.question_text: Job loss requires immediately:

options.text: Cutting discretionary spending, filing for unemployment, assessing emergency fund runway, and seeking new income

quiz_questions.question_text: A baby adds approximately _____ per year in expenses.
Marriage affects budgets because:

options.text: Combined income, shared expenses (potential savings), and aligned financial goals (or conflicts) change the entire picture

quiz_questions.question_text: Buying a home changes the budget by adding:

options.text: Mortgage + property taxes + insurance + maintenance (1-3% of home value/year) + utilities increase

quiz_questions.question_text: A $3,000 bonus or tax refund should be:

options.text: Applied strategically: emergency fund (if not full) → high-interest debt → savings/investments

quiz_questions.question_text: Retirement budget adjustments include:

options.text: Potentially lower income, no commute costs, higher healthcare costs, and shifting from saving to spending mode

quiz_questions.question_text: Health crisis financial impact can include:

options.text: Medical bills + reduced income + increased insurance + potential disability — emergency fund and insurance are critical

quiz_questions.question_text: When transitioning from two incomes to one (e.g., parental leave):

options.text: Budget BEFORE the change: test living on one income for 3-6 months while saving the second income

quiz_questions.question_text: Divorce typically reduces household wealth by _____ due to splitting assets and maintaining two households.
Moving to a higher-cost city requires:

options.text: Recalculating ALL budget categories: housing (+30-100%), transportation, food, and adjusting savings goals

quiz_questions.question_text: Annual inflation of 3% means budgets must increase by _____ per year just to maintain the same lifestyle.

options.text: 3% (expenses naturally rise — budget must account for this)

quiz_questions.question_text: A car accident requiring $5,000 in repairs (with $1,000 deductible) affects your budget for _____ months if absorbing from monthly budget.

options.text: 2-4 months (depending on how much 'flex' money exists monthly — this is why emergency funds matter)

quiz_questions.question_text: After paying off a $300/month student loan, the freed-up money should:

options.text: Be redirected to the next financial priority (invest, next debt, or increase savings — don't let it leak into spending)

quiz_questions.question_text: Periodic budget reviews should check:

options.text: Income, expenses, savings rate, debt progress, goal alignment, and any upcoming life changes

quiz_questions.question_text: A promotion bringing $10,000 more annually creates the most wealth if _____ is saved/invested.

options.text: At least 50% — ideally even more, since you were already living on less before the raise

quiz_questions.question_text: Having a child ages 0-17 costs approximately _____ total (2024, middle-income family).
A financial buffer in the budget (5-10% unassigned) helps because:

options.text: It absorbs small unexpected expenses without derailing the entire budget — reducing stress and maintaining consistency

quiz_questions.question_text: The most successful long-term budgeters:

options.text: Regularly review and adjust their budget as life changes — flexibility keeps the plan relevant and motivating

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, budget adjustments demonstrate that personal finance is:

options.text: A dynamic, ongoing process requiring regular application of financial math concepts to changing real-world circumstances

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Understanding Income Tax
summary_sections.content_html:
Income Tax Fundamentals

How Income Tax Works

  • Gross income: All taxable income (wages, interest, dividends, etc.).
  • Adjusted Gross Income (AGI): Gross income minus above-the-line deductions.
  • Taxable income: AGI minus standard/itemized deductions and exemptions.
  • Tax owed: Calculated by applying tax brackets to taxable income.

The US uses a progressive tax system — higher income is taxed at higher rates.

flashcards.definition: System where higher income portions are taxed at higher rates; everyone pays the same rate on the same levels.
All taxable income before deductions: wages, salaries, interest, dividends, business income.
Gross income minus specific above-the-line deductions (student loan interest, IRA contributions, etc.).
A fixed amount subtracted from AGI before calculating tax ($14,600 single / $29,200 married filing jointly, 2024).
The amount actually subject to tax: AGI minus deductions (standard or itemized).

quiz_questions.question_text: The US federal income tax system is:

options.text: Progressive (higher portions of income taxed at increasingly higher rates)

quiz_questions.question_text: In a progressive system, moving to a higher tax bracket means:

options.text: ALL your income is taxed at the higher rate
Only the income ABOVE the bracket threshold is taxed at the higher rate

quiz_questions.question_text: With $80,000 taxable income (2024 single), the tax is calculated by:

options.text: Applying 10% to first $11,600, 12% to next $35,550, and 22% to the remainder — layered brackets

quiz_questions.question_text: The standard deduction for a single filer in 2024 is approximately:
Taxable income = AGI minus:

options.text: Standard deduction (or itemized deductions, whichever is larger)

quiz_questions.question_text: A person earning $50,000 with $14,600 standard deduction has taxable income of:
Marginal tax rate vs. effective tax rate: marginal is _____, effective is _____.

options.text: The rate on the LAST dollar earned, the AVERAGE rate on ALL income

quiz_questions.question_text: Someone in the '22% bracket' probably pays an effective rate of:

options.text: Less than 22% (because lower brackets tax the first portions of income at 10% and 12%)

quiz_questions.question_text: W-2 employees have taxes withheld from:

options.text: Each paycheck by their employer (estimated tax payments throughout the year)

quiz_questions.question_text: Self-employed workers must pay:

options.text: Income tax PLUS self-employment tax (15.3% for Social Security/Medicare — both employee and employer portions)

quiz_questions.question_text: FICA taxes include:

options.text: Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) = 7.65% from employee, matched by employer

quiz_questions.question_text: A W-4 form determines:

options.text: How much federal tax your employer withholds from each paycheck

quiz_questions.question_text: Filing status options include:

options.text: Single, Married Filing Jointly, Married Filing Separately, Head of Household, Qualifying Widow(er)

quiz_questions.question_text: Married Filing Jointly typically results in _____ tax than filing separately.

options.text: Lower (wider brackets and higher standard deduction benefit most couples)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-exempt income includes:

options.text: Municipal bond interest, Roth IRA qualified withdrawals, gifts (under annual exclusion)

quiz_questions.question_text: The tax filing deadline is typically:

options.text: April 15 (can be extended with Form 4868, but taxes owed are still due April 15)

quiz_questions.question_text: Penalties for underpaying estimated taxes apply to:

options.text: Self-employed and others who owe more than $1,000 at filing and didn't pay enough quarterly

quiz_questions.question_text: State income taxes:

options.text: Vary dramatically — from 0% (TX, FL, WA, etc.) to over 13% (California, at highest bracket)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding tax brackets helps you:

options.text: Plan income timing, maximize deductions, and make informed decisions about retirement contributions and investments

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, income tax calculations apply:

options.text: Percentage calculations, progressive bracket math, and deduction analysis to one of life's largest expenses

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Brackets & Marginal Rates

flashcards.definition: A range of income taxed at a specific rate in the progressive system.
The tax rate applied to the LAST dollar of income — the rate of the highest bracket you reach.
Total tax paid ÷ total income — the average rate actually paid across all brackets.
The total amount of federal (and state) income tax you owe for the year.
When inflation pushes income into higher brackets; brackets are adjusted annually for inflation to offset this.

quiz_questions.question_text: On $50,000 taxable income (single, 2024), the first $11,600 is taxed at:
Tax on the first $11,600 =
Income from $11,601 to $47,150 is taxed at _____ in the second bracket.
Tax on income from $11,601 to $47,150 ($35,550) =
On $50,000 taxable, the amount in the 22% bracket is:
Tax on the 22% bracket portion ($2,850) =
Total federal tax on $50,000 taxable income ≈
Effective tax rate on $50,000 taxable income ≈
The marginal rate for someone with $50,000 taxable income is:

options.text: 22% (the bracket of the last dollar earned)

quiz_questions.question_text: A common misconception: 'If I earn $1 more and enter a higher bracket, I lose money.' This is:

options.text: False — only the income ABOVE the bracket threshold is taxed at the higher rate; you always net more

quiz_questions.question_text: On $100,000 taxable income (single), total tax ≈
Effective rate on $100,000 taxable ≈
On $200,000 taxable income, the marginal rate is:
Capital gains (long-term, held >1 year) are taxed at _____ rates than ordinary income.

options.text: Lower (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income — preferential treatment for long-term investments)

quiz_questions.question_text: Qualified dividends are taxed at:

options.text: Long-term capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20% — lower than ordinary income rates)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax planning strategies include:

options.text: Maximizing deductions, contributing to retirement accounts, timing income, and harvesting losses

quiz_questions.question_text: Contributing $5,000 to a traditional IRA in the 22% bracket saves _____ in taxes.
The alternative minimum tax (AMT) ensures:

options.text: High-income taxpayers with many deductions pay at least a minimum amount of tax

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax bracket calculations in financial math require:

options.text: Multi-step progressive calculation: applying different rates to different portions of income

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding brackets helps with:

options.text: Deciding between Traditional vs. Roth contributions, timing bonuses, and making investment decisions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Property, Sales, & Other Taxes
summary_sections.content_html:
Property, Sales, & Other Taxes

Property Tax

Based on assessed value of property. Rate varies by location (0.3% to 2.5%+).

Sales Tax

Percentage added to purchases. Varies by state (0% to 7.25%+) plus local additions.

Other Taxes

  • Estate/Inheritance tax: On transfers at death (federal exemption: $13.6M in 2024).
  • Excise tax: On specific goods (gas, alcohol, tobacco).
  • Payroll tax: FICA (Social Security + Medicare).

flashcards.definition: Annual tax on real estate based on assessed value; funds local services (schools, fire, police, roads).
The value assigned to a property by the tax assessor for property tax calculation (may differ from market value).
Tax added to retail purchases at point of sale; varies by state and locality.
Federal tax on the transfer of assets at death; applies only above the exemption ($13.6M in 2024).
Tax on specific goods: gasoline (~$0.18/gallon federal + state), alcohol, tobacco, luxury items.

quiz_questions.question_text: Property tax is calculated as:
A home assessed at $250,000 with a 1.8% property tax rate owes:
Property taxes primarily fund:

options.text: Local services: schools, fire departments, police, roads, and infrastructure

quiz_questions.question_text: States with NO state income tax include:

options.text: Texas, Florida, Washington, Nevada, Wyoming, among others

quiz_questions.question_text: States with no income tax often have ____ as compensation.

options.text: Higher sales or property taxes (states need revenue from somewhere)

quiz_questions.question_text: A $100 purchase with 8.5% sales tax costs:
Annual sales tax paid by an average household spending $50,000 with 7% sales tax rate (on taxable goods ≈ 60%) is:
The federal estate tax exemption in 2024 is approximately:
Excise tax on gasoline is approximately _____ per gallon federally.
FICA payroll tax total (employee share) is:
Self-employed people pay _____ in FICA because they pay both employee and employer portions.
The Social Security tax wage base in 2024 is approximately:

options.text: $168,600 (income above this is NOT subject to Social Security tax; Medicare has no cap)

quiz_questions.question_text: Capital gains tax rates (long-term) are:

options.text: 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income (lower than ordinary income rates)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-loss harvesting saves money by:

options.text: Using investment losses to offset gains, reducing the capital gains tax owed

quiz_questions.question_text: Property tax protests/appeals can reduce taxes if:

options.text: The assessed value is higher than fair market value (homeowners can challenge assessments)

quiz_questions.question_text: Homestead exemption reduces property tax by:

options.text: Reducing the taxable assessed value for owner-occupied primary residences (varies by state)

quiz_questions.question_text: Gift tax exemption (2024) allows giving _____ per person per year tax-free.
The total tax burden (income + payroll + sales + property) for a middle-income family is often:

options.text: 30-40%+ of gross income (when ALL types of taxes are combined)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax incidence refers to:

options.text: Who actually bears the economic burden of a tax (e.g., who 'pays' for higher gas taxes — producers or consumers?)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, understanding various taxes helps calculate:

options.text: True cost of purchases, real estate ownership costs, investment returns, and overall financial planning accuracy

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Types of Insurance
summary_sections.content_html:
Types of Insurance

Core Insurance Types

  • Health insurance: Covers medical expenses (deductible, copay, coinsurance, out-of-pocket max).
  • Auto insurance: Liability, collision, comprehensive.
  • Homeowner's/Renter's: Property damage, liability, personal belongings.
  • Life insurance: Term (pure protection) vs. Whole (protection + cash value).
  • Disability insurance: Replaces income if you can't work.

flashcards.definition: The regular payment (monthly/annually) you make to maintain insurance coverage.
The amount you pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins covering costs.
A fixed amount you pay for a covered service (e.g., $30 doctor visit, $10 prescription).
Pure death benefit for a specific term (10-30 years); no cash value; most affordable protection.
Replaces 60-70% of income if you're unable to work due to illness or injury — often the most overlooked coverage.

quiz_questions.question_text: An insurance premium is:

options.text: The regular payment you make to keep your insurance policy active

quiz_questions.question_text: A deductible is:

options.text: The amount you pay out-of-pocket before insurance starts covering costs

quiz_questions.question_text: Higher deductible plans typically have:

options.text: Lower premiums (you accept more risk in exchange for lower regular payments)

quiz_questions.question_text: Health insurance coinsurance of 80/20 means:

options.text: Insurance pays 80%, you pay 20% of covered costs (after deductible)

quiz_questions.question_text: The out-of-pocket maximum is:

options.text: The most you'll pay in a year for covered services — after reaching this, insurance pays 100%

quiz_questions.question_text: Auto liability insurance covers:

options.text: Damage you cause to OTHER people and their property in an accident

quiz_questions.question_text: Collision coverage pays for:

options.text: Your car's repair after an accident (regardless of fault)

quiz_questions.question_text: Comprehensive auto coverage pays for:

options.text: Non-collision damage: theft, weather, vandalism, animal collisions, falling objects

quiz_questions.question_text: Term life insurance provides:

options.text: Pure death benefit for a specific time period (10-30 years) — most affordable type

quiz_questions.question_text: Whole life insurance differs from term because it:

options.text: Has a cash value component that grows over time PLUS a death benefit; costs 5-15× more than term

quiz_questions.question_text: Most financial advisors recommend _____ life insurance for typical families.

options.text: Term (much cheaper; invest the premium difference for better returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: The recommended amount of life insurance is typically:

options.text: 10–12× annual income (enough to replace income for the family if the earner dies)

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance covers:

options.text: Your personal belongings, liability, and additional living expenses if your rental is uninhabitable

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance costs approximately:
Disability insurance is critical because:

options.text: You're more likely to become disabled than die during working years; it protects your greatest asset: your income

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term disability insurance typically replaces _____ of income.
An umbrella insurance policy provides:

options.text: Extra liability coverage beyond what auto and homeowner's policies cover (e.g., $1-5 million additional)

quiz_questions.question_text: Insurance is based on the concept of:

options.text: Risk pooling (many people pay premiums; the few who have claims are covered — spreading risk across the group)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important purpose of insurance is:

options.text: Protecting against catastrophic financial loss that you couldn't afford to pay on your own

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, insurance involves:

options.text: Premium comparison, deductible/premium tradeoff analysis, coverage needs calculations, and expected value

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk Management Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk Management

Risk Management Strategies

  • Avoid: Don't do the risky activity (don't own a pool).
  • Reduce: Take precautions (smoke detectors, safe driving).
  • Transfer: Insurance shifts financial risk to the insurer.
  • Retain: Accept the risk and self-insure (high deductible, emergency fund).

Expected Value

E(X) = Probability × Impact. Used to evaluate whether insurance is worthwhile.

flashcards.definition: Eliminating a risk entirely by not engaging in the activity (don't own a boat = no boat liability).
Taking steps to decrease the probability or impact of a risk (security system, healthy habits).
Shifting financial risk to another party, typically through insurance (pay premium → insurer absorbs loss).
Accepting a risk and self-insuring — viable for small, manageable risks (choosing a high deductible).
Probability of an event × its financial impact; used to decide whether to insure against a risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: The four risk management strategies are:
Insurance is an example of risk:

options.text: Transfer (you pay a premium; the insurer assumes the financial risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: Self-insuring (high deductible + emergency fund) is an example of risk:

options.text: Retention (you accept the risk and would pay smaller losses yourself)

quiz_questions.question_text: Expected value of a risk: 2% chance of $50,000 loss per year =
If insurance against a $50,000 loss (2% probability) costs $1,200/year:

options.text: The premium ($1,200) exceeds expected value ($1,000) — but it provides certainty and prevents catastrophe

quiz_questions.question_text: People buy insurance even when premium > expected value because:

options.text: Catastrophic losses would be devastating — the peace of mind and protection from financial ruin is worth the 'overpayment'

quiz_questions.question_text: You should insure against risks that are:

options.text: Low probability but HIGH impact (where a loss would devastate your finances)

quiz_questions.question_text: You should self-insure (retain) risks that are:

options.text: Low impact (losses you can absorb from savings/emergency fund without hardship)

quiz_questions.question_text: A smoke detector is an example of risk:

options.text: Reduction (decreases the probability/severity of fire damage)

quiz_questions.question_text: Wearing a seatbelt is risk _____ for car accident injuries.
Not owning a motorcycle is risk _____ for motorcycle accidents.
An emergency fund serves as self-insurance for:

options.text: Small to medium unexpected expenses that would otherwise go on credit cards (car repair, appliance replacement)

quiz_questions.question_text: The purpose of deductibles in insurance is:

options.text: To share risk — the insured retains small losses (deductible) while the insurer covers large losses

quiz_questions.question_text: Higher deductibles make sense for people with:

options.text: Healthy emergency funds who can afford to pay smaller losses out-of-pocket for lower premiums

quiz_questions.question_text: Diversification in investing is a form of risk:

options.text: Reduction (spreading across many investments reduces the impact of any single loss)

quiz_questions.question_text: A business liability umbrella policy is risk _____ for lawsuit damages.

options.text: Transfer (the insurance company bears the financial risk of a large lawsuit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Risk management order of preference (general):

options.text: First avoid unnecessary risks, then reduce remaining risks, transfer catastrophic ones, retain manageable ones

quiz_questions.question_text: Cost-benefit analysis of insurance: $2,400/year for coverage vs. $500,000 potential liability:

options.text: Worth it — the potential loss far exceeds the premium; this is catastrophic risk you can't self-insure

quiz_questions.question_text: The law of large numbers in insurance means:

options.text: With many policyholders, actual losses approach expected losses (predictable for the insurer, making the business model work)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, risk management uses:

options.text: Expected value, probability, cost-benefit analysis, and present value to make rational decisions about protecting assets

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Planning Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Tax Planning Case Studies

Case 1: Traditional vs. Roth IRA

Alex (22% bracket): $6,500 Traditional IRA deduction saves $1,430 in taxes NOW. In retirement (12% bracket), withdrawals are taxed less — Traditional wins. But if Alex expects HIGHER bracket in retirement, Roth wins.

Case 2: Tax-Loss Harvesting

Beth has $10,000 gains and $4,000 losses. Net taxable gain = $6,000. At 15% capital gains rate, she saves $600 in taxes by harvesting the loss.

flashcards.definition: If current tax rate > retirement rate: Traditional IRA wins. If current rate < retirement rate: Roth wins.
Selling investments at a loss to offset gains, reducing taxes. Net loss up to $3,000 can offset ordinary income.
Investments in Traditional IRA/401(k) grow without annual taxes; taxes owed on withdrawal (vs. taxable accounts).
Using realized losses to reduce or eliminate taxes on realized gains in the same year.
The tax savings from a deduction = deduction amount × marginal tax rate (e.g., $5,000 deduction at 22% saves $1,100).

quiz_questions.question_text: A $6,500 Traditional IRA contribution in the 22% bracket saves _____ in current-year taxes.
If Alex expects to be in the 12% bracket in retirement, Traditional IRA saves _____ per $6,500 vs. Roth.
A Roth IRA is better when you expect _____ tax rates in retirement.

options.text: Higher (pay lower taxes now; avoid higher taxes on withdrawals later)

quiz_questions.question_text: Beth's $10,000 gain offset by $4,000 loss results in net taxable gain of:
Beth saves _____ in taxes from harvesting the $4,000 loss at 15% capital gains rate.
Net capital losses above gains can offset ordinary income up to _____ per year.

options.text: $3,000 ($3,000 limit; remainder carries forward to future years)

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 in a traditional 401(k) at 22% bracket saves _____ in current taxes.
Tax-deferred growth means the investment:

options.text: Grows without annual taxes on dividends/gains — all money stays invested and compounds (taxes due at withdrawal)

quiz_questions.question_text: $100,000 growing at 7% for 20 years: taxable (losing 1% to annual taxes) vs. tax-deferred:

options.text: Tax-deferred: ~$387K vs. Taxable: ~$320K — tax deferral adds ~$67K through more efficient compounding

quiz_questions.question_text: Bunching deductions means:

options.text: Combining multiple years of deductions into one year to exceed the standard deduction threshold, then taking standard the other year

quiz_questions.question_text: Charitable giving in a high-income year saves more because:

options.text: Higher marginal rate means each dollar donated saves more in taxes (32% bracket: $1,000 donation saves $320 vs. 12% saves $120)

quiz_questions.question_text: Health Savings Account (HSA) is 'triple tax-advantaged' because:

options.text: Contributions are pre-tax, growth is tax-free, AND qualified withdrawals are tax-free

quiz_questions.question_text: If both spouses work and one earns much more, _____ filing status usually provides the least tax.

options.text: Married Filing Jointly (wider brackets and higher standard deduction reduce combined tax)

quiz_questions.question_text: Timing a year-end bonus: deferring $10,000 to January moves income to a potentially:

options.text: Lower bracket year (if income varies), saving taxes on that $10,000

quiz_questions.question_text: Municipal bond interest is _____ from federal income tax.

options.text: Exempt (and often state tax-exempt if the bond is from your state — valuable for high-bracket investors)

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth conversion ($50,000 from Traditional to Roth in the 22% bracket) costs:

options.text: $11,000 in current taxes (50000 × 0.22) — but future growth and withdrawals are then tax-free forever

quiz_questions.question_text: Roth conversions are best done in:

options.text: Low-income years (when your marginal rate is low, the conversion tax is minimized)

quiz_questions.question_text: For a business owner, choosing between S-corp and sole proprietorship affects:

options.text: Self-employment tax liability — S-corp can save thousands in FICA on reasonable salary/distribution split

quiz_questions.question_text: The wash-sale rule prevents you from:

options.text: Claiming a loss if you buy the same or 'substantially identical' stock within 30 days (before or after the sale)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax planning case studies show that tax optimization:

options.text: Is legal and prudent use of tax code provisions — potentially saving thousands per year through informed decisions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Insurance in Real Life
summary_sections.content_html:
Insurance in Real Life

Case 1: Health Insurance Plan Comparison

Plan A: Premium $300/month, $1,000 deductible, 80/20 coinsurance, $5,000 max OOP.
Plan B: Premium $200/month, $3,000 deductible, 70/30 coinsurance, $8,000 max OOP.
For healthy year: Plan B saves $1,200. For major illness: Plan A saves up to $3,000.

Case 2: Life Insurance Needs

Couple with $80K income, $200K mortgage, 2 young kids. Need: ~$800K-1M coverage (10-12× income).

flashcards.definition: Analyzing total costs across scenarios (healthy year vs. major illness) for different insurance options.
High-deductible health plan (HDHP) that qualifies for a Health Savings Account — triple tax advantage.
Calculating coverage amount based on income replacement, debts, education costs, and final expenses.
Steps to file and receive insurance benefits: document, report, file claim, adjuster review, settlement/payment.
Period without insurance coverage, or a situation not covered by your policy — identify and address gaps.

quiz_questions.question_text: Plan A ($300/month premium, $1,000 deductible) costs _____ in premiums per year.
Plan B ($200/month premium, $3,000 deductible) costs _____ in premiums per year.
In a healthy year (no claims), Plan B saves _____ vs. Plan A.
In a major illness year, Plan A's better coverage means max OOP of $5,000 + $3,600 premiums = $8,600 total. Plan B:

options.text: $8,000 max OOP + $2,400 premiums = $10,400 total — Plan A saves $1,800 in a bad year

quiz_questions.question_text: An HSA-eligible HDHP has a minimum deductible of _____ (individual, 2024).
The HSA contribution limit (individual, 2024) is approximately:
HSA funds roll over _____ (unlike FSA).

options.text: Indefinitely — there is no 'use it or lose it' rule for HSA funds

quiz_questions.question_text: Life insurance needs for $80K/income family with mortgage: 10× income =
A 30-year-old can get a 20-year $500,000 term life policy for approximately:
Whole life insurance for the same coverage would cost approximately:
For most families, the best life insurance strategy is:

options.text: Buy affordable term insurance and invest the premium difference in index funds

quiz_questions.question_text: Auto insurance: $100,000/$300,000/$100,000 coverage means:

options.text: $100K per person bodily injury / $300K per accident total / $100K property damage

quiz_questions.question_text: Uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage protects you if:

options.text: The other driver has no insurance or not enough to cover your damages

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance at $20/month provides approximately _____ in personal property coverage.
Flood insurance is _____ included in standard homeowner's policies.

options.text: NOT (separate policy needed, often through NFIP — critical in flood-prone areas)

quiz_questions.question_text: Long-term care insurance covers:

options.text: Extended care needs: nursing homes, assisted living, in-home care (average cost: $60,000-100,000+/year)

quiz_questions.question_text: The $#1 reason insurance claims are denied is:

options.text: Policy exclusions (the event isn't covered) or insufficient documentation — always READ your policy

quiz_questions.question_text: Annual insurance review is important because:

options.text: Life changes (marriage, baby, home, health, assets) require coverage updates to avoid gaps or overpaying

quiz_questions.question_text: Bundling insurance (auto + home with same company) typically saves:
For financial math, insurance decisions use:

options.text: Expected value, cost-benefit, scenario analysis, and present value to make optimal protection decisions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Tax Software & Filing Tools
summary_sections.content_html:
Tax Software & Filing Tools

Tax Preparation Options

  • Free File: IRS Free File for income ≤ $79,000 (2024).
  • Tax software: TurboTax, H&R Block, TaxAct — guided interviews.
  • CPA/Tax professional: For complex situations (business, rental, investments).
  • DIY: Form 1040 — good for understanding the math behind software.

flashcards.definition: Free tax preparation software available for income ≤ $79,000; accesses IRS-approved software at no cost.
Employer-provided form showing annual wages and tax withholdings; needed for tax filing.
Various forms reporting non-wage income: 1099-INT (interest), 1099-DIV (dividends), 1099-NEC (freelance).
The main US individual income tax return form; all filers use this form.
The difference when you've overpaid taxes through withholding — it's YOUR money being returned, not a gift from the government.

quiz_questions.question_text: IRS Free File is available for income up to:

options.text: $79,000 (2024) — multiple software options available for free

quiz_questions.question_text: A W-2 form is provided by:

options.text: Your employer (showing wages earned and taxes withheld during the year)

quiz_questions.question_text: 1099-INT reports:

options.text: Interest income from banks and investments

quiz_questions.question_text: 1099-NEC (replacing 1099-MISC for payments) reports:
A tax refund means:

options.text: You overpaid taxes during the year and are getting YOUR money back (not a bonus)

quiz_questions.question_text: A large tax refund ($3,000+) means:

options.text: You've been giving the government a free loan — adjust withholding to keep more in each paycheck

quiz_questions.question_text: TurboTax and H&R Block software guide filing through:

options.text: Step-by-step interview questions that translate your answers into the correct forms automatically

quiz_questions.question_text: When should you hire a CPA/tax professional?

options.text: When you have complex situations: business income, rental property, stock options, multi-state filing, major life changes

quiz_questions.question_text: Average cost of professional tax preparation is:
E-filing has _____ advantages over paper filing.

options.text: Multiple: faster refund (21 days vs. 6+ weeks), fewer errors, automatic calculations, confirmation of receipt

quiz_questions.question_text: The standard deduction vs. itemizing: take itemized if deductions exceed:

options.text: The standard deduction ($14,600 single / $29,200 MFJ in 2024)

quiz_questions.question_text: Common itemized deductions include:

options.text: Mortgage interest, state/local taxes (SALT up to $10,000), charitable donations, and medical expenses (above 7.5% of AGI)

quiz_questions.question_text: The SALT deduction cap is _____ per tax return.
Tax extensions (Form 4868) give extra time to:

options.text: File your return (6 more months) — but estimated taxes are still due by April 15

quiz_questions.question_text: Direct deposit of refund is:

options.text: The fastest way to receive your refund (~21 days for e-filed returns with direct deposit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Amended returns (Form 1040-X) are filed when:

options.text: You discover errors or omitted information on a previously filed return

quiz_questions.question_text: The IRS Free File Fillable Forms (for any income) provide:

options.text: Electronic blank tax forms with basic calculations — no guided interview (for those who know how to fill out forms)

quiz_questions.question_text: Identity theft protection for tax filing includes:

options.text: Filing early, using secure software, protecting your SSN, and using an IP PIN (Identity Protection PIN) from the IRS

quiz_questions.question_text: For students filing taxes for the first time, the best starting point is:

options.text: IRS Free File or free software (simple W-2 income + standard deduction = straightforward first filing)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, tax filing applies:

options.text: Income calculations, deduction analysis, bracket math, and technology tools directly to a real annual obligation everyone faces

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Starting a Business
summary_sections.content_html:
Starting a Business

Business Formation Basics

  • Sole Proprietorship: Simplest; owner = business. Unlimited personal liability.
  • Partnership: Two+ owners share profits, losses, and liability.
  • LLC: Limited liability protection; pass-through taxation. Most popular for small businesses.
  • Corporation: Separate legal entity. C-Corp: double taxation. S-Corp: pass-through.

Start-up Costs

One-time costs (equipment, licenses, legal fees) vs. recurring costs (rent, utilities, payroll).

flashcards.definition: Simplest business structure; owner has complete control but unlimited personal liability for all debts.
Business structure providing personal liability protection with pass-through taxation — combines benefits of corporation and sole proprietorship.
One-time expenses to launch a business: equipment, inventory, legal fees, licenses, marketing materials.
A formal document detailing business goals, strategies, market analysis, financial projections, and funding needs.
Fixed: don't change with output (rent, insurance). Variable: change with production volume (materials, labor per unit).

quiz_questions.question_text: A sole proprietorship's biggest disadvantage is:

options.text: Unlimited personal liability (personal assets at risk for business debts)

quiz_questions.question_text: An LLC provides:

options.text: Limited liability protection with pass-through taxation (profits taxed on personal return)

quiz_questions.question_text: A C-Corporation faces 'double taxation' because:

options.text: It pays taxes twice a year
Corporate profits are taxed at the corporate level, then dividends to shareholders are taxed again on personal returns

quiz_questions.question_text: An S-Corporation avoids double taxation by:

options.text: Passing income through to shareholders' personal returns (like a partnership)

quiz_questions.question_text: Typical start-up costs for a small business range from:
A business plan is important because it:

options.text: Forces you to research the market, plan finances, and serves as the document lenders/investors need

quiz_questions.question_text: Fixed costs include:

options.text: Rent, insurance, salaries, loan payments (don't change regardless of production volume)

quiz_questions.question_text: Variable costs include:

options.text: Raw materials, packaging, shipping per unit, hourly labor directly tied to production

quiz_questions.question_text: The break-even point is when:

options.text: Total revenue = total costs (no profit, no loss — the minimum viable sales volume)

quiz_questions.question_text: An entrepreneur investing $50,000 of personal savings uses _____ financing.

options.text: Equity (personal investment — no repayment required but owner bears the risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: A bank loan for a business is _____ financing.

options.text: Debt (must be repaid with interest regardless of business success)

quiz_questions.question_text: The SBA (Small Business Administration) helps by:

options.text: Guaranteeing loans (reducing bank risk), providing resources, training, and counseling for small businesses

quiz_questions.question_text: A business license is typically required from:

options.text: Local and/or state government (requirements vary by location and business type)

quiz_questions.question_text: Working capital is:

options.text: The cash available for day-to-day operations (current assets − current liabilities)

quiz_questions.question_text: Most small businesses fail in the first 5 years at a rate of approximately:

options.text: ~50% (emphasizing the importance of financial planning and adequate capitalization)

quiz_questions.question_text: An EIN (Employer Identification Number) is:

options.text: A federal tax ID number for your business (required for hiring employees, opening business accounts)

quiz_questions.question_text: Franchise businesses have the advantage of:

options.text: Proven business model, brand recognition, training, and systems — but less creative freedom and ongoing franchise fees

quiz_questions.question_text: Franchise fees typically range from:
The opportunity cost of starting a business includes:

options.text: The salary you forgo from employment, the return you could earn investing the capital elsewhere, and your time

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, starting a business applies:

options.text: Break-even analysis, cost classification, capital budgeting, ROI projections, and risk assessment

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Profit & Loss Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Profit & Loss (Income Statement)

Key Components

  • Revenue: Total income from sales.
  • Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): Direct costs of producing what was sold.
  • Gross Profit: Revenue − COGS.
  • Operating Expenses: Rent, utilities, marketing, salaries (not directly tied to production).
  • Net Profit: Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses − Taxes.

flashcards.definition: Total income from sales before any expenses are deducted (also called gross sales or top line).
Direct costs of producing the goods/services sold: materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead.
Revenue minus COGS; shows profitability of core product/service before overhead.
Final profit after ALL expenses, including operating costs, interest, and taxes.
Net profit ÷ Revenue × 100; shows what percentage of each revenue dollar becomes profit.

quiz_questions.question_text: Revenue is also called:

options.text: Top line (the first line on an income statement)

quiz_questions.question_text: A bakery sells $200,000 in goods with $80,000 in COGS. Gross profit =
Gross profit margin for the bakery =
If operating expenses are $70,000, net profit before taxes =
Net profit margin = $50,000 ÷ $200,000 =
COGS for a service business includes:

options.text: Direct labor costs and supplies for delivering the service

quiz_questions.question_text: A positive net profit means the business:
A negative net profit (net loss) means:

options.text: Expenses exceeded revenue (the business lost money during the period)

quiz_questions.question_text: Operating expenses include:

options.text: Rent, utilities, marketing, administrative salaries, insurance (overhead not directly tied to production)

quiz_questions.question_text: Depreciation expense represents:

options.text: The gradual allocation of a long-term asset's cost over its useful life (non-cash expense)

quiz_questions.question_text: A business with $400K revenue, 45% gross margin, and $100K operating expenses has net income of approximately:
Break-even revenue = total fixed costs ÷ gross margin percentage:

options.text: If fixed costs = $90,000 and gross margin = 60%, break-even revenue = $150,000 (90000 ÷ 0.60)

quiz_questions.question_text: Contribution margin = selling price per unit − variable cost per unit. If price = $50 and variable cost = $30:
Units to break even = fixed costs ÷ contribution margin per unit. $90,000 fixed ÷ $20/unit =
A profit and loss statement covers:

options.text: A specific time period (month, quarter, year) — shows performance over time

quiz_questions.question_text: Comparing P&L statements across periods helps identify:

options.text: Trends in revenue growth, cost management, and profitability changes over time

quiz_questions.question_text: A business with declining gross margin should examine:

options.text: Whether COGS are rising faster than revenue (pricing pressure, supply cost increases)

quiz_questions.question_text: Operating leverage: a business with high fixed costs and low variable costs will see profits _____ when revenue increases.

options.text: Increase rapidly (each additional sale has low marginal cost, so more drops to profit)

quiz_questions.question_text: For a subscription business, COGS is typically:

options.text: Relatively low (low marginal cost per subscriber), leading to high gross margins (often 70-90%)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, P&L analysis applies:

options.text: Revenue calculations, margin percentages, break-even analysis, trend analysis, and cost classification

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Break-Even Analysis
summary_sections.content_html:
Break-Even Analysis

The Break-Even Point

Break-even (units) = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price per Unit − Variable Cost per Unit)

Break-even (revenue) = Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin Ratio

Graphical Representation

Total Revenue line crosses Total Cost line at the break-even point. Below = loss; above = profit.

flashcards.definition: The sales volume where total revenue = total costs; no profit or loss. The minimum sales needed to survive.
Selling price minus variable cost per unit; the amount each unit 'contributes' toward covering fixed costs.
Contribution margin ÷ selling price; the percentage of each dollar of revenue available to cover fixed costs.
Costs that remain constant regardless of production volume: rent, insurance, salaries, loan payments.
Current sales minus break-even sales; how much sales can drop before the business loses money.

quiz_questions.question_text: Break-even units = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price − Variable Cost). With $60,000 fixed costs, $40 price, $15 variable cost:
Contribution margin per unit with $40 price and $15 variable cost =
Contribution margin ratio = $25 ÷ $40 =
Break-even revenue = $60,000 ÷ 0.625 =
If selling 3,000 units (above BEP of 2,400), profit =
If fixed costs increase by $10,000, new BEP = $70,000 ÷ $25 =
If variable cost increases $5 (to $20), new CM = $20, new BEP = $60,000 ÷ $20 =
Raising price to $50 (CM = $35), new BEP = $60,000 ÷ $35 ≈
Margin of safety (selling 3,000 vs. BEP of 2,400) =

options.text: 600 units (or $24,000 in revenue, or 20% of current sales)

quiz_questions.question_text: Margin of safety percentage = 600 ÷ 3,000 =
On the break-even graph, the area BELOW the BEP represents:

options.text: Loss zone (total costs exceed total revenue)

quiz_questions.question_text: On the break-even graph, the area ABOVE the BEP represents:

options.text: Profit zone (total revenue exceeds total costs)

quiz_questions.question_text: A business with high fixed costs has a _____ break-even point.

options.text: Higher (more units must be sold just to cover the large fixed costs)

quiz_questions.question_text: DOL (Degree of Operating Leverage) = Contribution Margin ÷ Net Income. High DOL means:

options.text: Small changes in revenue cause LARGE changes in profit (high risk and high reward)

quiz_questions.question_text: To achieve a TARGET profit of $20,000: units = (Fixed Costs + Target Profit) ÷ CM = ($60K + $20K) ÷ $25 =
Multi-product break-even analysis requires:

options.text: Weighted-average contribution margin based on product sales mix (more complex but essential for multi-product businesses)

quiz_questions.question_text: Sensitivity analysis on break-even asks:

options.text: 'What happens to BEP if price changes 10%? If costs increase? If sales mix shifts?' — testing multiple scenarios

quiz_questions.question_text: A restaurant with $15,000 monthly fixed costs and $8 CM per meal breaks even at:
Break-even analysis helps entrepreneurs decide:

options.text: Whether a business idea is viable, what pricing strategy is needed, and how much volume is required to survive

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, break-even analysis is foundational because it:

options.text: Combines fixed/variable cost classification, contribution margin math, and algebraic equations in real business contexts

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Small Business Financing
summary_sections.content_html:
Small Business Financing

Funding Sources
