  • Bootstrapping: Self-funding from savings, revenue reinvestment.
  • Bank loans: Traditional debt with interest; requires collateral and good credit.
  • SBA loans: Government-backed; easier qualification, favorable terms.
  • Angel investors: Wealthy individuals investing for equity stake.
  • Venture capital: Firms investing large sums for significant equity in high-growth startups.
  • Crowdfunding: Small amounts from many people (Kickstarter, GoFundMe).

flashcards.definition: Self-funding a business using personal savings and reinvesting early profits; maintains full ownership and control.
Small Business Administration-backed loan; government guarantee reduces bank risk, enabling favorable terms for small businesses.
Wealthy individual who invests personal money in startups, typically $25,000-$500,000, in exchange for equity and mentorship.
Professional investment firms providing $500K-$50M+ to high-growth startups in exchange for significant equity (20-50%+).
Equity: selling ownership shares (no repayment, but diluted control). Debt: borrowing money (repayment required, but you keep 100% ownership).

quiz_questions.question_text: Bootstrapping means funding a business by:

options.text: Using personal savings and reinvesting business profits (self-funding)

quiz_questions.question_text: The main advantage of bootstrapping is:

options.text: You maintain 100% ownership and control (no debt obligations or investors to answer to)

quiz_questions.question_text: SBA loans offer _____ compared to traditional bank loans.

options.text: Lower interest rates, longer repayment terms, and smaller down payments (government guarantee reduces bank risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: Angel investors typically invest:

options.text: $25,000 – $500,000 (in exchange for equity and often mentorship/advice)

quiz_questions.question_text: Venture capital firms typically invest:

options.text: $500,000 – $50 million+ (in high-growth startups with huge market potential)

quiz_questions.question_text: The main downside of equity financing is:

options.text: Loss of ownership and control (investors get a share of the business and a say in decisions)

quiz_questions.question_text: The main downside of debt financing is:

options.text: Required repayment with interest regardless of business performance (adds fixed cost burden)

quiz_questions.question_text: A $100,000 SBA loan at 7% for 10 years costs approximately _____ per month.
Total interest paid on that SBA loan over 10 years ≈
Crowdfunding platforms like Kickstarter work by:

options.text: Collecting small contributions from many people; often donors receive the product or perks in exchange

quiz_questions.question_text: The average success rate for Kickstarter campaigns is approximately:

options.text: ~40% (varies by category; technology projects have lower rates)

quiz_questions.question_text: A line of credit differs from a loan because:

options.text: You only pay interest on the amount you actually draw (like a credit card for the business)

quiz_questions.question_text: Factoring (selling receivables) provides:

options.text: Immediate cash by selling unpaid invoices to a 3rd party at a discount (typically 80-90% of invoice value)

quiz_questions.question_text: Equipment leasing vs. buying: leasing advantages include:

options.text: Lower upfront cost, tax deductions, and ability to upgrade — but higher total cost over time vs. buying

quiz_questions.question_text: The cost of equity (giving up 20% ownership) on a business worth $500K =

options.text: $100,000 (20% of $500,000 in value — and this grows as the business grows)

quiz_questions.question_text: If that business grows to $5M, the 20% stake given up is now worth:
A business applying for a bank loan typically needs:

options.text: A business plan, financial statements, collateral, good personal credit, and sometimes a personal guarantee

quiz_questions.question_text: Personal guarantee on a business loan means:

options.text: The bank pays if you default
The owner is personally responsible for repayment if the business can't pay (even with an LLC)

quiz_questions.question_text: The best financing strategy often combines:

options.text: Multiple sources: bootstrapping + bank loan + possibly grants or angel investment (diversified funding)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, business financing involves:

options.text: Loan amortization, interest cost comparison, equity dilution analysis, ROI on invested capital, and cost of capital

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Entrepreneurship Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Entrepreneurship Case Studies

Case 1: Food Truck Startup

Start-up: $85,000 (truck $50K, equipment $20K, licenses/permits $5K, initial inventory $10K). Monthly fixed costs: $3,000 (parking fees, insurance, loan payment). Variable cost: $4/meal. Selling price: $12/meal. CM: $8/meal. BEP: 375 meals/month.

Case 2: Online Store

Start-up: $5,000 (website, initial inventory). Fixed: $500/month. Variable: 40% of revenue. CM ratio: 60%. BEP revenue: $833/month.

flashcards.definition: Typical startup $50-150K; lower overhead than restaurant; BEP often 300-500 meals/month depending on pricing and costs.
Low startup ($1-10K); very low fixed costs; variable costs include product COGS + shipping + platform fees.
Cash flow = actual money moving in/out. Profit = accounting measure. A business can be profitable but cash-poor (or vice versa).
(Net Profit ÷ Total Investment) × 100; measures how effectively invested capital generates profit.
Time to recover initial investment from profits; shorter = faster return of capital to the investor.

quiz_questions.question_text: Food truck BEP: $3,000 fixed ÷ $8 CM per meal =
If the food truck sells 500 meals/month, monthly profit =
Annual profit at 500 meals/month =
ROI on $85,000 food truck investment with $12,000 annual profit =
Payback period = $85,000 ÷ $12,000/year ≈
Online store BEP revenue: $500 ÷ 0.60 =
If the online store earns $3,000/month revenue, monthly profit =
Online store ROI: $1,300/month × 12 = $15,600/year on $5,000 investment =
The food truck has _____ startup costs but _____ ROI compared to the online store.

options.text: Higher startup costs but lower ROI (percentage-wise)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cash flow problems in a food truck might include:

options.text: Seasonality (slow winter months), unexpected repairs, and payment timing gaps

quiz_questions.question_text: A food truck selling 600 meals/month needs to evaluate whether to:

options.text: Hire help (increased labor cost but capacity to serve more customers — is the marginal revenue > marginal cost?)

quiz_questions.question_text: Scaling the online store from $3K to $10K/month revenue primarily requires:

options.text: Marketing investment, inventory management, and possibly additional staff — variable costs scale proportionally

quiz_questions.question_text: A subscription-based online business has the advantage of:

options.text: Recurring predictable revenue (monthly), higher lifetime customer value, and easier cash flow forecasting

quiz_questions.question_text: Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) = marketing spend ÷ new customers. $2,000 spent / 50 customers =
If average customer lifetime value (LTV) is $200 and CAC is $40, the LTV:CAC ratio =
A business should expand if the marginal unit's:

options.text: Marginal revenue exceeds marginal cost (each additional unit sold adds more to revenue than to cost)

quiz_questions.question_text: Seasonality can be managed by:

options.text: Diversifying products, building cash reserves during peak months, and adjusting expenses

quiz_questions.question_text: The food truck's variable cost increase from $4 to $5/meal (new CM = $7) changes BEP to:
Both case studies demonstrate that entrepreneurship requires:

options.text: Careful financial analysis: BEP, ROI, cash flow management, and continuous cost monitoring

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, case studies apply:

options.text: BEP, ROI, payback period, cash flow, CAC/LTV, and profitability analysis to realistic scenarios

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Marketing & Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Marketing & Finance

Marketing Budget & ROI

Marketing budget is typically 5-10% of revenue for established businesses, 15-20% for startups.

Key Marketing Metrics

  • CAC: Customer Acquisition Cost = Marketing Spend ÷ New Customers.
  • LTV: Customer Lifetime Value = Avg Purchase × Frequency × Customer Lifespan.
  • ROAS: Return on Ad Spend = Revenue from Ads ÷ Cost of Ads.
  • Conversion Rate: Purchasers ÷ Visitors × 100.

flashcards.definition: Total marketing spend ÷ number of new customers acquired; how much it costs to gain one customer.
Total revenue expected from a customer over the entire relationship; LTV should be ≥ 3× CAC for a healthy business.
Revenue generated ÷ advertising cost; a ROAS of 4:1 means $4 revenue per $1 spent on ads.
Percentage of visitors/leads who complete a desired action (purchase, sign-up); typically 2-5% for e-commerce.
(Revenue from Marketing − Marketing Cost) ÷ Marketing Cost × 100; measures overall marketing effectiveness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A startup spending $5,000/month on marketing to acquire 100 customers has a CAC of:
If each customer spends $30/month for an average of 12 months, LTV =
LTV:CAC ratio = $360:$50 =
A Google Ads campaign: $2,000 spent generates $10,000 revenue. ROAS =

options.text: 5:1 ($10,000 ÷ $2,000 — every $1 in ads generates $5 in revenue)

quiz_questions.question_text: A website with 10,000 visitors and 250 purchases has a conversion rate of:
To increase revenue, a business can optimize:

options.text: Conversion rate (more buyers from same traffic), average order value, and customer retention — all cheaper than acquiring new customers

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing conversion rate from 2.5% to 3.5% on 10,000 visitors adds _____ more customers.
Email marketing typically has the highest ROI of any channel at approximately:

options.text: $36-40 per $1 spent (extremely high because the audience is already engaged)

quiz_questions.question_text: Social media marketing is most effective for:

options.text: Brand awareness, community building, and customer engagement (conversion to sales often requires additional steps)

quiz_questions.question_text: A/B testing in marketing compares:

options.text: Two versions of an ad, email, or webpage to determine which performs better (data-driven optimization)

quiz_questions.question_text: The marketing funnel stages are:

options.text: Awareness → Interest → Consideration → Conversion → Retention (guiding prospects to become loyal customers)

quiz_questions.question_text: Retention marketing costs _____ as much as acquisition marketing.

options.text: 1/5 to 1/7 (retaining existing customers is 5-7× cheaper than acquiring new ones)

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing customer retention by 5% can increase profits by:

options.text: 25-95% (existing customers buy more often and refer others)

quiz_questions.question_text: Price elasticity measures:

options.text: How much demand changes when price changes (elastic: demand drops significantly; inelastic: demand barely changes)

quiz_questions.question_text: A product with inelastic demand can:

options.text: Increase price without losing many customers (necessities, unique products, strong brands)

quiz_questions.question_text: Break-even on a marketing campaign: $3,000 spend needs to generate how much revenue at 30% margin?

options.text: $10,000 ($3,000 ÷ 0.30 — because each dollar of revenue only contributes $0.30 to cover the marketing cost)

quiz_questions.question_text: Churn rate is:

options.text: Percentage of customers who stop buying in a given period (monthly/annually)

quiz_questions.question_text: Reducing churn from 5% to 3% monthly on 1,000 customers saves _____ customers/month.
Marketing attribution determines:

options.text: Which marketing channels (social, email, search, etc.) actually drove the conversion — critical for allocating budget

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, marketing finance applies:

options.text: CAC, LTV, ROAS, conversion rates, elasticity, and ROI — all percentage and ratio-based financial metrics

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Business Risk Management
summary_sections.content_html:
Business Risk Management

Types of Business Risk

  • Market risk: Demand changes, competition, economic downturns.
  • Financial risk: Cash flow problems, excessive debt, interest rate changes.
  • Operational risk: Supply chain disruptions, equipment failure, employee issues.
  • Legal/Regulatory risk: Lawsuits, regulation changes, compliance failures.

flashcards.definition: Risk from changing customer demand, new competitors, or economic conditions affecting sales.
Risk from cash flow shortfalls, overleveraging (too much debt), or interest rate increases.
Risk from internal processes: supply chain disruption, equipment failure, key employee departure.
Spreading risk across multiple products, markets, or revenue streams to reduce dependence on any single source.
Policies protecting against specific risks: liability, property, workers' comp, business interruption, cyber liability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Market risk includes:

options.text: Demand changes, new competitors, economic downturns, and shifting consumer preferences

quiz_questions.question_text: A business with one product and one customer has _____ risk.

options.text: Very high (single point of failure; loss of that customer or product = total revenue loss)

quiz_questions.question_text: Diversification reduces risk by:

options.text: Spreading revenue across multiple products/markets so one failure doesn't destroy the business

quiz_questions.question_text: Cash flow risk can be managed by:

options.text: Maintaining cash reserves (3-6 months operating expenses), diversifying revenue, and monitoring receivables

quiz_questions.question_text: Excessive debt (overleveraging) is dangerous because:

options.text: Fixed debt payments must be made regardless of revenue — a sales downturn can cause default and bankruptcy

quiz_questions.question_text: The debt-to-equity ratio measures:

options.text: How much of the business is financed by debt vs. owner investment (high ratio = more financial risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: A business with $200,000 debt and $100,000 equity has a debt-to-equity ratio of:
Business interruption insurance covers:

options.text: Lost income and operating expenses when a covered event (fire, natural disaster) forces temporary closure

quiz_questions.question_text: Cyber liability insurance covers:

options.text: Data breaches, hacking incidents, ransomware attacks, and customer data compromises

quiz_questions.question_text: Workers' compensation insurance is _____ in most states.

options.text: Legally required (covers employee injuries/illness occurring during work)

quiz_questions.question_text: Key person insurance protects a business if:

options.text: A critical employee (founder, top salesperson, key engineer) dies or becomes disabled

quiz_questions.question_text: Supply chain risk can be mitigated by:

options.text: Using multiple suppliers, maintaining safety stock, and having backup sourcing plans

quiz_questions.question_text: A SWOT analysis examines:

options.text: Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats — internal and external factors affecting the business

quiz_questions.question_text: Scenario planning involves:

options.text: Creating multiple 'what if' scenarios (best case, worst case, most likely) to prepare for various outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: An emergency fund for a business should cover:

options.text: 3-6 months of operating expenses (protects against temporary revenue disruptions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Professional liability (errors & omissions) insurance protects:

options.text: Against claims that your professional service/advice caused financial harm to a client

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest risk to small businesses is often:

options.text: Cash flow problems (running out of money before becoming profitable or during slow periods)

quiz_questions.question_text: Risk monitoring involves:

options.text: Ongoing assessment: tracking KPIs, reviewing financial statements monthly, and updating risk plans regularly

quiz_questions.question_text: Business continuity planning ensures:

options.text: The business can continue operating (or recover quickly) after a disaster, cyberattack, or major disruption

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, business risk management uses:

options.text: Ratio analysis (D/E, current ratio), insurance cost-benefit, cash flow forecasting, and scenario modeling

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Business Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Business Finance

Essential Business Finance Tools

  • Accounting software: QuickBooks, Xero, FreshBooks — automate bookkeeping.
  • Payment processing: Square, Stripe, PayPal — accept digital payments.
  • Payroll services: Gusto, ADP — automate payroll taxes and compliance.
  • Spreadsheet modeling: Excel/Google Sheets for financial projections and analysis.

flashcards.definition: Leading small business accounting software; tracks income, expenses, invoicing, and generates financial reports automatically.

Real-time visual display of key financial metrics (revenue, expenses, cash flow, profit) for quick business health assessment.

Financial technology — innovations like digital payments, automated lending, blockchain, and AI-driven financial tools.

quiz_questions.question_text: QuickBooks and Xero are primarily used for:

options.text: Payment processing + inventory tracking + sales analytics + receipt generation + customer data

quiz_questions.question_text: Automated invoicing saves time by:

options.text: Generating, sending, tracking, and following up on invoices automatically — reducing human error and speeding collections

quiz_questions.question_text: Expense tracking apps categorize spending by:

options.text: Automatically categorizing transactions, scanning receipts, and generating reports for tax preparation

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial projections in Excel use _____ to model scenarios.

options.text: Formulas, charts, pivot tables, what-if analysis, Goal Seek, and Solver for complex financial modeling

quiz_questions.question_text: The NPV function in Excel calculates:

quiz_questions.question_text: The PMT function in Excel calculates:

quiz_questions.question_text: Blockchain technology in business finance enables:

options.text: Transparent, tamper-proof transaction records, smart contracts, and cryptocurrency payments

quiz_questions.question_text: AI in business finance helps with:

options.text: Fraud detection, cash flow forecasting, automated bookkeeping, credit scoring, and financial pattern recognition

quiz_questions.question_text: Integration between accounting and banking software:

options.text: Automatically imports bank transactions into accounting records, reducing manual data entry and errors

quiz_questions.question_text: The cost of accounting software for small businesses is typically:

options.text: $25-75/month (very affordable relative to the time saved and accuracy gained)

quiz_questions.question_text: Technology reduces business finance errors by:

options.text: 80-90% (automated calculations, bank reconciliation, and built-in error checks dramatically reduce mistakes)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, business technology tools:

options.text: Apply course concepts (NPV, PMT, break-even, projections) through software that professionals actually use daily

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Stock Market Basics
summary_sections.content_html:
Stock Market Basics

What Is a Stock?

A stock represents fractional ownership of a company. Shareholders earn returns through price appreciation and dividends.

Major Exchanges

  • NYSE: New York Stock Exchange — largest by market capitalization.
  • NASDAQ: Technology-heavy electronic exchange.
  • Market capitalization = Share Price × Shares Outstanding.

flashcards.definition: A share of ownership in a company; entitles the holder to a portion of profits (dividends) and voting rights.
Share price × total shares outstanding; classifies companies as large-cap (>$10B), mid-cap, or small-cap (<$2B).
A portion of company profits distributed to shareholders, usually quarterly; expressed as yield (annual dividend ÷ price).
Period of rising stock prices (generally 20%+ sustained increase); associated with economic optimism.
Period of falling stock prices (20%+ decline from recent highs); associated with recession fears.

quiz_questions.question_text: A stock represents:
Market capitalization of a company with 50 million shares at $80/share =
A large-cap stock has a market cap of:
The NYSE is the world's largest exchange by:

options.text: Market capitalization (total value of all listed companies)

quiz_questions.question_text: NASDAQ is known for listing many:

options.text: Technology companies (Apple, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, etc.)

quiz_questions.question_text: A dividend yield of 3% on a $100 stock means annual dividends of:
A bull market is characterized by:

options.text: Rising prices (generally 20%+ sustained increase) with investor optimism

quiz_questions.question_text: A bear market occurs when prices fall:
An IPO (Initial Public Offering) is when:

options.text: A private company sells shares to the public for the first time on a stock exchange

quiz_questions.question_text: Stock exchanges match:

options.text: Buyers and sellers of stocks (facilitating price discovery and trade execution)

quiz_questions.question_text: The S&P 500 tracks:

options.text: 500 large US companies (a widely used benchmark for the overall US stock market)
