options.text: Competition and technology led most major brokers to offer $0 commission trades

quiz_questions.question_text: A stock split (2-for-1) means:

options.text: Each share becomes 2 shares at half the price (total value unchanged; more affordable per share)

quiz_questions.question_text: Market orders execute:

options.text: Immediately at the current market price (fastest but price not guaranteed)

quiz_questions.question_text: Limit orders execute:

options.text: Only at your specified price or better (control over price but may not execute)

quiz_questions.question_text: The stock market has historically returned approximately _____ per year (long-term average, S&P 500).
For financial math, the stock market applies:

options.text: Percentage returns, market cap calculations, dividend yields, and compound growth — core financial concepts

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Reading Financial Tables
summary_sections.content_html:
Reading Financial Tables & Statements

Stock Quote Components

  • Ticker: Abbreviated symbol (AAPL, MSFT).
  • Open/Close: Price at market open/close.
  • High/Low: Highest/lowest price during the trading day.
  • Volume: Number of shares traded.
  • P/E Ratio: Price ÷ Earnings Per Share — valuation metric.

flashcards.definition: Short identifier for a publicly traded company (e.g., AAPL = Apple, MSFT = Microsoft, GOOGL = Alphabet).
Price-to-Earnings ratio; stock price ÷ earnings per share. Higher P/E suggests higher growth expectations.
Net income ÷ shares outstanding; fundamental measure of company profitability on a per-share basis.
Number of shares traded in a given period; high volume = high interest/liquidity.
Highest and lowest prices over the past year; provides context for current price level.

quiz_questions.question_text: A stock's ticker symbol is:

options.text: A short abbreviated identifier used on exchanges (e.g., AAPL for Apple)

quiz_questions.question_text: If a stock opens at $150 and closes at $155, the day's change is:
Volume of 10 million shares means:

options.text: 10 million shares were bought/sold during that trading session

quiz_questions.question_text: High volume with rising prices generally indicates:

options.text: Strong buying interest and bullish sentiment

quiz_questions.question_text: P/E ratio = Price $200 ÷ EPS $8 =
A P/E of 25 vs. industry average of 15 suggests:

options.text: The stock is relatively expensive (market expects higher growth than the industry average)

quiz_questions.question_text: EPS = Net income $500M ÷ 100M shares =
A stock at $100 with EPS of $5 has a P/E of:
The 52-week high/low provides context for:

options.text: Where the current price sits relative to its past year range (near highs vs. lows)

quiz_questions.question_text: Day range: $148.50-$152.30 means:

options.text: The lowest trade was $148.50 and highest was $152.30 during that session

quiz_questions.question_text: Market cap on a quote page is calculated as:
Dividend yield = Annual dividends per share ÷ current stock price. $3.20 dividend ÷ $80 price =
PEG ratio = P/E ÷ earnings growth rate. P/E of 25 with 20% growth = PEG of:

options.text: 1.25 (PEG < 1 is considered undervalued; > 1 may be overvalued relative to growth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Price-to-Book (P/B) ratio compares stock price to:

options.text: Book value per share (total assets minus liabilities ÷ shares outstanding)

quiz_questions.question_text: A stock trading below book value (P/B < 1) may be:

options.text: Potentially undervalued (but could also indicate underlying problems — requires further analysis)

quiz_questions.question_text: Analyst ratings of 'Buy,' 'Hold,' 'Sell' are:

options.text: Opinions based on analysis — not guarantees; always do your own research

quiz_questions.question_text: An income statement shows:

options.text: Revenue, expenses, and net income over a period (profitability report)

quiz_questions.question_text: A balance sheet shows:

options.text: Assets, liabilities, and equity at a point in time (financial position snapshot)

quiz_questions.question_text: Reading financial tables requires understanding:

options.text: Multiple interconnected metrics: price, volume, P/E, EPS, yield, market cap, and how they relate

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, financial literacy includes:

options.text: Interpreting ratios, percentages, and financial statements to make informed investment decisions

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Market Indices & Benchmarks
summary_sections.content_html:
Market Indices & Benchmarks

Major US Indices

  • S&P 500: 500 large US companies — the most-used US market benchmark.
  • Dow Jones (DJIA): 30 large industrial companies — price-weighted.
  • NASDAQ Composite: All NASDAQ-listed stocks — tech-heavy.
  • Russell 2000: 2,000 small-cap companies.

Index Weighting

Market-cap weighted (S&P 500): larger companies have more influence. Price-weighted (DJIA): higher-priced stocks have more influence.

flashcards.definition: Index of 500 large US companies weighted by market cap; covers ~80% of US stock market value; primary benchmark.
Index of 30 large US companies weighted by stock price; oldest widely followed US index (since 1896).
A fund that passively tracks an index (like S&P 500); low fees because no active stock picking is needed.
A standard for measuring investment performance (e.g., 'My fund returned 12% vs. S&P 500's 10%').
Index weighting method where larger companies (higher market cap) have proportionally greater influence on the index.

quiz_questions.question_text: The S&P 500 represents approximately _____ of the total US stock market value.
The Dow Jones tracks _____ companies and is _____ weighted.
In a price-weighted index, a stock at $300 has _____ influence than a stock at $50.

options.text: More (price-weighted means higher-priced stocks move the index more, regardless of company size)

quiz_questions.question_text: In a market-cap weighted index (S&P 500), Apple at $3T market cap has _____ influence than a $30B company.

options.text: Much more (100× larger market cap = 100× more influence on the index)

quiz_questions.question_text: The NASDAQ Composite is heavily weighted toward:
The Russell 2000 tracks:

options.text: Small-cap stocks (the 2,000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000)

quiz_questions.question_text: An index fund that tracks the S&P 500 holds:

options.text: ~500 stocks in proportions matching the index (passive replication)

quiz_questions.question_text: Index fund expense ratios are typically:

options.text: 0.03-0.20% (very low because no active management or stock picking is needed)

quiz_questions.question_text: Actively managed funds charge _____ than index funds.

options.text: More (typically 0.5-1.5% — they need to pay fund managers and research teams)

quiz_questions.question_text: Over 20 years, approximately _____ of actively managed funds underperform their benchmark index.

options.text: 80-90% (this is why index investing has become so popular)

quiz_questions.question_text: If the S&P 500 returns 10% and your fund returns 8%, the fund _____ the benchmark.
Total Stock Market indices (like VTI) include:

options.text: Large, mid, small, and micro-cap stocks — the entire US stock market

quiz_questions.question_text: International indices include:

options.text: MSCI EAFE (developed international), MSCI Emerging Markets, and others tracking non-US stocks

quiz_questions.question_text: The Dow going from 30,000 to 33,000 represents a gain of:
Historical S&P 500 average annual return (1926-present) is approximately:
Dollar cost averaging into an S&P 500 index fund means:

options.text: Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals (buying more shares when prices are low, fewer when high)

quiz_questions.question_text: The volatility of the Russell 2000 (small-cap) is typically _____ than the S&P 500.

options.text: Higher (small-cap stocks are more volatile but have historically provided higher long-term returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Bond indices (like Bloomberg Aggregate) track:

options.text: The bond market: government, corporate, and mortgage-backed bonds (used as a fixed-income benchmark)

quiz_questions.question_text: A balanced portfolio might use _____ as its benchmark.

options.text: A blended benchmark like 60% S&P 500 + 40% Bond Aggregate (matching the portfolio's asset allocation)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, indices provide:

options.text: Percentage return calculations, benchmark comparison, and data for long-term compound growth analysis

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Trading Strategies
summary_sections.content_html:
Trading & Investment Strategies

Approaches to the Market

  • Buy and Hold: Long-term ownership; ignoring short-term fluctuations.
  • Value Investing: Buying undervalued stocks (low P/E, below book value).
  • Growth Investing: Buying companies with high earnings growth potential.
  • Index Investing: Passive; match the market through index funds.
  • Dollar Cost Averaging: Fixed amount at regular intervals.

flashcards.definition: Long-term strategy of purchasing quality investments and holding through market ups and downs; lowest cost, historically very effective.
Strategy of finding stocks trading below their intrinsic value (low P/E, P/B); pioneered by Benjamin Graham and Warren Buffett.
Strategy focused on companies with high revenue/earnings growth, often with higher P/E ratios; accepts higher valuations for faster growth.
Investing a fixed dollar amount at regular intervals regardless of price; reduces timing risk and emotional decision-making.
Dividing investments among asset classes (stocks, bonds, cash, real estate) based on risk tolerance, goals, and time horizon.

quiz_questions.question_text: Buy and hold works best over:

options.text: Long periods (10+ years) — time in the market beats timing the market

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 invested in the S&P 500 in 1990 would be worth approximately _____ by 2024 (reinvesting dividends).

options.text: ~$200,000+ (the power of long-term compounding at ~10% average annual returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Value investors look for stocks with:

options.text: Low P/E ratios, low price-to-book, and prices below estimated intrinsic value

quiz_questions.question_text: Warren Buffett's approach is primarily:

options.text: Value investing (buying great companies at fair prices and holding forever)

quiz_questions.question_text: Growth investors are willing to pay:

options.text: Higher P/E ratios for companies with rapid revenue and earnings growth (betting on future expansion)

quiz_questions.question_text: Index investing involves:

options.text: Buying funds that track entire market indices (passive, low-cost, highly diversified)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar cost averaging: investing $500/month when prices are $50, $40, and $60 buys:

options.text: 10, 12.5, and 8.33 shares (more shares when cheap, fewer when expensive — average cost < average price)

quiz_questions.question_text: The average cost per share with DCA ($1,500 total ÷ 30.83 total shares) ≈
Asset allocation of 80/20 (stocks/bonds) is appropriate for:

options.text: Young investors with a long time horizon (can weather stock volatility for higher returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Asset allocation of 40/60 (stocks/bonds) is appropriate for:

options.text: Near-retirees or conservative investors who need stability and income over growth

quiz_questions.question_text: Rebalancing a portfolio means:

options.text: Periodically adjusting back to target allocation (sell what grew above target, buy what fell below)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-efficient investing places _____ in taxable accounts and _____ in tax-advantaged accounts.

options.text: Index funds/stocks (low turnover, capital gains), and bonds/REITs (high tax on income) in tax-advantaged

quiz_questions.question_text: Day trading (buying and selling within one day) is:

options.text: Extremely risky — studies show 70-90% of day traders lose money; emotional decisions and transaction costs erode returns

quiz_questions.question_text: The EMH (Efficient Market Hypothesis) suggests:

options.text: Stock prices reflect all available information — making it very difficult to consistently beat the market

quiz_questions.question_text: Momentum investing buys stocks that are:

options.text: Rising in price (trend-following — betting that recent winners will continue to outperform near-term)

quiz_questions.question_text: Contrarian investing does the opposite: buying when others are:

options.text: Selling/fearful (buying undervalued stocks during pessimism), and selling when others are greedy

quiz_questions.explanation: Be greedy when others are fearful.

quiz_questions.question_text: A diversified portfolio of 60% US stocks, 25% international stocks, 15% bonds provides:

options.text: Geographic diversification, asset class diversification, and reduced correlation for smoother long-term returns

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important factor in long-term investment success is:

options.text: Asset allocation and consistent investing over time (staying the course through market cycles)

quiz_questions.question_text: Compound returns: $500/month at 10% for 30 years grows to approximately:

options.text: ~$1.1 million (the power of consistent investing and compound growth)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, investment strategies apply:

options.text: Compound interest, percentage returns, ratio analysis, and probability to building long-term wealth

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Global Finance & Currency
summary_sections.content_html:
Global Finance & Currency

Foreign Exchange (Forex)

Currencies trade in pairs (USD/EUR). Exchange rates fluctuate based on supply/demand, interest rates, and economics.

Key Concepts

  • Exchange rate: Price of one currency in terms of another.
  • Appreciation/Depreciation: Currency gaining/losing value relative to another.
  • Purchasing Power Parity: Theory that exchange rates should equalize prices across countries.

flashcards.definition: The price of one currency expressed in another (e.g., 1 USD = 0.92 EUR means $1 buys €0.92).
When a currency gains value relative to another (stronger dollar means it buys more foreign currency).
When a currency loses value relative to another (weaker dollar means it buys less foreign currency).
Theory that exchange rates should adjust so identical goods cost the same across countries in the long run.
Investment by a company or individual in one country into business interests in another country (factories, offices).

quiz_questions.question_text: An exchange rate of 1 USD = 110 JPY means:
Converting $500 to euros at 1 USD = 0.92 EUR:
Converting €200 to dollars at 1 EUR = 1.09 USD:
If the dollar strengthens (appreciates), American tourists abroad:

options.text: Pay less (their dollars buy more foreign currency, making foreign goods cheaper)

quiz_questions.question_text: If the dollar weakens (depreciates), US exports become:

options.text: Cheaper abroad (foreign buyers need less of their own currency to buy US goods)

quiz_questions.question_text: A strong dollar hurts US companies that:

options.text: Export goods (their products become more expensive for foreign buyers — reducing competitiveness)

quiz_questions.question_text: The forex market trades approximately _____ per day.

options.text: $6-7 trillion (the largest financial market in the world)

quiz_questions.question_text: Interest rate increases by the Fed typically cause the dollar to:

options.text: Strengthen (higher rates attract foreign capital seeking better returns — increasing demand for USD)

quiz_questions.question_text: Inflation typically causes a currency to:

options.text: Weaken/depreciate (higher prices reduce purchasing power relative to other currencies)

quiz_questions.question_text: Purchasing Power Parity suggests a Big Mac should cost:

options.text: Approximately the same (when converted) across countries, adjusted for exchange rates

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'Big Mac Index' is an informal measure of:

options.text: Currency valuation — comparing Big Mac prices worldwide to assess whether currencies are over/undervalued

quiz_questions.question_text: Currency hedging is used by companies to:

options.text: Protect against exchange rate changes that could reduce profits from international operations

quiz_questions.question_text: A US company expecting €1M payment in 3 months can hedge by:

options.text: Buying a forward contract to lock in today's exchange rate (guaranteeing the dollar amount regardless of rate changes)

quiz_questions.question_text: Trade deficit means a country:

options.text: Imports more than exports (buying more from other countries than selling to them)

quiz_questions.question_text: The US trade deficit is approximately:
GDP (Gross Domestic Product) measures:

options.text: The total value of all goods and services produced in a country during a specific period

quiz_questions.question_text: Emerging market investments offer:

options.text: Potentially higher returns but with higher risk (political instability, currency volatility, less regulation)

quiz_questions.question_text: International diversification benefits investors because:

options.text: Different countries' markets don't perfectly correlate — when US markets decline, other markets may hold steady or rise

quiz_questions.question_text: Cryptocurrency as a global currency is:

options.text: Still evolving — used for some transactions but faces volatility, regulation, and adoption challenges

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, global finance applies:

options.text: Currency conversion, exchange rate calculations, and international pricing — essential for an increasingly global economy

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Market Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
Market Case Studies

Case 1: 2008 Financial Crisis

S&P 500 fell ~57% from Oct 2007 to Mar 2009. An investor who stayed invested recovered by Mar 2013 and gained ~400% by 2024. One who sold at the bottom locked in losses permanently.

Case 2: COVID Crash & Recovery (2020)

S&P 500 dropped 34% in 23 trading days (fastest bear market ever). Recovery: new highs within 5 months.

flashcards.definition: A rapid, severe decline in stock prices (often 20%+ in days or weeks); caused by panic, economic shocks, or systemic failures.
The period needed for markets to return to pre-crash levels; historically ranges from months to years.
Study of psychological biases affecting financial decisions: panic selling, herd mentality, overconfidence, loss aversion.
Emotionally driven selling during market drops; often locks in losses at or near the bottom — the worst time to sell.
Tendency of markets to return to long-term averages over time; crashes are typically followed by recoveries.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the 2008 crisis, the S&P 500 fell approximately:
An investor who stayed invested through 2008 recovered fully by approximately:
$100,000 invested at the 2009 bottom grew to approximately _____ by 2024.

options.text: ~$600,000+ (one of the greatest buying opportunities in history)

quiz_questions.question_text: The COVID crash of 2020 saw the S&P 500 drop _____ in just 23 trading days.
The COVID recovery to new all-time highs took approximately:

options.text: ~5 months (one of the fastest recoveries in history)

quiz_questions.question_text: The main lesson from both crashes for long-term investors is:

options.text: Sell immediately at the first sign of trouble
Stay invested — time in the market beats timing the market; panic selling locks in losses

quiz_questions.question_text: Panic selling during a crash is harmful because:

options.text: You sell at low prices and typically miss the rebound (the best days often follow the worst days)

quiz_questions.question_text: Missing the 10 best days in the market over 20 years can cut returns by:

options.text: More than 50% (many of the best days occur immediately after the worst days, during recoveries)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar cost averaging during a crash means:

options.text: Buying more shares at lower prices (DCA into falling markets = lower average cost = higher returns on recovery)

quiz_questions.question_text: The dot-com bubble (2000) primarily affected:

options.text: Technology stocks (NASDAQ fell ~78% from peak — speculative valuations collapsed)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2008 crisis was primarily caused by:

options.text: Subprime mortgages, excessive leverage, credit default swaps, and inadequate regulation (housing bubble burst)

quiz_questions.question_text: Diversification during the 2008 crisis helped because:

options.text: Bonds gained value as stocks fell — offsetting some losses in a balanced portfolio

quiz_questions.question_text: Recency bias causes investors to:

options.text: Overweight recent events (expect crashes after crashes, or endless gains after bull markets) — clouding rational judgment

quiz_questions.question_text: Loss aversion means:

options.text: The pain of losing $1 feels 2× worse than the pleasure of gaining $1 — leading to panic selling and missed opportunities

quiz_questions.question_text: Herd mentality in investing means:

options.text: Following the crowd — buying when everyone buys (FOMO) and selling when everyone sells (panic)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1987 'Black Monday' crash saw the Dow fall _____ in ONE day.

options.text: ~22% (the largest single-day percentage drop in history)

quiz_questions.question_text: Japan's Nikkei index peaked in 1989 and took _____ to recover to that level.

options.text: ~34 years (2024) — a cautionary tale about concentration in a single country

quiz_questions.question_text: The lesson from Japan for portfolio construction is:

options.text: Diversify internationally — no single country's market is guaranteed to perform well over any given period

quiz_questions.question_text: Market crashes happen on average every _____ years (declines of 20%+).

options.text: ~3-5 (bear markets are a normal part of investing — you will experience several in a lifetime)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, market history teaches:

options.text: Compound growth, recovery math, the cost of emotional decisions, and the power of staying invested through volatility

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Technology in Markets
summary_sections.content_html:
Technology in Financial Markets

Modern Trading Technology

  • Online brokerages: Fidelity, Schwab, Robinhood — $0 commission trades.
  • Robo-advisors: Betterment, Wealthfront — automated portfolio management.
  • Trading apps: Mobile access to real-time quotes, trading, and account management.
  • Algorithmic trading: Computer programs executing trades at high speed based on algorithms.

flashcards.definition: Digital platform for buying/selling stocks, ETFs, and other securities; most major brokers now offer $0 commissions.
Automated investment platform that creates and manages a diversified portfolio based on your risk tolerance and goals; low fees (~0.25%).
Computer programs executing trades based on mathematical rules at speeds impossible for humans.
Ability to buy a portion of a share (e.g., $50 worth of a $3,000 stock); makes expensive stocks accessible to all investors.
Technology-driven innovation transforming financial services: mobile banking, peer-to-peer payments, cryptocurrency, automated investing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Major online brokerages now charge _____ per stock/ETF trade.

options.text: $0 (commission-free trading has become the industry standard)

quiz_questions.question_text: Robo-advisors typically charge:

options.text: ~0.25% annually (much less than traditional financial advisors' 1%+)

quiz_questions.question_text: A robo-advisor with $50,000 at 0.25% charges _____ per year.
A traditional advisor at 1% on $50,000 charges _____ per year.
The fee difference ($500 − $125 = $375/year) compounded over 30 years at 8% ≈

options.text: ~$42,000+ in savings (fee differences compound dramatically over decades)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fractional shares allow investors to:

options.text: Buy any dollar amount of any stock (e.g., $10 of Amazon or $25 of Berkshire Hathaway)

quiz_questions.question_text: ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds) combine benefits of:

options.text: Mutual fund diversification with stock-like trading (buy/sell anytime during market hours, low fees)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most popular ETFs (SPY, VOO, VTI) track:

options.text: Broad market indices (S&P 500 or Total Stock Market)

quiz_questions.question_text: Algorithmic trading accounts for approximately _____ of US stock market volume.
High-frequency trading (HFT) involves:

options.text: Executing thousands of trades per second using algorithms to profit from tiny price differences

quiz_questions.question_text: Mobile trading apps have _____ investor participation.

options.text: Dramatically increased (especially among younger investors who can invest from their phones)

quiz_questions.question_text: A concern with easy mobile trading is:

options.text: Gamification of investing leading to excessive trading, speculation, and emotional decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: Social trading platforms (like eToro) allow:

options.text: Copying the trades of experienced investors (but past performance doesn't guarantee future results)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cryptocurrency exchanges (Coinbase, Kraken) allow:

options.text: Buying, selling, and trading digital currencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum, etc.)

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-loss harvesting is automated by robo-advisors to:

options.text: Systematically sell losing positions to offset gains — potentially saving thousands in taxes annually

quiz_questions.question_text: Real-time market data and analysis tools give retail investors access to information that was once:

options.text: Only available to professional traders and institutions (leveling the playing field)

quiz_questions.question_text: Blockchain technology could revolutionize stock trading by:

options.text: Enabling instant settlement (currently T+1), reducing intermediaries, and increasing transparency

quiz_questions.question_text: AI-powered investment tools can:

options.text: Analyze vast amounts of data, identify patterns, and assist with portfolio optimization — but can't predict the future

quiz_questions.question_text: The risks of fintech include:

options.text: Security breaches, algorithmic errors, overreliance on automation, and potential for flash crashes from algorithmic trading

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, technology tools:

options.text: Make it easier to apply financial math concepts (compounding, diversification, tax optimization) but understanding the math behind the tools is still essential

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Behavioral Finance
summary_sections.content_html:
Behavioral Finance

Common Cognitive Biases

  • Loss aversion: Pain of loss > pleasure of equal gain (2:1 ratio).
  • Confirmation bias: Seeking info that supports existing beliefs.
  • Anchoring: Over-relying on first piece of information (purchase price).
  • Overconfidence: Overestimating one's ability to pick stocks or time markets.
  • Recency bias: Overweighting recent events in predictions.

flashcards.definition: The psychological tendency where losses feel ~2× worse than equivalent gains feel good; leads to panic selling.
Tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information that confirms pre-existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.
Fixating on a reference point (like purchase price) when making decisions, even when it's irrelevant to current value.
Overestimating one's ability to predict markets or pick winners; leads to excessive trading and concentrated positions.
Anxiety about missing profitable opportunities; drives buying at peaks (when 'everyone' is talking about a stock).

quiz_questions.question_text: Loss aversion means the pain of losing $100 feels equivalent to the pleasure of gaining approximately:

options.text: $200 (2× ratio — losses hurt twice as much as equivalent gains feel good)

quiz_questions.question_text: Loss aversion leads investors to:

options.text: Both — sell winners too early (to 'lock in' gains) and hold losers too long (hoping to break even)

quiz_questions.question_text: Confirmation bias causes an investor who loves Tesla to:

options.text: Seek out positive Tesla news and dismiss negative data (selectively processing information)

quiz_questions.question_text: Anchoring on a purchase price of $100 might cause you to hold a stock worth $60 because:

options.text: You're 'anchored' to $100 and waiting to 'get back to even' — but the market doesn't care what YOU paid

quiz_questions.question_text: Overconfidence leads to:

options.text: Excessive trading (which increases costs and taxes), concentrated positions, and failure to diversify

quiz_questions.question_text: Studies show that investors who trade most frequently:

options.text: Earn significantly lower returns than buy-and-hold investors (transaction costs and bad timing erode returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Recency bias after a market crash causes investors to:

options.text: Expect more declines and sell/avoid investing (recent bad experience dominates their outlook, even when recovery is likely)

quiz_questions.question_text: FOMO during a bull market leads investors to:

options.text: Buy at high valuations because 'everyone else is making money' (often entering near market peaks)

quiz_questions.question_text: Herd mentality means:

options.text: Following the crowd — buying when everyone buys, selling when everyone sells (amplifying market swings)

quiz_questions.question_text: The sunk cost fallacy in investing means:

options.text: Continuing to hold a losing investment because you've 'already invested so much' (past costs shouldn't affect future decisions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Endowment effect causes people to:

options.text: Overvalue what they already own (demanding a higher price to sell than they'd pay to buy the same thing)

quiz_questions.question_text: Mental accounting causes someone to treat:

options.text: Money differently based on source (treating a $5,000 tax refund differently than $5,000 salary — but it's all money)

quiz_questions.question_text: Availability bias means:

options.text: Overestimating the probability of events that come easily to mind (recent crashes, headline-grabbing events)

quiz_questions.question_text: Framing effect: '90% success rate' vs. '10% failure rate' — same data, but people:

options.text: Respond more positively to '90% success' even though both convey identical information

quiz_questions.question_text: The antidote to most behavioral biases is:

options.text: A written investment plan followed systematically (automation, rules-based investing, removing emotion from decisions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dollar cost averaging combats behavioral biases by:

options.text: Removing the decision of 'when to invest' — automated, consistent investing regardless of market conditions or emotions

quiz_questions.question_text: Index investing combats overconfidence by:

options.text: Acknowledging that most people can't beat the market — and matching market returns is a winning strategy

quiz_questions.question_text: Keeping an investment journal helps because:

options.text: It creates accountability and allows you to review past decisions objectively (identifying patterns of bias)

quiz_questions.question_text: The efficient market hypothesis (EMH) relates to behavioral finance because:

options.text: EMH says markets are rational; behavioral finance shows individual investors often are NOT — creating both opportunities and risks

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, behavioral finance shows that:

options.text: Understanding the math AND the psychology behind financial decisions is essential — knowing the right answer doesn't help if emotions override rational choices

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Time Value of Money
summary_sections.content_html:
Time Value of Money (TVM)

Core Principle

A dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow because it can be invested to earn a return.

Key Formulas

  • Future Value: FV = PV × (1 + r)^n
  • Present Value: PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
  • FV of Annuity: FV = PMT × [((1+r)^n − 1) / r]
  • PV of Annuity: PV = PMT × [(1 − (1+r)^−n) / r]

flashcards.definition: Core finance principle: money available now is worth more than the same amount in the future due to its earning potential.
The current worth of a future sum of money discounted at a given rate; answers 'What is $X in N years worth today?'
The value of a current amount after earning interest for N periods; FV = PV × (1+r)^n.
The interest rate used to determine present value; reflects opportunity cost and risk.
A series of equal payments made at regular intervals (mortgage payments, retirement withdrawals, insurance premiums).

quiz_questions.question_text: The time value of money means:

options.text: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future because it can earn a return

quiz_questions.question_text: PV of $50,000 needed in 15 years at 7% discount rate = $50,000 / (1.07)^15 ≈
The Rule of 72 estimates doubling time: 72 ÷ rate. At 8%, money doubles in approximately:
At 6%, money doubles in approximately _____ years.
FV of an annuity: $500/month at 8% annually (0.667%/month) for 30 years ≈
The same $500/month at 10% for 30 years ≈
The difference between 8% and 10% over 30 years on $500/month is ≈

options.text: ~$385,000 (rate differences compound enormously over long periods)

quiz_questions.question_text: PV of an annuity: $2,000/month for 25 years at 5% annual rate ≈
This PV is useful for determining:

options.text: How much you need TODAY to fund $2,000/month withdrawals for 25 years of retirement

quiz_questions.question_text: A lottery choice: $1M now vs. $50,000/year for 30 years. At 5% discount, PV of annuity ≈

options.text: ~$769,000 (lump sum of $1M now is worth more in present value terms)

quiz_questions.question_text: The discount rate reflects:

options.text: Opportunity cost — the return you could earn if you invested the money alternatively

quiz_questions.question_text: Higher discount rates make future cash flows worth _____ today.

options.text: Less (future money is 'discounted' more heavily when alternative returns are higher)

quiz_questions.question_text: Continuous compounding: FV = PV × e^(rt). $10,000 at 8% for 10 years ≈
A perpetuity (infinite annuity) has PV = PMT / r. $1,000/year at 5% =
Growing annuity: payments that increase annually (like salary). PV formula includes:

options.text: Both the discount rate AND the growth rate of payments

quiz_questions.question_text: Real vs. nominal rates: nominal 8% with 3% inflation gives real return of approximately:
TVM is the foundation of:

options.text: Nearly all financial decision-making: loans, investments, retirement planning, business valuation, insurance

quiz_questions.question_text: A $200,000 home purchased in 1995 at 3% annual appreciation is worth approximately _____ in 2025.
For financial math, TVM is:

options.text: THE most important concept — understanding present and future value is essential for every financial decision

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Net Present Value (NPV)
summary_sections.content_html:
Net Present Value

NPV Formula

NPV = −Initial Investment + Σ (Cash Flow_t / (1+r)^t)

If NPV > 0, the investment adds value. If NPV < 0, it destroys value.

Decision Rule

Accept projects with NPV > 0. Among multiple projects, choose the highest NPV.

flashcards.definition: Sum of all discounted future cash flows minus the initial investment; measures whether an investment creates value.
The required rate of return used to discount future cash flows; often the cost of capital or opportunity cost.
The net money flowing in or out during each period; includes revenue minus expenses.
NPV > 0: accept (creates value). NPV < 0: reject (destroys value). NPV = 0: break-even return.
The process of evaluating and selecting long-term investments using NPV, IRR, and payback period.

quiz_questions.question_text: NPV measures:

options.text: The net value created (or destroyed) by an investment in today's dollars

quiz_questions.question_text: A $50,000 investment generating $15,000/year for 5 years at 10% discount rate: Year 1 PV =
Since NPV > 0, the investor should:

options.text: Accept the project (it earns more than the 10% required return)

quiz_questions.question_text: If the discount rate were 15% instead, NPV would be:

options.text: Lower (higher discount rate reduces the PV of future cash flows)

quiz_questions.question_text: At 15% discount, NPV of the same project ≈ $50,281 − $50,000 =

options.text: ~$281 (barely positive — much less attractive at higher required return)

quiz_questions.question_text: At 20% discount, NPV would likely be:

options.text: Negative (the cash flows discounted at 20% are worth less than the $50,000 investment)

quiz_questions.question_text: Choosing between Project A (NPV = $10,000) and Project B (NPV = $7,000):

options.text: Choose A (higher NPV = more value created for the investor)

quiz_questions.question_text: NPV assumes cash flows are reinvested at:

options.text: The discount rate (this is a key assumption of the NPV method)

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate investment: $200,000 purchase, $1,500/month rent, $2,500/month expenses. Annual net cash flow =
Including 3% annual appreciation, the property value after 10 years ≈
Total NPV for the property must include:

options.text: Both annual cash flows (possibly negative) AND terminal sale value, all discounted to present

quiz_questions.question_text: Sensitivity analysis on NPV varies:

options.text: Discount rate, cash flow estimates, and timing to see how NPV changes under different scenarios

quiz_questions.question_text: NPV in Excel uses the formula =NPV(rate, cash_flows) + initial_investment where initial investment is:

options.text: Negative (cash outflow at time zero — note: Excel's NPV function starts at period 1)

quiz_questions.question_text: The WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) is often used as the discount rate because it represents:

options.text: The blended cost of all funding sources (debt and equity) — the minimum return the company needs to create value

quiz_questions.question_text: Standalone NPV ignores:

options.text: Strategic value, synergies, learning effects, and options the project creates for future opportunities

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, NPV is:

options.text: One of the most important tools in finance — used for business investments, real estate, and any decision involving future cash flows

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
summary_sections.content_html:
Internal Rate of Return

IRR Definition

The discount rate that makes NPV = 0. It's the project's effective annual return.

Decision Rule

If IRR > required return (hurdle rate): accept. If IRR < hurdle rate: reject.

flashcards.definition: The discount rate that makes NPV exactly zero; represents the project's annualized effective return rate.
The minimum acceptable rate of return for an investment; IRR must exceed this for the project to be accepted.
IRR gives a rate of return (percentage); NPV gives a dollar value. NPV is generally preferred for conflicting projects.
Modified IRR that assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital (more realistic than IRR's reinvestment assumption).
Traditional method for finding IRR by testing discount rates until NPV ≈ 0; modern tools (Excel) calculate instantly.

quiz_questions.question_text: IRR is the discount rate that makes NPV equal to:

options.text: Zero (the rate at which the project breaks even in present value terms)

quiz_questions.question_text: If a project's IRR is 15% and the required return is 10%, you should:

options.text: Accept it (IRR exceeds the hurdle rate by 5 percentage points)

quiz_questions.question_text: If IRR is 8% and the hurdle rate is 12%, you should:

options.text: Reject it (the project doesn't meet the minimum required return)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the earlier example ($50K investment, $15K/year for 5 years), IRR ≈

options.text: ~15.2% (the rate where NPV of these cash flows = $50,000)

quiz_questions.question_text: Excel calculates IRR using:

options.text: =IRR(range_of_cash_flows) — starting with the negative initial investment

quiz_questions.question_text: IRR assumes reinvestment of intermediate cash flows at:

options.text: The IRR itself (which may be unrealistically high — a key limitation)

quiz_questions.question_text: MIRR (Modified IRR) addresses this by assuming reinvestment at:

options.text: The cost of capital (a more realistic assumption)

quiz_questions.question_text: When comparing two mutually exclusive projects, _____ is more reliable.

options.text: NPV (it measures absolute value created; IRR can give conflicting rankings for different-sized projects)

quiz_questions.question_text: Project A: $10K investment, IRR 25%. Project B: $100K investment, IRR 18%. Which creates more value?

options.text: B likely creates more dollar value (NPV) despite lower IRR — $100K × 18% > $10K × 25% in absolute terms

quiz_questions.question_text: Multiple IRRs can occur when:

options.text: Cash flows switch signs more than once (e.g., large initial investment, positive cash flows, then large terminal cost)

quiz_questions.question_text: A project with alternating positive and negative cash flows may have:

options.text: Multiple IRRs or no real IRR (making NPV the better decision tool)

quiz_questions.question_text: IRR of a bond held to maturity equals its:

options.text: Yield to Maturity (YTM) — the rate that equates the PV of all coupon payments + face value to current price

quiz_questions.question_text: Startup investors use IRR to evaluate:

options.text: The annualized return on their investment based on the exit value relative to the entry price and holding period

quiz_questions.question_text: A VC investing $1M for a $5M return in 5 years has an IRR of:
A VC investing $1M for a $10M return in 7 years has an IRR of:
Real estate IRR includes:

options.text: All cash flows: purchase cost, rental income, expenses, tax benefits, and final sale price

quiz_questions.question_text: Payback period is simpler than IRR because:

options.text: It only measures how quickly you get your money back (ignoring time value and cash flows beyond payback)

quiz_questions.question_text: Discounted payback period improves payback by:

options.text: Discounting each year's cash flow before calculating when cumulative PV = initial investment

quiz_questions.question_text: Together, NPV, IRR, and payback period provide:

options.text: Complementary perspectives: dollar value (NPV), percentage return (IRR), and time to recovery (payback)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, IRR is essential for:

options.text: Evaluating any investment where you invest now and receive returns over time — from business projects to personal investments

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Financial Modeling
summary_sections.content_html:
Financial Modeling

Building Financial Models

  • Inputs: Assumptions (growth rate, costs, discount rate).
  • Calculations: Revenue projections, expense forecasts, cash flows.
  • Outputs: NPV, IRR, break-even, financial statements.

Scenario Analysis

Best case, base case, worst case — testing how changes in assumptions affect outcomes.

flashcards.definition: A spreadsheet-based tool that projects financial performance using assumptions, formulas, and scenarios.
Testing best case, base case, and worst case assumptions to understand the range of possible outcomes.
Changing ONE variable at a time to see its impact on the output (e.g., how does profit change if revenue drops 10%?).
Projected/forecasted financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow) based on assumptions.
Advanced modeling using thousands of random scenarios to produce probability distributions of outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A financial model takes _____ and produces _____.
The three essential financial statements in a model are:

options.text: Income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement (all interconnected)

quiz_questions.question_text: Scenario analysis tests:

options.text: Multiple outcomes: best case, base case, and worst case scenarios with different assumptions

quiz_questions.question_text: In a base case: revenue grows 10%, costs grow 5%. In worst case: revenue grows 2%, costs grow 8%. The model shows:

options.text: Dramatically different profitability — the worst case may show losses, guiding risk management decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: Sensitivity analysis differs from scenario analysis because it:

options.text: Changes ONE variable at a time (holding all others constant) to isolate each factor's impact

quiz_questions.question_text: A 'data table' in Excel performs sensitivity analysis by:

options.text: Automatically calculating outputs across a range of input values (e.g., NPV for discount rates from 5% to 15%)

quiz_questions.question_text: Revenue projection: Year 1 = $100,000 growing at 15% annually. Year 3 revenue =
If COGS is 40% of revenue, Year 3 COGS =
Year 3 gross profit = $132,250 − $52,900 =
A 5-year DCF model discounts projected free cash flows to find:

options.text: Enterprise value (the present value of all future cash flows the business is expected to generate)

quiz_questions.question_text: Terminal value in a DCF model represents:

options.text: The value of all cash flows beyond the explicit forecast period (often 60-80% of total enterprise value)

quiz_questions.question_text: Terminal value using the perpetuity growth method: TV = FCF × (1+g) / (r−g). If FCF = $1M, g = 3%, r = 10%:
Monte Carlo simulation:

options.text: Runs thousands of random scenarios based on probability distributions to produce a range of likely outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: The output of Monte Carlo simulation is:

options.text: A probability distribution showing the likelihood of various outcomes (e.g., 'there's a 90% chance NPV > $0')

quiz_questions.question_text: Model validation involves:

options.text: Comparing model outputs to historical data, industry benchmarks, and logical sense-checks

quiz_questions.question_text: Garbage in, garbage out (GIGO) means:

options.text: Model outputs are only as good as the assumptions — unrealistic inputs produce unrealistic results

quiz_questions.question_text: Key assumptions to document in any model include:

options.text: Growth rates, margins, discount rate, tax rate, capital expenditures, and working capital needs

quiz_questions.question_text: Break-even analysis in a financial model shows:

options.text: The combination of price, volume, and costs where the business achieves zero profit — a critical viability threshold

quiz_questions.question_text: Professional financial models follow best practices:

options.text: Color coding (blue = inputs, black = formulas), clear structure, documentation, and error checking

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, financial modeling integrates:

options.text: TVM, NPV, IRR, break-even, growth projections, and scenario analysis — the culmination of all financial math skills

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Corporate Finance Fundamentals
summary_sections.content_html:
Corporate Finance

Capital Structure

The mix of debt (loans, bonds) and equity (stock, retained earnings) used to finance a company.

Cost of Capital

  • Cost of Debt: Interest rate on borrowing × (1 − tax rate) — interest is tax-deductible.
  • Cost of Equity: Return required by shareholders (typically estimated via CAPM).
  • WACC: Weighted average of debt and equity costs.

flashcards.definition: The proportion of debt vs. equity a company uses for financing; affects risk, cost of capital, and returns.
The blended rate: (% debt × cost of debt after tax) + (% equity × cost of equity); the minimum return for new projects.
Cost of equity = Risk-free rate + Beta × (Market return − Risk-free rate); relates required return to risk.
Measure of a stock's volatility relative to the market; β=1 moves with market, β>1 more volatile, β<1 less volatile.
Using debt to amplify returns (and risks); measured by debt-to-equity ratio.

quiz_questions.question_text: Capital structure refers to:

options.text: The mix of debt and equity financing a company uses

quiz_questions.question_text: Debt financing has a tax advantage because:

options.text: Interest payments are tax-deductible (reducing the effective cost of debt)

quiz_questions.question_text: Cost of debt: 6% interest rate × (1 − 25% tax rate) = after-tax cost of:
CAPM: Risk-free rate 3% + Beta 1.2 × (Market return 10% − Risk-free 3%). Cost of equity =
A beta of 1.5 means the stock is approximately _____ as volatile as the market.
A beta of 0.7 means the stock is approximately _____ as volatile as the market.

options.text: 30% less volatile (moves less than the market in either direction)

quiz_questions.question_text: WACC with 40% debt at 4.5% after-tax, 60% equity at 11.4%:
A new project should be accepted if its expected return exceeds:

options.text: WACC (the project must earn at least the blended cost of financing it)

quiz_questions.question_text: Too much debt (overleveraging) increases:

options.text: Financial risk: interest payments are fixed obligations that must be paid regardless of revenue

quiz_questions.question_text: Optimal capital structure balances:

options.text: The tax benefits of debt against the increased bankruptcy risk of too much debt

quiz_questions.question_text: Modigliani-Miller theorem (with taxes) states:

options.text: The value of a company increases with debt (due to tax shields) — up to the point where bankruptcy risk offsets the tax benefit

quiz_questions.question_text: Retained earnings are:

options.text: Internal equity — profits reinvested in the business rather than paid as dividends (cheapest form of equity)

quiz_questions.question_text: Dividend policy decisions involve:

options.text: Whether to pay profits as dividends (returning cash to shareholders) or retain them for growth and reinvestment

quiz_questions.question_text: Stock buybacks reduce:

options.text: Shares outstanding (increasing EPS and often stock price — an alternative to dividends)

quiz_questions.question_text: Free Cash Flow (FCF) = Operating cash flow minus:

options.text: Capital expenditures (the cash available to pay debt holders and equity holders)

quiz_questions.question_text: Enterprise Value = Market Cap + Debt − Cash. If market cap = $500M, debt = $100M, cash = $50M:
EV/EBITDA ratio is used to:

options.text: Value and compare companies (a lower ratio may indicate undervaluation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial leverage ratio = Total Assets / Total Equity. $10M assets / $4M equity =

options.text: 2.5 (for every $1 of equity, the company has $2.50 in assets)

quiz_questions.question_text: Working capital management focuses on:

options.text: Short-term assets and liabilities: inventory, receivables, payables — ensuring adequate cash flow for daily operations

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, corporate finance applies:

options.text: WACC, CAPM, leverage ratios, and capital budgeting — quantitative tools used by finance professionals worldwide

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Risk Analysis & Quantification
summary_sections.content_html:
Risk Analysis

Measuring Risk

  • Standard Deviation: Measures volatility of returns (higher = more risk).
  • Sharpe Ratio: (Return − Risk-free Rate) / Standard Deviation — risk-adjusted return.
  • Value at Risk (VaR): Maximum expected loss at a given confidence level.

flashcards.definition: A measure of investment return volatility; higher σ = more dispersion around the average return = more risk.
(Portfolio return − risk-free rate) ÷ standard deviation; measures excess return per unit of risk. Higher is better.
The maximum expected loss over a given period at a specified confidence level (e.g., '95% VaR of $50,000' means 95% chance of losing less than $50K).
Measure of how two investments move relative to each other; −1 (opposite) to +1 (perfectly together). Low correlation = better diversification.
Return compared to the risk taken; two investments with the same return but different risk levels have different risk-adjusted returns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Standard deviation in finance measures:

options.text: Volatility — how much returns deviate from the average (higher σ = more uncertainty)

quiz_questions.question_text: Investment A: 10% avg return, 15% std dev. Investment B: 10% avg return, 5% std dev. Which is riskier?

options.text: A (same return but 3× the volatility — wider range of possible outcomes)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sharpe ratio for A (10% return, 3% risk-free, 15% std dev) =
The Sharpe ratio for B (10% return, 3% risk-free, 5% std dev) =
Investor should choose _____ because it has a higher Sharpe ratio.

options.text: B (same return with much less risk — more efficient risk-adjusted return)

quiz_questions.question_text: A Sharpe ratio above 1.0 is generally considered:

options.text: Good (the investment's excess return exceeds its risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: VaR at 95% confidence of $100,000 means:

options.text: There's a 95% chance you'll lose LESS than $100K in the specified period (5% chance of losing more)

quiz_questions.question_text: Correlation of +1 between two stocks means:

options.text: They move perfectly together (no diversification benefit)

quiz_questions.question_text: Correlation of −1 means:

options.text: They move in exactly opposite directions (maximum diversification benefit)

quiz_questions.question_text: A portfolio of stocks with low correlation to each other has:

options.text: Lower total risk than the average of individual risks (diversification effect)

quiz_questions.question_text: The efficient frontier shows:

options.text: The set of portfolios offering maximum return for each level of risk (or minimum risk for each return level)

quiz_questions.question_text: Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) was developed by:

options.text: Harry Markowitz (1952) — foundational theory that diversification reduces portfolio risk

quiz_questions.question_text: Portfolio A: 12% return, 18% std dev. Portfolio B: 10% return, 10% std dev. If risk-free = 3%:

options.text: A Sharpe = 0.50, B Sharpe = 0.70 — B is better risk-adjusted despite lower absolute return

quiz_questions.question_text: Systematic risk (market risk) can _____ be diversified away.

options.text: Never (it affects the entire market: interest rates, recessions, geopolitical events)

quiz_questions.question_text: Unsystematic risk (company-specific) can _____ be diversified away.

options.text: Usually (holding 20-30 diversified stocks eliminates most company-specific risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: Beta measures only _____ risk.

options.text: Systematic (market-related volatility that cannot be diversified away)

quiz_questions.question_text: The risk-return tradeoff states:

options.text: Higher risk investments must offer higher EXPECTED returns to attract investors (compensation for bearing risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: Stress testing a portfolio involves:

options.text: Simulating extreme scenarios (market crash, interest rate spike, pandemic) to see how the portfolio performs under stress

quiz_questions.question_text: Maximum drawdown measures:

options.text: The largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value (how bad the worst period was)

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, risk analysis provides:

options.text: Quantitative tools (σ, Sharpe, VaR, correlation) to objectively compare investments and construct optimal portfolios

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Economics & Finance Connections
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Economics & Finance Connections

Macro Concepts in Finance

  • GDP: Total economic output — impacts corporate earnings and stock market.
  • Inflation: Erodes purchasing power; affects interest rates and investment decisions.
  • Federal Reserve: Sets monetary policy (interest rates, money supply) affecting all financial markets.
  • Fiscal Policy: Government spending and taxation affecting economic growth.

flashcards.definition: GDP growth drives corporate earnings; recessions (negative GDP) trigger bear markets. Strong GDP = bullish for stocks.
Inflation erodes real returns. At 3% inflation, a 7% nominal return yields only ~4% real return.
US central bank controlling monetary policy: setting interest rates (federal funds rate) and managing money supply.
Fed actions to control money supply and interest rates: raising rates slows economy/inflation; lowering rates stimulates growth.
Stock prices are determined by supply/demand: more buyers than sellers = price rises; more sellers = price falls.

quiz_questions.question_text: When GDP grows, stock markets tend to:

options.text: Rise (economic growth means higher corporate earnings, which drives stock prices up)

quiz_questions.question_text: During a recession (negative GDP growth), stocks typically:

options.text: Fall (reduced economic activity means lower corporate profits and investor pessimism)

quiz_questions.question_text: Inflation at 3% reduces the purchasing power of $100 to approximately _____ after 10 years.

options.text: Combat inflation (higher rates reduce borrowing, slow spending, and cool the economy)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Federal Reserve lowers interest rates to:

options.text: Stimulate economic growth (cheaper borrowing encourages spending and investment)

quiz_questions.question_text: When the Fed raises rates, bond prices:

options.text: Fall (existing bonds with lower rates become less attractive; their prices must drop to offer competitive yields)

quiz_questions.question_text: When the Fed raises rates, stock prices tend to:

options.text: Often fall (higher rates increase borrowing costs, reduce future earnings, and make bonds more competitive)

quiz_questions.question_text: Fiscal policy involves:

options.text: Government spending and taxation decisions (Congress/President) — stimulus spending, tax cuts, infrastructure investment

quiz_questions.question_text: Expansionary fiscal policy (more spending, lower taxes) tends to:

options.text: Stimulate economic growth (but may increase deficits and future inflation)

quiz_questions.question_text: The national debt impacts finance because:

options.text: Large debt may lead to higher future taxes, inflation, or crowding out private investment as government competes for borrowing

quiz_questions.question_text: Supply and demand determines stock prices because:

options.text: When more people want to buy (demand) than sell (supply), the price rises; when more want to sell, the price falls
Only the company sets the price

quiz_questions.question_text: Opportunity cost in investment decisions means:

options.text: The return on the next best alternative you give up (investing in Stock A means giving up potential returns from Stock B)

quiz_questions.question_text: Comparative advantage applies to:

options.text: Both countries (trade) and individuals/businesses (specializing in what you do most efficiently)

quiz_questions.question_text: The yield curve (plotting bond yields by maturity) normally slopes:

options.text: Upward (longer-term bonds yield more because they carry more risk from inflation and uncertainty)

quiz_questions.question_text: An inverted yield curve (short-term rates > long-term) often predicts:

options.text: Recession (historically one of the most reliable recession indicators)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures:

options.text: The average change in prices paid by consumers for goods and services (the primary inflation measure)

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding macroeconomics helps investors because:

options.text: Economic conditions (GDP, inflation, rates, fiscal policy) drive market performance and should inform investment strategy

quiz_questions.question_text: The business cycle (expansion → peak → contraction → trough) means:

options.text: Different investment strategies work better in different phases (cyclical vs. defensive stocks, bonds vs. stocks)

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam and financial math, economics-finance connections show:

options.text: How macroeconomic forces directly drive financial markets — connecting classroom economic theory to real-world investment implications

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: International Finance Case Studies
summary_sections.content_html:
International Finance Case Studies

Case 1: Currency Impact on Investment Returns

A US investor buys European stock at €100 when 1 EUR = 1.10 USD (cost: $110). Stock rises 10% to €110. If EUR weakens to 1 EUR = 1.05 USD: $110 × 1.05 = $115.50 return on $110 cost = 5% return (vs. 10% in euros).

Case 2: Emerging Market Opportunities

Higher growth potential (7-8% GDP) vs. developed markets (2-3%), but with currency risk, political risk, and volatility.

flashcards.definition: The risk that exchange rate changes will reduce the value of international investments when converted back to the investor's home currency.
Using forward contracts, options, or currency-hedged ETFs to protect against unfavorable exchange rate movements.
Developing countries with rapidly growing economies (China, India, Brazil); higher potential returns but higher risk.
Borrowing in a low-interest-rate currency and investing in a high-interest-rate currency; profitable until rates or exchange rates shift.
The risk that a foreign government defaults on debt, imposes capital controls, or makes policy changes that harm investments.

quiz_questions.question_text: A US investor buying European stock must consider:

options.text: Both stock performance AND currency exchange rate changes (returns can be enhanced or reduced by FX moves)

quiz_questions.question_text: €100 stock bought at 1 EUR = 1.10 USD. Cost in dollars =
Stock rises 10% to €110. EUR weakens to 1.05 USD. Value in dollars =

options.text: ~5% (currency weakness eroded half the stock gain)

quiz_questions.question_text: If EUR instead strengthened to 1.15 USD, the dollar value would be:

options.text: $126.50 (110 × 1.15) — a 15% return thanks to both stock gains and currency gains

quiz_questions.question_text: Currency-hedged ETFs attempt to:

options.text: Eliminate currency fluctuation effects so returns more closely match the underlying foreign stock performance

quiz_questions.question_text: Emerging market GDP growth rates are typically _____ than developed markets.

options.text: Higher (5-8% vs. 2-3% in developed markets — faster economic growth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Emerging market risks include:

options.text: Currency volatility, political instability, less regulation, lower liquidity, and corporate governance concerns

quiz_questions.question_text: The MSCI Emerging Markets index includes stocks from:

options.text: Multiple countries: China, India, Brazil, Taiwan, South Korea, South Africa, and more

quiz_questions.question_text: A carry trade borrowing yen at 0.1% to invest in Brazil at 10% earns approximately:

options.text: ~9.9% spread (but highly risky if the yen strengthens or Brazilian real weakens)

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1997 Asian Financial Crisis demonstrated:

options.text: The risks of currency pegs, excessive borrowing, and lack of financial regulation in emerging markets

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2001 Argentine debt crisis showed:

options.text: Countries can default on debt, devalue currency, and impose capital controls — devastating for foreign investors

quiz_questions.question_text: International diversification benefits US investors because:

options.text: Different countries' economic cycles don't perfectly correlate — international stocks may zig when US stocks zag

quiz_questions.question_text: The optimal allocation to international stocks is often cited as:

options.text: 20-40% of equity allocation (significant international exposure for diversification)

quiz_questions.question_text: ADRs (American Depositary Receipts) allow US investors to:

options.text: Buy foreign stocks on US exchanges (traded in USD, eliminating some — but not all — complexity of foreign investing)

quiz_questions.question_text: Transfer pricing and tax treaties affect:

options.text: Multinational corporations' tax obligations across countries (complex international tax planning)

quiz_questions.question_text: The World Bank and IMF help:

options.text: Developing countries with loans, economic advice, and crisis support (promoting global economic stability)

quiz_questions.question_text: Global supply chains mean that even domestic companies face:

options.text: International risks (currency fluctuations, trade policies, and geopolitical disruptions affect supply costs and availability)

quiz_questions.question_text: Trade wars and tariffs affect investments by:

options.text: Increasing costs, disrupting supply chains, and creating uncertainty that can reduce stock prices and economic growth

quiz_questions.question_text: For financial math, international finance adds:

options.text: Currency conversion math, exchange rate risk analysis, and diversification calculations to the broader financial math toolkit

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Review of Key Formulas
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Financial Math Formulas

Time Value of Money

  • FV = PV × (1 + r)^n
  • PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
  • Rule of 72: Years to double ≈ 72 / rate

Business Math

  • Break-even (units) = Fixed Costs / (Price − Variable Cost)
  • Profit Margin = Net Profit / Revenue × 100
  • ROI = (Gain − Cost) / Cost × 100

Investment Math

  • NPV = −Investment + Σ(CF_t / (1+r)^t)
  • Sharpe Ratio = (Return − Risk-free) / Std Dev
  • WACC = (% Debt × Cost of Debt × (1−Tax)) + (% Equity × Cost of Equity)

flashcards.definition: Future value formula: how much a present sum grows with compound interest over n periods at rate r.
Present value formula: today's equivalent of a future sum, discounted at rate r for n periods.
Break-even units = Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin per unit (Price minus Variable Cost).
Net Present Value: sum of all discounted future cash flows minus the initial investment.
Sharpe ratio: excess return per unit of risk; higher = better risk-adjusted performance.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Exam Practice
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Practice Problems

Multi-Step Calculations

AP problems require combining multiple concepts: TVM, tax effects, risk analysis, and economic reasoning.

Free Response Strategy

  • Show ALL work and calculations.
  • Label formulas before plugging in numbers.
  • Explain economic reasoning, not just math.

flashcards.definition: Show all work, label formulas, state assumptions, explain reasoning, and connect math to economic principles.
A problem requiring sequential application of multiple concepts (e.g., calculate after-tax return, then find PV, then compare NPV).
Converting between annual/monthly/daily rates and periods: monthly rate = annual rate ÷ 12; periods = years × 12.
After calculating, verify the answer makes sense (e.g., PV should be less than FV; higher risk should mean higher return).
Connecting mathematical results to real-world economic implications and decision-making in AP responses.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Economics Context
summary_sections.content_html:
AP Economics Connections

Microeconomics Connections

Supply/demand curves → market price → stock pricing. Elasticity → pricing strategy. Marginal analysis → optimal decisions.

Macroeconomics Connections

GDP → corporate earnings → market returns. Inflation → interest rates → bond prices. Fed policy → all financial markets.

flashcards.definition: Making decisions by comparing marginal benefit to marginal cost — a core principle unifying economics and finance.
Price elasticity of demand determines pricing power: inelastic goods (necessities) can bear price increases; elastic goods cannot.
Monetary: Fed controls rates/money supply. Fiscal: Congress controls spending/taxes. Both impact financial markets.
The degree to which market prices reflect all available information — connected to both micro and macro analysis.
GDP, unemployment, CPI, consumer confidence — data that drives financial market movements and investment decisions.

quiz_questions.question_text: In microeconomics, the equilibrium price is where:

options.text: Supply equals demand (similar to how stock prices find equilibrium between buyers and sellers)

quiz_questions.question_text: Marginal cost = marginal revenue at the:

options.text: Profit-maximizing output level (the same principle applies to business financial decisions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Price elasticity of demand: if a 10% price increase causes only 3% demand drop, the product is:

options.text: Inelastic (% change in quantity < % change in price — the company has pricing power)

quiz_questions.question_text: Companies with inelastic demand can raise prices because:

options.text: Consumers don't reduce purchases much (necessities, drugs, unique products) — revenue increases with price

quiz_questions.question_text: GDP growth of 3% typically corresponds to stock returns that are:

options.text: Positive (economic growth drives corporate earnings growth, which drives stock prices)

quiz_questions.question_text: When unemployment rises, consumer spending:

options.text: Decreases (less income = less spending = lower corporate revenue = lower stock prices)

quiz_questions.question_text: The Phillips Curve relates:

options.text: Inflation and unemployment (historically, lower unemployment tends to coincide with higher inflation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Quantitative easing (QE) is when the Fed:

options.text: Buys bonds to inject money into the economy and lower long-term interest rates (stimulating borrowing and investment)

quiz_questions.question_text: QE generally leads to _____ stock prices.

options.text: Higher (lower rates push investors toward stocks; abundant liquidity inflates asset prices)

quiz_questions.question_text: Trade deficits affect currency because:

options.text: Importing more than exporting creates selling pressure on the domestic currency (more dollars flowing out to pay for imports)

quiz_questions.question_text: Comparative advantage explains why countries trade because:

options.text: Countries benefit by specializing in what they produce most efficiently and trading for other goods

quiz_questions.question_text: Monopolies vs. competitive markets: monopolies earn _____ profits.

options.text: Above-normal (no competition forces prices down — relevant for evaluating companies' competitive positions)

quiz_questions.question_text: Consumer surplus in financial markets represents:

options.text: The difference between what investors are willing to pay and what they actually pay (getting stocks 'below' their perceived value)

quiz_questions.question_text: Information asymmetry (one party knows more) in markets causes:

options.text: Adverse selection and moral hazard (e.g., insider trading, used car 'lemons problem' — drives regulation)

quiz_questions.question_text: Game theory applies to business decisions because:

options.text: Companies must consider competitors' reactions: pricing, market entry, advertising — strategy depends on others' actions

quiz_questions.question_text: The tragedy of the commons applies to:

options.text: Shared resources including financial markets (short-term individual greed can destroy long-term collective value)

quiz_questions.question_text: Behavioral economics + finance shows:

options.text: People systematically deviate from rational decision-making — understanding biases improves financial outcomes

quiz_questions.question_text: The AD-AS (Aggregate Demand/Supply) model shows:

options.text: Overall price level and output — shifts in AD or AS affect inflation, GDP, and financial markets broadly

quiz_questions.question_text: Understanding AP economics strengthens financial math because:

options.text: Economics provides the WHY behind financial math formulas — understanding incentives, markets, and policy makes financial decisions more informed

quiz_questions.question_text: The integration of economics and financial math is tested on the AP exam through:

options.text: Problems requiring both quantitative calculation AND qualitative economic reasoning and interpretation

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Financial Math Applications

Everyday Decisions

  • Renting vs. Buying: Comparing total costs including opportunity cost of down payment.
  • Car Buying: Lease vs. buy analysis; total cost of ownership.
  • College ROI: Tuition cost vs. lifetime earnings increase.
  • Retirement Planning: How much to save monthly to reach a goal.

flashcards.definition: Comparing total cost of renting (rent + renter's insurance) vs. buying (mortgage, taxes, insurance, maintenance, opportunity cost of down payment) over time.
Purchase price + insurance + gas + maintenance + depreciation − resale value = true cost of a vehicle over ownership period.
(Lifetime earnings with degree − lifetime earnings without − total college cost) / total college cost × 100.
Using FV of annuity formula to determine monthly savings needed to reach a target retirement nest egg.
Guideline that retirees can withdraw 4% of their portfolio in year 1 (adjusting for inflation) with high probability of money lasting 30+ years.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most valuable personal finance habit is:

options.text: Consistently saving/investing 15-20% of income starting as early as possible (time + compound interest = wealth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial math is relevant to EVERY adult because:

options.text: Everyone earns income, pays taxes, borrows money, saves for goals, and needs insurance — financial literacy is universal

quiz_questions.question_text: The single most powerful concept in this course is:

options.text: Compound interest — Einstein reportedly called it the 'eighth wonder of the world'

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Capstone: Personal Financial Plan
summary_sections.content_html:
Capstone: Build Your Personal Financial Plan

Plan Components

  • 1. Net Worth Statement: Assets − Liabilities = Net Worth.
  • 2. Budget: Income − Expenses = Surplus (invest) or Deficit (cut spending).
  • 3. Emergency Fund: 3-6 months expenses in savings.
  • 4. Debt Payoff Plan: Priority: high-interest first (avalanche method).
  • 5. Investment Plan: Retirement contributions, asset allocation by age.
  • 6. Insurance Review: Adequate coverage for health, auto, life, disability.
  • 7. Estate Planning: Will, beneficiary designations, power of attorney.

flashcards.definition: Total assets minus total liabilities; your financial 'score' — track it quarterly to measure progress.
50% needs (rent, food, insurance), 30% wants (entertainment, dining), 20% savings/debt payoff.
Debt payoff strategy: pay minimums on all debts, put extra money toward the HIGHEST interest rate debt first.
Debt payoff strategy: pay off smallest balances first for psychological wins (higher total interest cost than avalanche).
Legal documents ensuring your assets are distributed according to your wishes: will, beneficiaries, POA, healthcare directive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Net worth = Assets − Liabilities. $50K savings + $20K car − $30K student loans − $5K credit card =
The 50/30/20 rule on $4,000/month take-home: needs budget =

options.text: Highest interest rate debt (mathematically optimal — saves the most money on interest)

quiz_questions.question_text: The snowball method prioritizes:

options.text: Smallest balance (quick wins for motivation — but costs more in total interest than avalanche)

options.text: High-yield savings account (accessible within 1-2 days, FDIC insured, earning some interest)

quiz_questions.question_text: Recommended retirement savings rate is at minimum:
