options.text: 90/10 or 80/20 stocks/bonds (long time horizon can weather volatility for higher returns)

quiz_questions.question_text: Asset allocation for a 60-year-old approaching retirement:

options.text: 50/50 or 40/60 stocks/bonds (protecting accumulated wealth is more important than growth)

quiz_questions.question_text: Employer 401(k) match of 50% up to 6% means: on $60K salary, contributing 6% ($3,600), employer adds:
NOT contributing enough to get the full employer match means:

options.text: Leaving free money on the table — always contribute at least enough to get the full match (immediate 50% return!)

quiz_questions.question_text: Life insurance needs: $70K income × 10 = _____ coverage recommended.
A 30-year, $750K term life policy costs approximately:

options.text: $25-50/month (very affordable for the protection it provides)

quiz_questions.question_text: A will ensures:

options.text: Your assets are distributed according to YOUR wishes (without a will, the state decides — intestate succession)

quiz_questions.question_text: Beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and insurance:

options.text: Override the will (they pass directly to the named beneficiary — keep them updated after life changes)
Are the same as a will

quiz_questions.question_text: The financial plan should be reviewed:

options.text: At least annually and after major life events (marriage, baby, job change, home purchase)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most expensive mistake in personal finance is:

options.text: Not starting early enough — every year of delay costs significantly more in catch-up contributions

quiz_questions.question_text: $500/month starting at 22 vs. 32 (both at 8% to age 65): the 10-year head start adds approximately:

options.text: ~$500,000+ more at retirement (the power of the first decade of compounding)

quiz_questions.question_text: The purpose of a personal financial plan is to:

options.text: Provide a structured, math-backed roadmap for achieving your financial goals throughout life

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Comprehensive Review
summary_sections.content_html:
Comprehensive Course Review

Course Journey

  • Units 1-2: Financial literacy fundamentals, interest, and banking.
  • Units 3-4: Loans, debt, investments, and retirement planning.
  • Units 5-6: Budgeting, taxes, and insurance.
  • Units 7-8: Business finance and stock markets.
  • Unit 9: Advanced concepts: TVM, NPV, IRR, corporate finance, risk analysis.

flashcards.definition: The ability to understand and effectively use financial skills: budgeting, investing, borrowing, taxation, and personal financial management.
Interest calculated on both the initial principal AND accumulated interest — the most powerful force in finance.
Spreading investments across asset classes, sectors, and geographies to reduce risk without proportionally reducing expected returns.
Structuring financial activities to legally minimize taxes: retirement accounts, asset location, loss harvesting, and strategic deductions.
Assess current position → set goals → create strategy → implement plan → monitor and adjust — continuous cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: The foundation of all financial math is:

options.text: The time value of money (a dollar today > a dollar tomorrow)

quiz_questions.question_text: Simple interest I = PRT. Compound interest uses:

options.text: Exponential growth: FV = PV(1+r)^n (interest on interest)

quiz_questions.question_text: $1 invested at 10% for 100 years becomes:

options.text: ~$13,781 (the exponential nature of compound interest over long periods)

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important personal finance habit is:

options.text: Spending less than you earn and consistently investing the difference

quiz_questions.question_text: Debt with interest rates above _____ should generally be paid off before investing.

options.text: 6-8% (if investment returns average ~8-10%, high-interest debt reduces net worth faster than investments grow it)

quiz_questions.question_text: Credit score affects:

options.text: Loan rates, insurance premiums, rental applications, and even some employment decisions

quiz_questions.question_text: The three types of income are:

options.text: Earned (wages), portfolio (investments), and passive (rental, royalties) — each taxed differently

quiz_questions.question_text: The progressive tax system means:

options.text: Higher income portions are taxed at higher rates — but effective rate is always less than marginal rate

quiz_questions.question_text: Insurance protects against:

options.text: Catastrophic losses that would devastate your finances (transfer risk to the insurer via premium)

quiz_questions.question_text: Break-even analysis is critical because it shows:

options.text: The minimum sales needed to cover costs — below this, the business loses money

quiz_questions.question_text: NPV > 0 means:

options.text: The investment earns more than the required return — it creates value (accept)

quiz_questions.question_text: For comparing risk-adjusted returns, use the:

options.text: Sharpe ratio (excess return per unit of risk)

quiz_questions.question_text: Asset allocation should be based on:

options.text: Your time horizon, risk tolerance, and financial goals

quiz_questions.question_text: The efficient market hypothesis suggests that the best strategy for most investors is:

options.text: Low-cost index fund investing (matching the market is a winning strategy over time)

quiz_questions.question_text: Behavioral finance teaches that the biggest threat to investment returns is:

options.text: Your own emotional reactions (panic selling, FOMO buying, overconfidence)

quiz_questions.question_text: Global diversification matters because:

options.text: Different economies and markets don't move in perfect lockstep — reducing overall portfolio volatility

quiz_questions.question_text: Corporate finance uses WACC as the hurdle rate because it represents:

options.text: The blended minimum return needed to satisfy both debt holders and equity investors

quiz_questions.question_text: The personal financial plan integrates:

options.text: All course concepts: budgeting, tax planning, insurance, investing, debt management, and estate planning

quiz_questions.question_text: The goal of financial math education is:

options.text: Developing the quantitative skills and financial literacy to make informed decisions that improve your financial life

quiz_questions.question_text: Financial math is unique because it is:

options.text: Directly applicable to decisions EVERY person makes — from the first paycheck to retirement and beyond

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: AP Financial Math Scenarios
summary_sections.content_html:
AP-Style Integrated Scenarios

Scenario: Life After Graduation

Recent graduate: $45K salary, $28K student loans at 5.5%, needs apartment, car, insurance, and retirement savings. Apply ALL course concepts to make optimal decisions.

flashcards.definition: Applying multiple financial concepts simultaneously to a realistic life situation requiring integrated analysis.
1. Identify the decision. 2. Gather data. 3. Calculate options. 4. Compare quantitatively. 5. Consider qualitative factors. 6. Decide.
Optimization: finding the mathematically best solution. Satisficing: finding a 'good enough' solution that meets all constraints.
Considering all financial dimensions — income, taxes, debt, savings, insurance, and opportunity costs — simultaneously.
Small decisions compound dramatically: a $100/month savings difference at 22 vs. not starting = ~$350K+ difference by retirement.

If employer offers 401(k) with 50% match up to 6% of salary: 6% of $45K =
The employer match ($1,350/year) is a guaranteed _____ return on your contribution.

options.text: 50% (instant, risk-free return — always get the full match)

quiz_questions.question_text: Contributing $225/month to 401(k) leaves $259 − $225 = $34/month for additional savings. The first priority is:

options.text: Building an emergency fund ($12,000-18,000 target at $34/month will take a while — consider ways to increase income)

quiz_questions.question_text: Renter's insurance at ~$20/month provides _____ protection.

options.text: Essential (covers $20K+ in personal property, liability, and additional living expenses for just $240/year)

quiz_questions.question_text: Health insurance through employer at $150/month is likely:

options.text: A necessity — medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy (always maintain health coverage)

quiz_questions.question_text: The $225/month 401(k) contribution at 8% for 43 years (until 65) grows to approximately:
Adding the employer match ($1,350/yr at 8% for 43 yrs) adds another ≈
Total estimated retirement account at 65: $1M + $500K ≈
At the 4% rule, $1.5M supports annual income of:

options.text: $60,000/year (plus Social Security — potentially a comfortable retirement)

quiz_questions.question_text: Should the graduate pay extra on student loans (5.5%) or invest more (expected 8%)?

options.text: Mathematically, investing at 8% > paying 5.5% debt — but ensure emergency fund exists first and consider risk tolerance

quiz_questions.question_text: Buying vs. leasing a car: a reliable used car at $15K financed at 5% for 5 years costs ~$283/month. Lease at $250/month for 3 years, then:

options.text: Return it with nothing (leasing costs more long-term; buying builds equity in the vehicle)

quiz_questions.question_text: Credit score optimization: the graduate should:

options.text: Pay all bills on time, keep credit utilization below 30%, maintain oldest accounts — builds score for future mortgage rates

quiz_questions.question_text: The $45K salary with proper financial planning can lead to:

options.text: Millionaire status by retirement through consistent saving, employer match, and compound interest — financial literacy is the key

quiz_questions.question_text: The most important takeaway from this entire course:

options.text: Start early, be consistent, understand the math behind your decisions, and let compound interest work for you over decades
